topics.title: Introduction to Prompt Engineering and Language Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Prompt Engineering and Language Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prompt Engineering** is the disciplined, iterative process of designing, structuring, and refining natural-language inputs (called *prompts*) provided to a Large Language Model (LLM) to elicit desired, accurate, and contextually aligned outputs across a wide spectrum of downstream tasks — including text generation, reasoning, translation, summarization, code synthesis, and multi-turn dialogue.

A **Language Model (LM)** is a probabilistic computational system that estimates the likelihood of a token (word, sub-word, or character) appearing in a sequence, given the preceding context. Formally, it learns the joint probability distribution over token sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme defines Prompt Engineering as a *core elective competency* that bridges human intent and machine reasoning. The course PECST868 explicitly maps this module to **CO1**: *"Understand the foundational principles of language models and articulate the role of prompts in steering generative AI behaviour."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS for AI"

Imagine a **Large Language Model** as a vast, well-educated tourist in a foreign city who speaks every language, but has *no destination in mind*. They are brilliant, knowledgeable, and capable — but aimless. A **Prompt** is the **address you give them**: clear street, landmark, city, and country. The more precise your address, the closer they get to your intended spot.

**Prompt Engineering** is the skill of writing that address so well that you never have to repeat yourself or correct them. It is not "magic words" — it is *structured communication*.

| Element | Tourist Analogy | LLM Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| The Tourist | Knows everything, but no goal | Pre-trained LLM (e.g., GPT, LLaMA, Claude) |
| Your Address | "Take me to the museum near the river" | "Summarize this article in 3 bullet points" |
| Map Quality | Confusing directions → wrong place | Vague prompt → hallucinated response |
| Specificity | "Marine Drive, Kochi, gate 3" | "Act as a senior copywriter. Tone: formal. Audience: KTU students" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering:** In real KTU 2024 industry projects, a poorly-engineered prompt can cause an LLM to waste GPU-hours, hallucinate code, or violate compliance — costing both time and money.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Language Models

There are three fundamental architectural families, each forming the substrate on which prompt engineering operates:

1. **Statistical Language Models (SLMs)** — Based on n-gram probabilities (e.g., classical NLP pre-2013).
2. **Neural Language Models (NLMs)** — Use RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs to capture sequential dependencies.
3. **Large Language Models (LLMs)** — Transformer-based models with billions of parameters, trained on web-scale corpora (e.g., GPT-4, Gemini, LLaMA-3, Mistral).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** Students are expected to clearly differentiate between *generative* and *discriminative* language tasks, and identify whether a model is *autoregressive* (decoder-only, predicts next token) or *masked* (encoder-only, like BERT).

## 1.4 What Exactly is a "Prompt"?

A **prompt** is the complete input string passed to an LLM at inference time. It is not merely the "question" — it is the *entire context window* containing:

- **System message** (role, persona, constraints)
- **User instruction** (the actual task)
- **Context / Few-shot examples** (in-context demonstrations)
- **Output format specifier** (JSON, Markdown, table)
- **Chain-of-thought trigger** ("Let’s think step by step")

The art and science of orchestrating these components is the discipline of **Prompt Engineering**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How a Language Model Operates — The Underlying Logic

A language model, at its mathematical core, is a function that maps a sequence of tokens to a probability distribution over the vocabulary for the *next* token. The steps are:

1. **Tokenization** — Raw text is split into discrete units (tokens) using algorithms like **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**, **WordPiece**, or **SentencePiece**.
2. **Embedding** — Each token is mapped to a high-dimensional dense vector (typically 768 to 12,288 dimensions in modern LLMs).
3. **Contextual Encoding** — The Transformer layers (self-attention + feed-forward) produce *context-aware* representations.
4. **Decoding** — A final softmax layer produces probabilities for the next token.
5. **Sampling** — A decoding strategy (greedy, beam, top-k, top-p, temperature) selects the actual output token.

## 2.2 The Autoregressive Factorization

For any sequence of tokens $W = (w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n)$, an autoregressive language model decomposes the joint probability using the **chain rule of probability**:

$$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

**Why this is the "Why" of prompting:** When you write a prompt, you are essentially *conditioning* this product. You are setting the values of $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_k$ (your prompt tokens), and asking the model to compute the most probable continuation $w_{k+1}, w_{k+2}, \dots, w_n$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the high-yield mathematical formulations tested in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) for PECST868.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint Probability (Chain Rule) | $P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ | Deriving model likelihood |
| 2 | Shannon Entropy (per token) | $H(w) = -\sum_{w \in V} P(w) \log_2 P(w)$ | Measuring output uncertainty |
| 3 | Perplexity (PPL) | $\text{PPL}(W) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$ | Standard LM evaluation metric |
| 4 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(y_i \mid x_i)$ | Training objective |
| 5 | Softmax (next-token) | $P(w_i) = \dfrac{\exp(z_i / \tau)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$ | Decoding step |
| 6 | Temperature Scaling | $P_\tau(w) = \dfrac{\exp(z_w / \tau)}{\sum_{w'} \exp(z_{w'} / \tau)}$ | Controlling randomness |
| 7 | Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling | Choose smallest set $S$ s.t. $\sum_{w \in S} P(w) \geq p$ | Decoding strategy |
| 8 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\left(\dfrac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$ | Core Transformer op |
| 9 | BPE Merge Rule | Merge the most frequent adjacent byte-pair iteratively | Tokenization |
| 10 | Self-Attention Complexity | $O(n^2 \cdot d)$ per layer | Architectural bottleneck |

> **Notation Note:** $\mid$ in the formulas above denotes conditional probability, not table-cell separator. In markdown tables, all such bars are rendered using LaTeX `\mid` or `\vert` to prevent syntax breakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Prompt Engineering |
|---|---|
| **Software Engineering** | Automated code generation, refactoring, bug triage using Copilot-style workflows |
| **Healthcare** | Drafting clinical notes, summarizing patient records with strict PHI guardrails |
| **Education** | Adaptive tutoring, KTU-style question paper generation, rubric-based grading |
| **Cybersecurity** | Threat-intel summarization, log analysis, red-team prompt-injection testing |
| **Data Analytics** | Natural-language-to-SQL, automated EDA report drafting |

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Decoding Strategy

- **Greedy decoding** picks $\arg\max P(w_i)$ — fastest, but repetitive.
- **Beam search** keeps top-$k$ hypotheses — better for translation, but expensive.
- **Top-k sampling** restricts to $k$ highest-probability tokens — balances quality and diversity.
- **Top-p (nucleus) sampling** adapts the candidate set size dynamically — the *de facto* standard for chatbots.
- **Temperature $\tau$**: when $\tau \to 0$, the distribution sharpens toward greedy; when $\tau \to \infty$, the distribution flattens toward uniform randomness.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** A common 3-mark question is: *"Explain how temperature $\tau$ affects token sampling."* The model answer must explicitly mention the **sharpening** vs **flattening** effect on the softmax distribution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Chain Rule to Cross-Entropy to Perplexity

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU ESE Module 1 questions. We will derive perplexity from first principles.

### Step 1 — Start with the Autoregressive Likelihood

For a token sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$, the probability the model assigns to it is:

$$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

**Plain-English explanation:** The probability of the *whole* sentence equals the product of "probability of the next word, given all the words before it."

### Step 2 — Take the Logarithm (numerical stability)

Multiplying many small probabilities underflows. We convert to a sum using the identity $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$:

$$\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Explanation:** Log-transforms turn products into sums, and *monotonically preserve ordering*, so the model's preferred sequence still has the highest score.

### Step 3 — Average Over the Sequence Length

To get a per-token measure (independent of sentence length):

$$\frac{1}{n} \log P(W) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Explanation:** This is the **average log-likelihood per token** — the quantity we want to *maximize* during training.

### Step 4 — Negate to Get a Loss (Cross-Entropy)

Training minimizes loss, not maximizes likelihood. Negate the expression:

$$H(W) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

This is the **Cross-Entropy** of the model on the sequence $W$, measured in **nats** (natural log). If we used $\log_2$, the unit is **bits**.

### Step 5 — Exponentiate to Obtain Perplexity

Perplexity is defined as the exponential of the cross-entropy:

$$\text{PPL}(W) = \exp(H(W)) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$

**Intuition:** If a model has PPL = 50 on some text, it is "as confused" as if at each step it had to choose uniformly among 50 equally likely words. **Lower PPL = better model.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Step:** Examiners often ask students to show that minimizing cross-entropy is *equivalent* to minimizing perplexity, because $\exp(\cdot)$ is a monotonic function.

## 3.2 Derivation: Temperature-Scaled Softmax

We want to understand how a single hyperparameter $\tau$ reshapes the next-token distribution.

### Step 1 — Standard Softmax

Given raw logits $z_i$ from the model's output layer:

$$P(w_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j)}$$

**Explanation:** Softmax converts raw scores into a valid probability distribution summing to 1.

### Step 2 — Inject the Temperature

Divide every logit by $\tau$ **before** exponentiation:

$$P_\tau(w_i) = \frac{\exp(z_i / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$$

### Step 3 — Analyze Limiting Behaviour

- **Case 1:** $\tau \to 0$. The largest logit dominates, and $P_\tau(w^*) \to 1$ for the argmax token — equivalent to greedy decoding.
- **Case 2:** $\tau = 1$. Standard softmax — no modification.
- **Case 3:** $\tau \to \infty$. All exponents approach zero uniformly, and $P_\tau(w_i) \to \frac{1}{V}$ — uniform random sampling.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $V = 3$ with logits $z = (2.0,\ 1.0,\ 0.5)$:

**At $\tau = 1$** (compute $\exp(2.0)=7.389$, $\exp(1.0)=2.718$, $\exp(0.5)=1.649$):

$$P_1 = \left(\frac{7.389}{11.756},\ \frac{2.718}{11.756},\ \frac{1.649}{11.756}\right) \approx (0.629,\ 0.231,\ 0.140)$$

**At $\tau = 0.5$** (divide logits by 0.5: $(4.0,\ 2.0,\ 1.0)$):

$$P_{0.5} = \left(\frac{e^{4}}{e^{4}+e^{2}+e^{1}},\ \frac{e^{2}}{e^{4}+e^{2}+e^{1}},\ \frac{e^{1}}{e^{4}+e^{2}+e^{1}}\right) \approx (0.844,\ 0.114,\ 0.042)$$

**Observation:** Lower $\tau$ → more peaked → more deterministic. This is why chatbot APIs expose $\tau$ as a "creativity knob."

## 3.3 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention

The Transformer attention mechanism — the heartbeat of every modern LLM — is:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

**Step 1 — Compute raw similarity scores** as the dot product $Q K^\top$, giving an $(n \times n)$ matrix.

**Step 2 — Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$** to prevent the dot products from growing with dimension $d_k$, which would push softmax into saturated regions with vanishing gradients.

**Step 3 — Apply row-wise softmax** to convert each row into a probability distribution (attention weights).

**Step 4 — Multiply by $V$** to compute a weighted sum of value vectors — the contextualized output.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **scaling factor** $\sqrt{d_k}$. Examiners frequently deduct **1 mark** for omitting it in derivation sub-parts.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Prompt vs. Output Behaviour

| Prompt Type | Token-Probability Effect | Typical Use | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zero-shot | Model relies on pre-training only | Simple Q&A | "Translate to Malayalam" |
| One-shot | One in-context example biases $P(w_{k+1})$ | Format adherence | "Example: 2+2=4. Now 5+3=" |
| Few-shot | Multiple examples sharpen distribution | Classification, style transfer | 3–5 labelled examples |
| Chain-of-Thought (CoT) | Adds intermediate reasoning tokens | Math, logic | "Let's think step by step" |
| Role Prompting | Conditions persona tokens | Domain expertise | "You are a KTU examiner" |
| Constraint Prompting | Negative-logit bias on forbidden tokens | Safety, JSON mode | "Do not mention X" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Prompt Engineering Pipeline

The diagram below captures the complete information flow from a raw human intent to a refined, validated LLM response. Every block is a stage in the KTU-aligned engineering workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Intent] --> B[Prompt Designer]
    B --> C[System Role Definition]
    C --> D[Context and Few Shot Assembly]
    D --> E[Output Format Specification]
    E --> F[Constraint Injection]
    F --> G[Tokenization Layer]
    G --> H[Transformer Encoder Decoder]
    H --> I[Logit Vector Output]
    I --> J[Temperature and Top p Sampling]
    J --> K[Decoded Token Sequence]
    K --> L[Response Validator]
    L --> M{Valid Output}
    M -- Yes --> N[Final Response to User]
    M -- No --> O[Prompt Refinement Loop]
    O --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - The **left side** captures *prompt construction* (the engineering discipline).
> - The **center blocks** (G–K) represent the *LLM's internal mechanics* (out of the user's direct control, but steered by hyperparameters).
> - The **right side** (L–O) captures *evaluation and iterative refinement* — the "engineering" loop that distinguishes prompt *users* from prompt *engineers*.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Transformer Language Model Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Prompt Tokens] --> B[Token Embedding Matrix]
    B --> C[Positional Encoding]
    C --> D[Multi Head Self Attention]
    D --> E[Add and Norm]
    E --> F[Feed Forward Network]
    F --> G[Add and Norm]
    G --> H[Next Token Logits]
    H --> I[Softmax with Temperature]
    I --> J[Sampler Greedy Top k Top p]
    J --> K[Output Token]
    K --> A
```

> **KTU Reading Note:** The loop-back arrow from `K` to `A` illustrates the **autoregressive** nature — each newly generated token is appended to the prompt, becoming input for predicting the next token, until a stop token or max length is reached.

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Prompt Engineering Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1 Task Framing] --> L2[Layer 2 Persona and Role]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Context Window Management]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Reasoning Trigger CoT]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Output Schema Control]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Safety and Constraint Guardrails]
    L6 --> L7[Final Engineered Prompt]
```

**Explanation of Layers:**

- **Layer 1 — Task Framing:** Define the verb (summarize, classify, generate, compare).
- **Layer 2 — Persona and Role:** Assign expert identity ("Act as a senior DBMS instructor").
- **Layer 3 — Context Window Management:** Decide what to include/exclude within the model's context budget.
- **Layer 4 — Reasoning Trigger:** Inject CoT or ReAct-style scaffolding for multi-step tasks.
- **Layer 5 — Output Schema Control:** Force JSON, Markdown tables, or specific length.
- **Layer 6 — Safety & Constraint Guardrails:** Negative instructions, refusal policies, hallucination checks.
- **Layer 7 — Final Engineered Prompt:** The composed, ready-to-send input string.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Tip:** When answering design questions in ESE, explicitly label your prompt using these 6 layers. Examiners award bonus structure marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are direct, definition/concept-focused, and require crisp 3–4 sentence answers. Length: 50–80 words.

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]
**Define Prompt Engineering. List any four key components of an engineered prompt.**

**Model Answer:**
Prompt Engineering is the disciplined practice of designing and optimizing natural-language inputs to a Large Language Model to elicit accurate, reliable, and contextually aligned outputs.

The four key components are:
1. **System / Role instruction** — sets the persona.
2. **User task** — the actual instruction.
3. **Context / Few-shot examples** — in-context demonstrations.
4. **Output format specifier** — controls response structure (JSON, bullet, table).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for components)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Explain the role of *temperature* $\tau$ in next-token sampling. How does $\tau \to 0$ differ from $\tau \to \infty$?**

**Model Answer:**
Temperature $\tau$ is a hyperparameter that rescales the logits before the softmax operation, reshaping the next-token probability distribution.

When $\tau \to 0$, the distribution becomes sharply peaked at the argmax token, behaving like greedy decoding — highly deterministic and repetitive.

When $\tau \to \infty$, the distribution flattens toward uniform over the vocabulary — random and incoherent output.

At $\tau = 1$, the distribution reflects the model's raw confidence. Production chatbots typically use $\tau \in [0.7,\ 1.0]$ for balanced creativity.

*(3 marks: 1 for concept, 1 for low-$\tau$, 1 for high-$\tau$)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice** (either OR). Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Provide full derivations and diagrams.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for **perplexity (PPL)** of an autoregressive language model from first principles, starting from the chain rule of probability. Clearly state the meaning of each intermediate variable. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Transformer-based LLM inference pipeline** from tokenization to final output. Highlight the role of the **scaled dot-product attention** mechanism. **\[7 marks\]**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Joint probability decomposition:**
For a token sequence $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)$, by the chain rule:

$$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\log$:**
$$\log P(W) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 3 — Average:**
$$\frac{1}{n}\log P(W) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 4 — Negate (cross-entropy):**
$$H(W) = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) \quad \text{(in nats)}$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate (perplexity):**
$$\text{PPL}(W) = \exp(H(W)) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$

**Interpretation:** A PPL of $k$ means the model is as "perplexed" as if choosing uniformly from $k$ tokens. Lower is better.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

The pipeline consists of:
1. **Tokenization** — text → token IDs (BPE/WordPiece).
2. **Embedding + Positional Encoding** — token IDs → vectors with position info.
3. **$N$ stacked Transformer decoder blocks**, each containing:
   - Masked multi-head self-attention
   - Feed-forward network
   - Residual connections + LayerNorm
4. **Final linear projection** to vocabulary logits $z \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$.
5. **Softmax + sampling** to produce the next token.

**Scaled Dot-Product Attention** is the key operation:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

The $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling prevents gradient saturation; the softmax converts similarities into attention weights; the multiplication with $V$ produces a contextualized representation. **Multi-head** attention runs $h$ such operations in parallel, each learning a different relational subspace (syntax, semantics, coreference, etc.).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **different decoding strategies** (greedy, beam, top-$k$, top-$p$) used in language model inference. Compare their trade-offs in a tabular form. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss the **six functional layers of an engineered prompt** with a labelled block diagram. Show how a poorly-constructed prompt (missing layers) can degrade the output quality of a real-world task like *automated grading of KTU answer sheets*. **\[7 marks\]**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Greedy Decoding** — At each step, pick the token with the highest probability. Fast, deterministic, but suffers from repetition and myopia.

**Beam Search** — Maintain the top-$B$ candidate sequences (beams) and extend each. Better for translation/summarization; computationally expensive ($O(B \cdot V)$ per step).

**Top-$k$ Sampling** — Restrict sampling to the $k$ most-probable tokens, then sample. Fixed candidate count regardless of confidence.

**Top-$p$ (Nucleus) Sampling** — Choose the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds $p$. Adaptive set size — narrows when the model is confident, widens when uncertain.

| Strategy | Diversity | Coherence | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Greedy | Very Low | High (short) | $O(V)$ | Deterministic tasks |
| Beam | Low | High | $O(B \cdot V)$ | Translation |
| Top-$k$ | Medium | Medium | $O(V)$ | Creative writing |
| Top-$p$ | High | High | $O(V \log V)$ | Chatbots (industry default) |

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

The six functional layers are: **Task Framing → Persona/Role → Context Window → Reasoning Trigger → Output Schema → Safety Guardrails.**

In *automated grading of KTU answer sheets*, a poorly-constructed prompt that **omits the Output Schema layer** may cause the LLM to return free-form text instead of a structured `{marks, feedback, rubric_match}` JSON, breaking the downstream grading pipeline.

**Omitting the Persona layer** → the LLM may grade inconsistently (sometimes lenient, sometimes harsh) rather than as a strict KTU rubric-following examiner.

**Omitting the Reasoning Trigger (CoT)** → the model may jump to a final mark without showing step-by-step rubric matching, making the grade unauditable.

**Omitting the Safety layer** → the LLM may hallucinate KTU-specific references (e.g., "as per KTU 2019 scheme regulation 4.2") that don't exist, misleading students.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> In Part B answers, students often write a "wall of text" without diagrams or labelled blocks. **2 marks are reserved for a labelled block diagram.** Even a hand-drawn-style Mermaid diagram (reproduced cleanly on paper) earns full credit. Do not skip it.
> Additionally, examiners deduct **1 mark** if the perplexity derivation in Q1(a) does not start explicitly from the **chain rule of probability** — vague intros like "perplexity measures confusion" lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute KTU exam revision checklist — Module 1.**

- ✅ **Prompt Engineering** = designing inputs to LLMs to steer output behaviour.
- ✅ **Language Model** = a probabilistic model of token sequences; autoregressive ones factorize joint probability via the chain rule.
- ✅ **Chain Rule:** $P(W) = \prod_{i} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ — the *single most important equation* in this module.
- ✅ **Perplexity (PPL)** = $\exp(H(W))$ — lower is better; standard LM evaluation metric.
- ✅ **Cross-Entropy** = negated average log-likelihood per token — the *training loss* for LMs.
- ✅ **Softmax with temperature** controls randomness; $\tau \to 0$ is greedy, $\tau \to \infty$ is uniform.
- ✅ **Decoding strategies:** Greedy, Beam, Top-$k$, Top-$p$ — know their trade-offs.
- ✅ **Scaled Dot-Product Attention:** $\text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k})V$ — never forget the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling.
- ✅ **Tokenization** uses BPE/WordPiece/SentencePiece — affects vocabulary size and OOV handling.
- ✅ **Six Functional Layers of a Prompt:** Task Framing → Persona → Context → Reasoning → Schema → Safety.
- ✅ **Prompting Paradigms:** Zero-shot, One-shot, Few-shot, Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Role, Constraint.
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** Code generation, KTU QP generation, medical summarization, NLP-to-SQL, log analysis.
- ✅ **Always include a labelled diagram** in KTU Part B answers — it carries 2 free marks.
- ✅ **Always start perplexity derivations from the chain rule** — never from a generic "model confusion" definition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Natural Language Processing (NLP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Natural Language Processing (NLP) # Fundamentals of Natural Language Processing (NLP) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is a subfield of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**, **Computer Science**, and **Linguistics** that focuses on enabling computers to understand, interpret, generate, and respond to human language in a meaningful and useful way. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Prompt Engineering (PECST868)*, NLP forms the foundational layer upon which modern prompt design, instruction tuning, and Large Language Model (LLM) interactions are built. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECST868 – Module 1):** NLP is the prerequisite domain for prompt engineering. Every prompt you write is processed through NLP pipelines (tokenization, parsing, semantic analysis) before being fed to the underlying language model. Therefore, mastering NLP fundamentals is **non-negotiable** for any prompt engineer. Formally, an NLP system can be mathematically abstracted as a function: $$f: \mathcal{L} \rightarrow \mathcal{R}$$ where $\mathcal{L}$ represents the space of all possible human language inputs (sentences, paragraphs, documents) and $\mathcal{R}$ represents the structured representation or response space (labels, vectors, generated text). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **tourist translator in a foreign country**. You don't just swap word-for-word; you must: 1. **Hear** the sentence (Acoustic / Input layer) 2. **Break** it into words (Tokenization) 3. **Identify** the role of each word — who is doing what? (Parsing / POS Tagging) 4. **Understand** the meaning (Semantic Analysis) 5. **Translate** it into the local dialect (Generation) 6. **Speak** it out (Output) NLP is exactly this — a computer acts as that **multilingual, multi-skilled interpreter**, but operating at speeds of **millions of sentences per second** with **billions of parameters**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > *NLP is the discipline that designs and develops computational models and algorithms to process, analyze, and represent natural language text or speech for the purpose of achieving human-like language understanding and generation.* ### 1.3 Why NLP Matters for Prompt Engineering The course **PECST868 – Prompt Engineering** is essentially *applied NLP*. The most critical constants and metrics in the field include: - **Vocabulary Size (|V|):** typically **10,000 to 50,000** tokens in classical NLP, **50,000 to 100,000+** in modern LLMs like GPT-style architectures. - **Sequence Length (n):** context window, often **512**, **2048**, **4096**, or up to **128K tokens** in contemporary models. - **Embedding Dimension (d):** standard sizes — **128, 256, 512, 768, 1024, 4096**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Industrial Applications of NLP:** > - Chatbots and Virtual Assistants (Siri, Alexa, ChatGPT) > - Sentiment Analysis (brand monitoring) > - Machine Translation (Google Translate, DeepL) > - Spam Detection (Email filters) > - Search Engines (Google BERT, Bing) > - Code Generation (GitHub Copilot) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Seven Linguistic Levels of NLP Every NLP system — whether a simple rule-based parser or a 1.7-trillion-parameter LLM — processes language across hierarchical linguistic levels. Below is the **canonical KTU-recommended breakdown**: | # | Level | Question Answered | Example Technique | |---|-------|-------------------|-------------------| | 1 | **Phonological / Acoustic** | How are sounds structured? | Speech signal processing, MFCC | | 2 | **Morphological** | How are words formed from morphemes? | Stemming, Lemmatization | | 3 | **Lexical** | What is the dictionary meaning of words? | Tokenization, Stop-word removal | | 4 | **Syntactic** | How are words combined grammatically? | POS Tagging, Parsing | | 5 | **Semantic** | What does the sentence mean? | Named Entity Recognition, WSD | | 6 | **Discourse** | How do sentences relate to each other? | Coreference Resolution | | 7 | **Pragmatic** | What is the speaker's true intent? | Intent Recognition, Sarcasm Detection | ### 2.2 Core NLP Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic) A typical NLP pipeline executes the following **sequential stages**: 1. **Text Acquisition** → Raw corpus collection (web scraping, APIs, datasets). 2. **Text Cleaning** → Remove HTML, special characters, emojis (optional). 3. **Tokenization** → Split text into smaller units (words, sub-words, characters). 4. **Normalization** → Lowercasing, stemming, lemmatization. 5. **Stop-word Removal** → Drop high-frequency, low-information words (e.g., "the", "is"). 6. **Feature Extraction** → Convert text to numerical vectors (BoW, TF-IDF, embeddings). 7. **Modeling** → Apply ML/DL models (Naive Bayes, LSTM, Transformer). 8. **Post-processing** → Decode, format, and present output. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Range | |---|---------|--------------------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | **Bag-of-Words (BoW) Vector** | $V_{doc} = [c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_{\vert V \vert}]$ | Count of each vocabulary word in document | Integer counts | | 2 | **Term Frequency (TF)** | $TF(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Frequency of term $t$ in document $d$ | $[0, 1]$ | | 3 | **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** | $IDF(t) = \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right)$ | Rarity weight of term $t$ across $N$ documents | $\geq 0$ | | 4 | **TF-IDF Score** | $TF\text{-}IDF(t,d) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t)$ | Combined importance metric | $\geq 0$ | | 5 | **N-gram Probability (Unigram)** | $P(w_i) = \dfrac{C(w_i)}{\sum_{j} C(w_j)}$ | Probability of word $w_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | | 6 | **N-gram Probability (Bigram)** | $P(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \dfrac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$ | Conditional probability | $[0, 1]$ | | 7 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$ | Similarity between two vectors | $[-1, 1]$ | | 8 | **Perplexity (PP)** | $PP(W) = P(w_1 w_2 \ldots w_N)^{-1/N}$ | Model confidence metric | Lower is better | | 9 | **BLEU Score** | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Translation quality | $[0, 1]$ | | 10 | **Word2Vec Dot Product** | $\vec{v}_{w_i} \cdot \vec{v}_{w_j} = \Vert v_i \Vert \Vert v_j \Vert \cos(\theta)$ | Semantic similarity in embedding space | Real-valued | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in formulas (e.g., $|V|$ → write as $\vert V \vert$) when transcribing into your answer sheets to avoid rendering issues in LaTeX-compiled documents. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Search Engines** use TF-IDF and Cosine Similarity for ranking. - **Virtual Assistants** use N-gram language models and now transformers. - **Recommendation Systems** use embedding cosine similarity. - **Machine Translation evaluation** uses BLEU score as a standard benchmark. - **Prompt Engineers** use these metrics to evaluate prompt effectiveness on LLMs. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: TF-IDF from First Principles Let us derive the **TF-IDF** metric from its foundational concepts. Consider: - $N$ = total number of documents in the corpus $D$ - $t$ = a specific term (word) - $d$ = a specific document - $f_{t,d}$ = number of times $t$ appears in $d$ - $df(t)$ = number of documents containing $t$ #### Step 1: Compute Term Frequency (TF) $$TF(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$ **Logic:** We normalize raw counts by the total number of words in the document to prevent bias toward longer documents. A word appearing 5 times in a 100-word document is more "important" than 5 times in a 1000-word document. #### Step 2: Compute Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) $$IDF(t) = \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df(t)}\right)$$ **Logic:** If a word appears in *every* document (e.g., "the", "is"), $df(t) = N$, and $\log(N/N) = \log(1) = 0$. This naturally down-weights common words. The logarithm dampens the effect of very rare words, preventing them from skewing the metric. **Smoothed IDF** (used in practice to avoid division by zero): $$IDF(t) = \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df(t) + 1}\right) + 1$$ #### Step 3: Multiply TF and IDF $$TF\text{-}IDF(t,d) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t)$$ **Logic:** This product captures two intuitions simultaneously: - **High TF** → word is important *within* the document. - **High IDF** → word is rare *across* the corpus (hence discriminative). Their product gives high scores to words that are **frequent locally** but **rare globally** — exactly what we want for distinguishing documents. #### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example **Corpus:** - $D_1$: "The cat sat on the mat." - $D_2$: "The dog sat on the rug." - $D_3$: "Cats and dogs are pets." **Question:** Compute TF-IDF for the word **"cat"** in $D_1$. $$N = 3$$ **Step A — TF:** $$TF(\text{cat}, D_1) = \dfrac{1}{6} = 0.1667$$ (1 occurrence out of 6 total words in $D_1$) **Step B — IDF:** $$IDF(\text{cat}) = \log\left(\dfrac{3}{1 + 1}\right) + 1 = \log(1.5) + 1$$ $$IDF(\text{cat}) = 0.1761 + 1 = 1.1761$$ **Step C — TF-IDF:** $$TF\text{-}IDF(\text{cat}, D_1) = 0.1667 \times 1.1761 = 0.1960$$ > [!NOTE] > This metric is precisely what classical search engines like **Lucene/Elasticsearch** used before the deep learning revolution. Even today, it remains a strong baseline in many KTU lab examinations. ### 3.2 Derivation of Cosine Similarity Given two document vectors $\vec{A} = [a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n]$ and $\vec{B} = [b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n]$: $$\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2}}$$ **Why cosine, not Euclidean distance?** Cosine similarity measures the **angle** between vectors, making it **scale-invariant**. A 100-page document and a 1-page document about the same topic will have similar cosine similarity (~1.0) but large Euclidean distance. This is crucial in **prompt retrieval systems (RAG)**. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Classical NLP Techniques | Technique | Input | Output | Use Case | Limitation | |-----------|-------|--------|----------|------------| | **Tokenization** | Raw text | List of tokens | All NLP tasks | Language-dependent | | **Stemming (Porter)** | Tokens | Root form (may be non-word) | Information Retrieval | Over-stemming | | **Lemmatization** | Tokens | Dictionary base form | Question Answering | Slower (needs POS) | | **POS Tagging** | Sentence | Tagged sequence | Parsing, NER | Ambiguity | | **NER** | Sentence | Entity-tagged text | Information Extraction | Limited entity types | | **TF-IDF** | Corpus | Sparse vectors | Search, classification | Ignores word order | | **Word2Vec** | Corpus | Dense vectors | Semantic similarity | Static (no context) | | **BERT Embeddings** | Text | Contextual vectors | Modern NLU | Computationally expensive | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Classical NLP Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Text Input] --> B[Text Cleaning] B --> C[Tokenization] C --> D[Stop-word Removal] D --> E[Stemming and Lemmatization] E --> F[POS Tagging] F --> G[Named Entity Recognition] G --> H[Feature Extraction BoW or TFIDF or Embeddings] H --> I[Machine Learning Model] I --> J[Prediction or Classification Output] J --> K[Post Processing] K --> L[Final Structured Response] style A fill:#e1f5ff style L fill:#d4edda style H fill:#fff3cd style I fill:#f8d7da ``` ### 4.2 Levels of Linguistic Analysis Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD P[Phonological Level
Sound Structure] --> M[Morphological Level
Word Formation] M --> L[Lexical Level
Word Meaning] L --> S[Syntactic Level
Grammar and Structure] S --> SE[Semantic Level
Sentence Meaning] SE --> D[Discourse Level
Cross Sentence Relations] D --> PR[Pragmatic Level
Intent and Context] style P fill:#ffe7e7 style M fill:#ffe7d4 style L fill:#fff4d4 style S fill:#e7f5ff style SE fill:#d4f4dd style D fill:#e7d4f4 style PR fill:#f4d4e7 ``` ### 4.3 Prompt Engineering ↔ NLP Connection (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR USER[User Prompt] --> NLP1[NLP Tokenization Layer] NLP1 --> NLP2[NLP Semantic Parser] NLP2 --> NLP3[Intent Classification] NLP3 --> NLP4[Context Assembly] NLP4 --> LLM[Large Language Model Core] LLM --> NLP5[Output Decoder] NLP5 --> RESP[Final Response to User] style USER fill:#cfe2ff style LLM fill:#f8d7da style RESP fill:#d1e7dd style NLP1 fill:#fff3cd style NLP2 fill:#fff3cd style NLP3 fill:#fff3cd style NLP4 fill:#fff3cd style NLP5 fill:#fff3cd ``` ### 4.4 TF-IDF Computation Flow (Detailed Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD DOCS[Corpus of N Documents] --> T1[Step 1 Tokenize Each Document] T1 --> T2[Step 2 Build Vocabulary] T2 --> T3[Step 3 Compute Term Frequency TF per term per doc] T3 --> T4[Step 4 Compute Document Frequency DF for each term] T4 --> T5[Step 5 Compute IDF using log N divided by DF] T5 --> T6[Step 6 Multiply TF x IDF for each term] T6 --> OUT[TFIDF Vector Matrix of shape N x V] style DOCS fill:#e1f5ff style OUT fill:#d4edda style T5 fill:#fff3cd ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define Natural Language Processing (NLP). List any four real-world applications of NLP.* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence and Computational Linguistics that deals with the interaction between computers and human (natural) languages. It involves developing algorithms and models that enable machines to **understand, interpret, generate, and respond** to text or speech in a human-like manner. **Four Real-World Applications:** 1. **Machine Translation** — Google Translate, DeepL. 2. **Sentiment Analysis** — Brand monitoring on Twitter / X. 3. **Chatbots and Virtual Assistants** — Siri, Alexa, ChatGPT. 4. **Spam Detection** — Gmail's email classifier. *(Any 4 valid applications accepted — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for applications)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** *Differentiate between Stemming and Lemmatization with suitable examples.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Stemming | Lemmatization | |-----------|----------|---------------| | **Definition** | Crude heuristic process of chopping off word endings to reach a root form | Dictionary-based process of returning a word to its valid linguistic base form (lemma) | | **Linguistic Accuracy** | Low — may produce non-words | High — produces valid words | | **Speed** | Fast | Slower (requires morphological analysis) | | **Example (caring)** | Stem → **car** | Lemma → **care** | | **Example (better)** | Stem → **bett** | Lemma → **good** | | **Algorithm Example** | Porter Stemmer | WordNet Lemmatizer | *(3 marks — clear tabular comparison is the KTU-preferred format)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the seven levels of linguistic analysis in NLP with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the TF-IDF vector for the term "**data**" in the given corpus. Show all steps. **(7 marks)** **Corpus:** - $D_1$: "Data science is the future of data-driven decisions." - $D_2$: "Big data analytics transforms data into insights." - $D_3$: "Science and data go hand in hand." --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Seven Levels of Linguistic Analysis (7 marks):** 1. **Phonological Level** — Deals with the sound system of language. Example: distinguishing vowels from consonants in speech recognition systems. 2. **Morphological Level** — Studies the structure of words and morphemes (smallest meaning units). Example: "**unhappiness**" → *un* + *happy* + *ness*. 3. **Lexical Level** — Concerned with the meaning of individual words and their lexical categories. Example: Identifying that "**run**" can be a noun or verb (Word Sense Disambiguation). 4. **Syntactic Level** — Analyzes the grammatical structure of sentences. Example: Parsing "**The boy ate the apple**" into a Subject–Verb–Object (SVO) structure. 5. **Semantic Level** — Focuses on the literal meaning of sentences. Example: Interpreting "**Time flies like an arrow**" — recognizing "flies" as a verb, not a noun. 6. **Discourse Level** — Examines relationships *between* sentences. Example: Determining that "**he**" in the second sentence refers to "**John**" from the first sentence (Coreference Resolution). 7. **Pragmatic Level** — Considers the speaker's intent and real-world context. Example: "**Can you pass the salt?**" is a request, not a yes/no question (Speech Act Theory). *(1 mark per level, total 7 marks)* --- **Part (b) — TF-IDF Computation (7 marks):** **Step 1: Tokenize the corpus and build the vocabulary.** After cleaning and tokenization: - $D_1$ has **7 tokens**: {data, science, is, the, future, of, datadriven, decisions} - $D_2$ has **6 tokens**: {big, data, analytics, transforms, into, insights} - $D_3$ has **7 tokens**: {science, and, data, go, hand, in} **Step 2: Identify document frequency for the term "data".** $$df(\text{data}) = 3$$ (The term "data" appears in all 3 documents: $D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$.) **Step 3: Compute Total Documents and IDF.** $$N = 3$$ $$IDF(\text{data}) = \log\left(\dfrac{N}{df(\text{data})}\right) = \log\left(\dfrac{3}{3}\right) = \log(1) = 0$$ **Step 4: Compute TF for "data" in each document.** $$TF(\text{data}, D_1) = \dfrac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429$$ (Assuming "data" appears once in $D_1$ and "datadriven" is a single token or we count it as separate) $$TF(\text{data}, D_2) = \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$ $$TF(\text{data}, D_3) = \dfrac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429$$ **Step 5: Compute TF-IDF for "data" in $D_1$.** $$TF\text{-}IDF(\text{data}, D_1) = TF \times IDF = 0.1429 \times 0 = 0$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Insight:** Since "data" appears in *every* document, its IDF is **zero**, hence its TF-IDF is **zero** in all documents. This is the **correct mathematical behavior** — common words are penalized. Students who incorrectly compute a non-zero value will lose marks. **Step 6: Interpretation (1 mark).** The term "data" is a **stop-word-like** term in this corpus. It carries no discriminative power. A search engine using TF-IDF would **not** rank documents based on the word "data" alone. For meaningful retrieval, we should use **rarer** terms like "transforms" or "insights" from $D_2$. *(Full marks breakdown: 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for IDF, 2 marks for TF-IDF product, 1 mark for correct interpretation)* --- #### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain the term "Tokenization" in NLP. Describe the different tokenization techniques with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the Bag-of-Words (BoW) model and its limitations in representing textual data. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Tokenization (7 marks):** **Definition:** Tokenization is the process of breaking a stream of text into smaller units called **tokens**. These tokens can be words, sub-words, characters, or sentences. It is the **first and most fundamental step** in any NLP pipeline. **Tokenization Techniques:** 1. **Word Tokenization** — Splits text by whitespace and punctuation. - Input: `"NLP is fun!"` - Output: `["NLP", "is", "fun", "!"]` 2. **Sentence Tokenization** — Splits text into sentences using punctuation cues (`.`, `!`, `?`). - Input: `"Hello world. How are you?"` - Output: `["Hello world.", "How are you?"]` 3. **Character Tokenization** — Splits text into individual characters. Useful for languages without clear word boundaries (e.g., Chinese). - Input: `"cat"` - Output: `["c", "a", "t"]` 4. **Sub-word Tokenization (BPE/WordPiece)** — Splits rare words into frequent sub-units. Used in modern LLMs (GPT, BERT). - Input: `"unhappiness"` - Output: `["un", "happiness"]` or `["un", "happy", "ness"]` 5. **Whitespace Tokenization** — Simplest form; splits only on spaces. - Input: `"Hello world!"` - Output: `["Hello", "world!"]` - Limitation: Punctuation remains attached to words. 6. **Regex-based Tokenization** — Uses regular expressions for fine-grained control. - Pattern: `r'\b\w+\b'` - Example: `"e-mail"` → `["e", "mail"]` *(1 mark per technique with example + 1 mark for definition = 7 marks)* --- **Part (b) — Bag-of-Words (BoW) Model and Its Limitations (7 marks):** **BoW Model Explanation (3.5 marks):** The **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** model is a simplifying representation used in NLP and Information Retrieval. In this model, a text (such as a sentence or a document) is represented as the **multiset (bag)** of its words, **disregarding grammar and word order** but **keeping multiplicity**. **Mathematical Formulation:** Given a vocabulary $V = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_{\vert V \vert}\}$, a document $d$ is represented as a vector: $$\vec{d} = [c(w_1, d), c(w_2, d), \ldots, c(w_{\vert V \vert}, d)]$$ where $c(w_i, d)$ is the count of word $w_i$ in document $d$. **Example:** - $D_1$: "I love NLP and I love Python." - $D_2$: "Python is great for NLP." **Vocabulary:** $\{$ I, love, NLP, and, Python, is, great, for $\}$ **Vectors:** $$\vec{D_1} = [2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0]$$ $$\vec{D_2} = [0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1]$$ **Limitations (3.5 marks):** 1. **Loss of Word Order** — "Dog bites man" and "Man bites dog" become identical vectors. Critical meaning is destroyed. 2. **No Semantic Understanding** — The vectors treat "happy" and "joyful" as completely unrelated words, despite being synonyms. 3. **High Dimensionality & Sparsity** — For a vocabulary of 50,000 words, each document vector has 50,000 dimensions, most of which are zero. This causes memory inefficiency. 4. **Ignores Context** — "Bank" (river bank vs. financial bank) is treated as the same word. Polysemy is not handled. 5. **No Phrase Detection** — "ice cream" is treated as two separate words, losing the compound concept. 6. **Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) Issue** — New words not in the training vocabulary are simply dropped. > [!NOTE] > **Modern Alternative:** These limitations are precisely why **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe)** and **Contextual Embeddings (BERT, GPT)** were developed. They address semantic understanding and contextual meaning that BoW fundamentally cannot. *(7 marks — clean tabular or bulleted comparison preferred by KTU examiners)* --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in NLP Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Stemming with Lemmatization** — Examiners *will* deduct 0.5–1 mark if you use them interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the smoothing term (+1) in IDF** — When $df(t) = 0$ or $df(t) = N$, students forget the +1 and write $1/0$ or $\log(1) = 0$ incorrectly. Always state the smoothed version. > 3. **Skipping the interpretation step in TF-IDF** — Computing numbers without explaining what they *mean* loses the application-level marks (1–2 marks). > 4. **Writing Python code in theory paper** — KTU 2024 scheme is OBE-based. For NLP theory questions, **do not** write code. Use mathematical notation. > 5. **Not mentioning the limitation of BoW** — This is the most frequently skipped 1-mark question. > 6. **Mixing up Precision, Recall, and F1 with BLEU/Perplexity** — These are distinct evaluation metrics. Know the difference. > 7. **Forgetting to lowercase or normalize before tokenization** — Always mention pre-processing steps explicitly. --- ### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fundamentals of NLP** > > ✅ **NLP** = Computational Linguistics + AI + CS — enables machines to process human language. > > ✅ **7 Linguistic Levels** = Phonological → Morphological → Lexical → Syntactic → Semantic → Discourse → Pragmatic. > > ✅ **Pipeline Order** = Cleaning → Tokenization → Stop-word Removal → Normalization → Feature Extraction → Modeling → Output. > > ✅ **Tokenization Types** = Word, Sentence, Character, Sub-word (BPE), Whitespace, Regex. > > ✅ **Stemming vs Lemmatization** = Stemming is rule-based chopping (Porter); Lemmatization is dictionary-based (WordNet). > > ✅ **Bag-of-Words** = Count-based vector representation; **ignores order and semantics**. > > ✅ **TF** = $\dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum f_{t',d}}$ — Normalized term frequency. > > ✅ **IDF** = $\log\dfrac{N}{df(t)+1}$ — Logarithmic inverse document frequency. > > ✅ **TF-IDF** = $TF \times IDF$ — Standard baseline for IR and text classification. > > ✅ **Cosine Similarity** = $\dfrac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$ — Preferred for document similarity (scale-invariant). > > ✅ **BLEU** = measures translation quality; ranges 0 to 1. > > ✅ **Perplexity** = lower is better; measures language model confidence. > > ✅ **Modern NLP = Transformers** (BERT, GPT) handle context; BoW/TF-IDF are **classical baselines only**. > > ✅ **For Prompt Engineering (this course):** Every prompt is first tokenized, then semantically parsed, then intent-classified before reaching the LLM. **You cannot engineer prompts effectively without understanding these NLP layers.**

topics.title: Overview of Language Models: From Rule-Based Systems to Transformer Architectures (e.g., GPT, BERT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Language Models: From Rule-Based Systems to Transformer Architectures (e.g., GPT, BERT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Language Model (LM) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Language Model** is a probabilistic computational system that assigns a probability distribution over sequences of tokens (words, sub-words, or characters) drawn from a vocabulary $V$. Formally, an LM learns the joint probability $P(w_1, w_2, w_3, \dots, w_n)$ over a token sequence, which by the chain rule of probability decomposes into a product of conditional probabilities:
> $$P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

A language model's job, therefore, is to **predict the most probable next token** given the preceding context. Modern transformer-based LMs parameterize these conditional probabilities using neural networks with hundreds of millions to hundreds of billions of parameters $\theta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — The 4 Evolutionary Eras**
> 1. **Rule-Based Systems (1950s–1990s)** — Hand-crafted linguistic rules (e.g., ELIZA, SHRDLU).
> 2. **Statistical Language Models (1990s–2010s)** — N-gram models using Markov assumptions.
> 3. **Neural Language Models (2013–2017)** — RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs (word2vec, GloVe).
> 4. **Transformer Architectures (2017–Present)** — Self-attention based models (BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Eager Library Assistant"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for a First-Time Learner**
> Imagine a **library assistant** who has read every book in the library, and when you say a half-sentence aloud, the assistant finishes it for you the way the **most knowledgeable author** would.
>
> - **Old Rule-Based Assistant (1960s):** Only follows a **cheat-sheet of grammar rules** ("if you see *the*, expect a *noun* next"). Breaks the moment you use slang or an unfamiliar word.
> - **Statistical Assistant (1990s):** Counts **how often word-pairs/triples** appeared together in old books. Smart but short-sighted — forgets what was said 10 sentences ago.
> - **Neural Assistant (RNN, 2013):** Has a **short-term memory** that gets fuzzy over long sentences. Like a person who remembers the last few words perfectly but forgets the start.
> - **Transformer Assistant (2017+):** Has a **super-powered spotlight** that can instantly look at *every* word in the entire conversation at once and decide which words matter most for predicting the next one. This is **self-attention**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology — The KTU Vocabulary Bank

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Token** | Smallest unit processed by an LM (word-piece / sub-word) | "unhappiness" → `["un", "##happiness"]` |
| **Vocabulary ($V$)** | The finite set of all unique tokens the model knows | $\vert V \vert$ ≈ 50,000 for BERT-base |
| **Context Window** | Maximum number of tokens the model attends to at once | GPT-4: 128k tokens; BERT-base: 512 |
| **Embedding** | Dense vector representation of a token in $\mathbb{R}^d$ | $d = 768$ for BERT-base |
| **Perplexity (PPL)** | Standard LM evaluation metric — lower is better | PPL = 1 → perfect; PPL = 100 → confused |
| **Self-Attention** | Mechanism that lets every token look at every other token | Computes weighted importance scores |
| **Pre-training** | Initial unsupervised training on massive text corpora | Next-token prediction / Masked LM |
| **Fine-tuning** | Task-specific supervised training on smaller labeled data | Sentiment classification, QA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Metric Reminder**
> The **Perplexity** of a language model over a sequence of $N$ tokens is defined as:
> $$\text{PPL}(W) = P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)^{-1/N} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$
> A perplexity of $k$ means the model is as "perplexed" as choosing uniformly among $k$ possibilities.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Paradigms — Operational Breakdown

#### **Paradigm 1: Rule-Based Systems (1950s–1990s)**

- **Operational Logic:** Linguists hand-code `IF-THEN` production rules in formal grammars (e.g., Context-Free Grammars, Augmented Transition Networks).
- **Core Idea:** A sentence is **parsed as a syntax tree**; meaning is derived from rule-triggered semantic attachments.
- **Famous Example:** *ELIZA (1966)* by Joseph Weizenbaum — used simple pattern matching to simulate a Rogerian psychotherapist.
- **Why It Failed:** **B brittleness** — cannot generalize beyond coded rules; zero robustness to noise, ambiguity, or unseen vocabulary.
- **Real-World Utility Today:** Still used in **deterministic parsers**, **chatbot slot-filling backbones**, **regular-expression pre-processors**, and **lexical analyzers in compilers**.

#### **Paradigm 2: Statistical N-Gram Models (1990s–2010s)**

- **Operational Logic:** Approximate the full joint probability using the **Markov Assumption of order $n-1$**:
$$P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-(n-1)}, \dots, w_{i-1})$$
- **Estimation:** Frequencies from corpora with **Laplace (Add-k) smoothing** to handle zero-counts.
- **The Curse of Dimensionality:** The number of possible $n$-grams grows as $\vert V \vert^n$ — for $n=5$ and $\vert V \vert = 10^5$, this exceeds $10^{25}$, making storage and estimation infeasible.
- **Why It Failed:** Cannot capture **long-range dependencies** ("The **man** who came to dinner… brought **his** dog" — agreement is hundreds of words apart).

#### **Paradigm 3: Neural Language Models (2013–2017)**

- **Operational Logic:** Replace frequency tables with a **learned continuous embedding space**. Each word $w_i$ is mapped to a dense vector $e_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, and the conditional probability is computed by a neural network.
- **Architectures:** **RNN → LSTM → GRU → Bidirectional LSTM.**
- **Key Formulation (RNN-LM):** At time-step $t$, the hidden state $h_t$ summarizes the history:
$$h_t = f(W_h h_{t-1} + W_e e_{t-1} + b)$$
$$\hat{P}(w_t \mid w_{<t}) = \text{softmax}(W_o h_t + b_o)$$
- **Limitation:** Sequential processing ⇒ **no parallelism** during training; **vanishing gradients** still limit long-context recall.

#### **Paradigm 4: Transformer Architectures (2017–Present)**

- **Operational Logic:** **Discard recurrence entirely.** Use **self-attention** to compute, in parallel, how every token relates to every other token in the sequence.
- **Landmark Paper:** *Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need," NeurIPS 2017.*
- **Two Dominant Families:**
  - **Encoder-Only (Auto-Encoding):** BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) — best for *understanding* tasks (classification, NER, QA extraction).
  - **Decoder-Only (Auto-Regressive):** GPT (Generative Pre-Trained Transformer) — best for *generation* tasks (text completion, chat, code).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Key Equations for KTU Module 1**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Used In | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chain-Rule Decomposition | $P(w_{1:n}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$ | All LMs | Foundation of token prediction |
| 2 | Perplexity (PPL) | $\text{PPL} = \exp\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{N}\sum \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\bigr)$ | Evaluation | Lower = better LM |
| 3 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Training | Equivalent to NLL for next-token |
| 4 | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\tfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$ | Transformers | Core of every transformer block |
| 5 | Multi-Head Attention | $\text{MHA} = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\dots,\text{head}_h) W^O$ | Transformers | $h$ parallel attention subspaces |
| 6 | Sinusoidal Positional Encoding (even) | $PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\bigl(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{model}}\bigr)$ | Original Transformer | Injects order info |
| 7 | Sinusoidal Positional Encoding (odd) | $PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\bigl(pos / 10000^{2i/d_{model}}\bigr)$ | Original Transformer | Complements even form |
| 8 | Layer Normalization | $\text{LayerNorm}(x) = \gamma \odot \tfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma} + \beta$ | Transformer blocks | Stabilizes training |
| 9 | BERT MLM Objective | $L_{MLM} = -\mathbb{E}\bigl[\log P(x_{mask} \mid x_{\setminus mask})\bigr]$ | BERT Pre-training | Predict 15% masked tokens |
| 10 | GPT Autoregressive Objective | $L_{AR} = -\sum_{t=1}^{n} \log P(x_t \mid x_{<t})$ | GPT Pre-training | Next-token prediction |
| 11 | Word2Vec Skip-gram | $P(w_O \mid w_I) = \tfrac{\exp(v_{w_O}^{\top} v_{w_I})}{\sum_{w \in V} \exp(v_w^{\top} v_{w_I})}$ | Static embeddings | Context-free vectors |
| 12 | GloVe Co-occurrence | $J = \sum_{i,j} f(X_{ij})\bigl(v_i^{\top} \tilde{v}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij}\bigr)^2$ | Static embeddings | Global + local info |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note (KTU Board Exams)**
> When writing the scaled dot-product attention in your answer script, **never** write $\frac{QK^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ without the parentheses around the denominator — examiners often mark this wrong because the square root must apply to **only $d_k$**, not the entire fraction. Always write $\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ or parenthesize carefully.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** BERT reduced query-doc mismatch errors by ~10% after deployment in 2019.
- **Code Completion (Copilot, Cursor):** GPT-style decoder models trained on GitHub corpora.
- **Medical NLP (BioBERT, ClinicalBERT):** Domain-adapted transformers for clinical notes.
- **Speech & Translation (Whisper, Google Translate):** Transformer encoders + decoders.
- **Robotic Process Automation:** Fine-tuned BERT for invoice parsing and intent classification.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Chain Rule to Perplexity

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show rigorously how the perplexity metric follows from information-theoretic principles — a common KTU Part-B derivation.

**Step 1 — Start with the joint probability of a corpus $W = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N)$** using the chain rule of probability:

$$P(W) = P(w_1) \cdot P(w_2 \mid w_1) \cdot P(w_3 \mid w_1, w_2) \cdots P(w_N \mid w_1, \dots, w_{N-1})$$

**Step 2 — Write it compactly as a product of conditionals:**

$$P(W) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{i-1}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 3 — Take the logarithm to convert the product into a sum** (since logs of products = sums of logs, and this is numerically stable):

$$\log P(W) = \log \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

**Step 4 — Compute the average log-probability per token** (this is the **Cross-Entropy** of the model on the corpus, denoted $H$):

$$H(W) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$$

*Note the negative sign:* Cross-entropy is conventionally defined as the negative average log-likelihood.

**Step 5 — Recognize that perplexity is just the exponentiation of cross-entropy.** This converts the log-scale "bits per token" back into an "effective branching factor":

$$\text{PPL}(W) = e^{H(W)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent alternative form using the joint probability directly:**

$$\text{PPL}(W) = \left[ P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_N) \right]^{-1/N}$$

**Interpretation:** If $\text{PPL} = k$, the model behaves as if at every step it is choosing uniformly from $k$ equally likely next tokens.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Scaled Dot-Product Self-Attention

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the attention formula step-by-step, including the **why** behind the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling — a guaranteed KTU 14-mark question.

**Step 1 — Setup:** For an input sequence, we project the embeddings into three matrices using learned weight matrices $W^Q, W^K, W^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{model} \times d_k}$:

$$Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw compatibility scores** by taking the dot product of every query with every key:

$$S = Q K^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the scaling factor $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}$:**
  
*Why scale?* Assume $q$ and $k$ have components that are i.i.d. with mean $0$ and variance $1$. Then the variance of their dot product $q^{\top} k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i$ is $d_k$. For large $d_k$ (e.g., $64$ in the original paper), the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions of extremely small gradients (saturated softmax). Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to $1$:

$$\text{var}(q_i k_i) = \mathbb{E}[q_i^2]\mathbb{E}[k_i^2] - (\mathbb{E}[q_i]\mathbb{E}[k_i])^2 = 1 \cdot 1 - 0 = 1$$
$$\text{var}(q^{\top} k) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{var}(q_i k_i) = d_k$$
$$\text{std}(q^{\top} k) = \sqrt{d_k}$$

**Step 4 — Apply row-wise softmax to get attention weights (each row sums to 1):**

$$A = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Weighted sum of values using attention weights:**

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A V = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

**Step 6 — Extend to multi-head attention** (run $h$ heads in parallel, concatenate, project):

$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q, K W_i^K, V W_i^V)$$
$$\text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \dots, \text{head}_h) W^O$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sinusoidal Positional Encoding

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Justify the position-encoding formula. This is the most-asked derivation in KTU Module 1.

**Step 1 — The problem:** Self-attention is **permutation-invariant** — it treats input as an unordered set. We must inject position information.

**Step 2 — Desired properties:** (a) Unique encoding per position, (b) Generalization to longer sequences than seen in training, (c) Easy for the model to learn **relative** offsets $k$ via $PE(pos+k) = f(PE(pos))$.

**Step 3 — Use sinusoids of different frequencies** along dimension $i$:

$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i / d_{model}}}\right), \quad PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i / d_{model}}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Verify the relative-position property using the angle-addition formula** $\sin(a+b) = \sin a \cos b + \cos a \sin b$:

$$PE_{(pos+k, 2i)} = \sin(\omega_i(pos+k)) = \sin(\omega_i pos)\cos(\omega_i k) + \cos(\omega_i pos)\sin(\omega_i k)$$

$$PE_{(pos+k, 2i)} = PE_{(pos, 2i)} \cdot \cos(\omega_i k) + PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} \cdot \sin(\omega_i k)$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** $PE_{pos+k}$ is a **linear function of $PE_{pos}$** with coefficients $\cos(\omega_i k)$ and $\sin(\omega_i k)$ that depend only on the relative offset $k$. This is exactly what the model needs to learn relative attention.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: BERT vs. GPT

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Favorite Comparison Table (Asked Frequently)**

| Dimension | **BERT (Encoder-Only)** | **GPT (Decoder-Only)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Bidirectional self-attention | Masked (unidirectional) self-attention |
| **Attention Mask** | Full $n \times n$ (sees future too) | Lower-triangular (sees only past) |
| **Pre-training Objective** | Masked Language Modeling (MLM) + NSP | Autoregressive Next-Token Prediction |
| **Information Flow** | Sees context from **both sides** | Sees context only from the **left** |
| **Best Suited For** | Classification, NER, Extractive QA, Embeddings | Text generation, Chat, Summarization, Code |
| **Output** | Per-token vector representations | A single next-token probability distribution |
| **Original Paper** | Devlin et al., NAACL 2019 | Radford et al., 2018 (GPT-1) |
| **Typical Size Variants** | BERT-base (110M), BERT-large (340M) | GPT-2 (1.5B), GPT-3 (175B), GPT-4 (undisclosed) |
| **Position Encoding** | Learned absolute | Learned absolute (earlier) / RoPE (later) |
| **Activation** | GELU | GELU |
| **Normalization** | LayerNorm (post-norm in original) | LayerNorm (pre-norm in GPT-2+) |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a Language Model. With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an RNN-based Language Model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Language Model** is a probabilistic system that estimates the probability distribution $P(w_{1:n})$ over a token sequence, factorized via the chain rule as $P(w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$.

An **RNN-LM** processes tokens sequentially: at time-step $t$, the hidden state $h_t = f(W_h h_{t-1} + W_e e_{t-1})$ encodes the prefix, and $\hat{P}(w_t \mid w_{<t}) = \text{softmax}(W_o h_t)$ produces the next-token distribution. The same weights are reused at every step, giving it a **theoretically infinite context** — though in practice vanishing gradients limit this.

**Diagram (sketch in exam):** Boxes $h_1 \rightarrow h_2 \rightarrow h_3$ with arrows labeled $W_h$, and an output arrow from each $h_t$ to a softmax over the vocabulary.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between encoder-only and decoder-only transformer architectures. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Encoder-Only (e.g., BERT) | Decoder-Only (e.g., GPT) |
|---|---|---|
| Attention Direction | Bidirectional (sees full context) | Unidirectional (sees only past tokens) |
| Pre-training Task | Masked Language Modeling (predict 15% masked tokens) | Autoregressive next-token prediction |
| Use Case | Understanding tasks — classification, NER, QA | Generation tasks — text completion, dialogue |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning**
> A common 1-mark deduction occurs when students **write that BERT "predicts the next word"** — this is GPT's job. BERT predicts *masked* tokens, not the next token. Memorize the distinction.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Transformer Deep-Dive**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **Transformer Encoder** as proposed in *"Attention Is All You Need"* (Vaswani et al., 2017). Discuss the role of **Multi-Head Self-Attention**, **Layer Normalization**, and **Positional Encoding** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** formula from first principles. Justify **why** the scaling factor $\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_k}}$ is necessary and what would happen if it were omitted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (must draw; 2 marks):**

```
Input Tokens
     ↓
Input Embedding + Positional Encoding
     ↓
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Multi-Head Self-Attention          │ ← Sublayer 1
│  → Add & LayerNorm                  │
│  → Feed-Forward Network (FFN)       │
│  → Add & LayerNorm                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
     ↓ (repeated N = 6 times)
     ↓
Encoder Output
```

**Detailed explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Input Embedding (1 mark):** Each input token $w_i$ is mapped to a $d_{model}$-dimensional vector (e.g., $d_{model}=512$). Since self-attention is permutation-invariant, a **positional encoding** $PE \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_{model}}$ is added element-wise to inject order information. The original paper used fixed sinusoidal encodings:
$$PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})$$

2. **Multi-Head Self-Attention (2 marks):** The input is projected into $h$ parallel sets of $(Q, K, V)$ matrices. Each head learns to attend to information from a different representational subspace:
$$\text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(XW_i^Q, XW_i^K, XW_i^V)$$
The $h$ outputs are concatenated and projected with $W^O$. This allows the model to **jointly attend to information from different positions and different subspaces**.

3. **Add & LayerNorm (1 mark):** A **residual connection** wraps each sublayer: $\text{output} = \text{LayerNorm}(x + \text{Sublayer}(x))$. This mitigates vanishing gradients and stabilizes training of deep stacks.

4. **Feed-Forward Network (1 mark):** A position-wise two-layer MLP with ReLU/GELU activation: $\text{FFN}(x) = \max(0, xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2$. It adds **non-linearity** and increases representational capacity.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Given query matrix $Q$, key matrix $K$, value matrix $V$ all of shape $n \times d_k$:

1. **Raw scores:** $S = QK^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, where $S_{ij}$ measures the compatibility of token $i$'s query with token $j$'s key.

2. **Scaled scores:** $S' = \dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}$

3. **Attention weights:** $A = \text{softmax}(S') \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — each row is a probability distribution over keys.

4. **Output:** $\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = A V = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$

**Justification of scaling (2 marks):**

Assume each component of $q, k$ is i.i.d. with mean $0$, variance $1$. Then:
$$\text{var}(q^{\top}k) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{var}(q_i k_i) = d_k$$

For large $d_k$ (e.g., $64$), the dot products have large magnitude. The **softmax** function:
$$\text{softmax}(x_i) = \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$$

becomes saturated when the inputs are large — its output collapses to a one-hot vector and **its gradient approaches zero**, halting learning. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ keeps the variance of the dot product at $1$, ensuring the softmax operates in a well-conditioned regime with healthy gradients.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> Many students write *"scaling is needed to make the computation faster"*. This is **wrong** — mark loss of 2 marks. The correct reason is **variance normalization to prevent softmax saturation**. Memorize this distinction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **evolution of Language Models** from rule-based systems to transformer architectures. Highlight the **key limitations** of each era that motivated the next. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **BERT** and **GPT** in terms of architecture, pre-training objective, attention mechanism, and downstream applications. Why is **GPT better suited for open-ended generation** while **BERT excels at classification**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Four-era evolution table (must be presented in exam; 4 marks):**

| Era | Year Range | Representative System | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Rule-Based | 1950s–1990s | ELIZA, SHRDLU, parsers using CFG | Hand-crafted rules; **brittle**, no generalization |
| 2. Statistical N-Gram | 1990s–2010s | N-gram with Katz backoff, HMM-LM | **Curse of dimensionality**, no long-range context |
| 3. Neural RNN/LSTM | 2013–2017 | word2vec, ELMo, AWD-LSTM | **Sequential bottleneck**, vanishing gradients |
| 4. Transformer | 2017–Present | BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA | High compute cost; hallucination; bias |

**Narrative explanation (3 marks):**

- Rule-based systems relied on **explicit linguistic knowledge** but could not handle the **productivity** and **ambiguity** of natural language. The shift to **statistical methods** was driven by the availability of large digital corpora (e.g., Brown Corpus, Wall Street Journal) and the realization that *language is fundamentally probabilistic*.
- N-gram models, however, suffered from the **curse of dimensionality** — the number of possible trigrams is $\vert V \vert^3$, far exceeding any corpus size — and **Markov blindness** to long-range dependencies.
- The **neural revolution** (Bengio 2003, Mikolov 2010, word2vec 2013) replaced sparse counts with **dense embeddings**, capturing **semantic similarity** (king − man + woman ≈ queen). RNNs added a **recurrent hidden state**, theoretically unbounded context, but in practice gradients vanished over long sequences.
- The 2017 **Transformer** paper eliminated recurrence entirely, replacing it with **self-attention** — a fully parallel mechanism that directly computes pairwise token interactions in $O(1)$ path length. This unlocked **scale**, **parallelism on GPUs**, and **transfer learning via pre-training**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | BERT | GPT |
|---|---|---|
| Attention Type | **Bidirectional** — full $n \times n$ | **Causal/masked** — lower triangular $n \times n$ |
| Pre-training | MLM (mask 15% tokens, predict them) | Next-token prediction (autoregressive) |
| Token Visibility | Sees left + right context | Sees only left context |
| Strengths | Deep bidirectional **understanding** | Coherent, long-form **generation** |

**Why GPT excels at generation (2 marks):**

GPT's causal mask forces the model to learn $P(w_t \mid w_{<t})$ — a **proper language-modeling objective** that directly aligns with generation: at inference, you simply sample/argmax one token at a time and feed it back. The left-to-right factorization matches the natural left-to-right text we produce.

**Why BERT excels at classification (1 mark):**

BERT's bidirectional attention allows each token's representation to be informed by **both past and future context**, producing **rich contextual embeddings** ideal for tasks where the entire input is available upfront (e.g., sentiment classification, named-entity recognition). Its MLM pre-training also aligns well with **fill-in-the-blank** style tasks and produces a `[CLS]` vector that summarizes the whole input for sentence-level classification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Trap**
> A frequent 1–2 mark loss: students claim "BERT cannot generate text." This is **partially true** — vanilla BERT with MLM is not designed for free-form generation, but **BART, T5, and encoder-decoder variants** do generate. In the KTU context, the **expected answer** is the architectural explanation (causal vs. bidirectional), not a blanket claim.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Overview of Language Models**

- [ ] A **Language Model** estimates $P(w_{1:n}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(w_i \mid w_{<i})$.
- [ ] **Perplexity** = $\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i})\right)$; **lower is better**.
- [ ] **Four eras:** Rule-Based → Statistical N-Gram → Neural (RNN/LSTM) → **Transformer**.
- [ ] N-gram models use the **Markov assumption of order $n-1$** and suffer from the **curse of dimensionality**.
- [ ] RNNs add a **recurrent hidden state** but are **sequential and gradient-unstable**.
- [ ] **Self-attention** computes pairwise token interactions in **$O(1)$ path length**, enabling **full parallelism**.
- [ ] **Scaled Dot-Product Attention:** $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\dfrac{QK^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$.
- [ ] **Why $\sqrt{d_k}$?** To normalize the variance of dot products to $1$, **preventing softmax saturation**.
- [ ] **Multi-Head Attention** = $h$ parallel attention heads, concatenated and projected by $W^O$.
- [ ] **Positional encoding** is required because self-attention is **permutation-invariant**. Original paper uses **sinusoidal** $PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_{model}})$.
- [ ] **BERT (2018, Google)** = **Encoder-only**, **bidirectional**, pre-trained with **MLM (15% mask)** + NSP. Best for **understanding**.
