- [ ] **GPT (2018, OpenAI)** = **Decoder-only**, **causal/masked**, pre-trained with **next-token prediction**. Best for **generation**.
- [ ] Layer Normalization + residual connections are critical for **training stability** in deep transformer stacks.
- [ ] Feed-Forward Networks inside transformer blocks are **position-wise** (applied independently to each token).
- [ ] GELU is the standard activation in BERT/GPT; ReLU was used in the original transformer.
- [ ] The transformer paper is **Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need," NeurIPS 2017** — cite this in answers.
- [ ] **Context window** sizes: BERT-base = 512 tokens; GPT-4 Turbo = 128k tokens.
- [ ] Standard embedding dimensions: $d_{model} = 512$ (original), $768$ (BERT-base), $1024$ (BERT-large), $1280$ (GPT-2 medium).
- [ ] Always distinguish **encoder** vs **decoder** vs **encoder-decoder** (e.g., T5, BART) architectures in exam answers.
- [ ] Perplexity and cross-entropy are **monotonically related**: $\text{PPL} = e^{H}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Prompts: Definition, Importance, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Understanding Prompts: Definition, Importance, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Prompt** is any natural-language input — comprising instructions, context, examples, queries, or constraints — that is supplied to a **Large Language Model (LLM)** or generative AI system to elicit a specific, structured, and goal-oriented response. In the formal terminology of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Prompt Engineering* (PECST868), a prompt is defined as the **primary interface contract** between the human user and the underlying transformer-based language model, functioning as both the *intent specification* and the *behavioural boundary* for the AI's output.

Mathematically (in a simplified abstraction), if we denote the language model as a stochastic function $\mathcal{M}$, and the prompt as $\mathcal{P}$, then the generated output token sequence $\mathcal{Y}$ is obtained as:

$$\mathcal{Y} = \mathcal{M}(\mathcal{P})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 scheme frames prompts not merely as "questions" but as **structured artefacts** that include: (1) an *Instruction*, (2) *Context*, (3) *Input Data*, and (4) an *Output Indicator*. Examiners expect students to use this four-part vocabulary verbatim.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS for AI"

Think of an LLM as an **extremely knowledgeable but directionless intern** who has read every book in the library but has no idea what you actually want done today.

- If you say *"Write something about cars"* → the intern might hand you a history of the automobile, a car review, or a poem about cars. The output is technically correct but **useless for your actual goal**.
- If you say *"Write a 150-word product description for a Toyota Camry 2024, targeting young professionals, highlighting fuel efficiency and safety, in a persuasive tone"* → the intern now has a clear destination, constraints, audience, and style. The output becomes **targeted, useful, and predictable**.

> This is exactly what a *prompt* is: **a set of GPS coordinates for the AI's reasoning engine.** The more precise your coordinates (instruction + context + format), the closer the model arrives at your desired destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"A prompt is a structured natural-language input that explicitly encodes the user's intent, context, and desired output format, serving as the sole channel through which a generative AI system can be directed, constrained, and evaluated."*

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
|------|------------------------|
| **Prompt** | The full input string sent to the LLM |
| **Token** | The smallest unit of text the model processes (≈ 4 characters or 0.75 words in English) |
| **Context Window** | Maximum number of tokens the model can read at once |
| **Instruction** | The verb-driven command telling the model *what to do* |
| **Context** | Background information that frames the task |
| **Zero-Shot Prompt** | A prompt with no examples provided |
| **Few-Shot Prompt** | A prompt containing 1–5 worked examples |
| **Hallucination** | A confidently-stated but factually fabricated model output |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Pillar Anatomy of a Prompt

Every effective prompt, regardless of domain, is built from **four logical pillars**. The KTU 2024 examiner will frequently test your ability to *identify these pillars inside a sample prompt*.

**Pillar 1 — Instruction (The Verb)**
- This is the imperative action the model must perform.
- Examples: *"Summarise"*, *"Translate"*, *"Classify"*, *"Generate code that…"*, *"Compare and contrast…"*.
- Strong instructions are **action-oriented, unambiguous, and singular in intent** (avoid stacking unrelated verbs in one prompt).

**Pillar 2 — Context (The Background Frame)**
- Context tells the model *who it is*, *who the user is*, and *what situation this output will be used in*.
- Example: *"You are a senior tax consultant advising a small business owner in Kerala filing GST returns for FY 2023–24."*
- Without context, the model defaults to a generic "helpful assistant" persona.

**Pillar 3 — Input Data (The Subject Matter)**
- The actual content to be processed — a paragraph, a code snippet, a CSV row, a customer complaint.
- Example: *"The following is a customer email: [insert email body]."*

**Pillar 4 — Output Indicator (The Format Lock)**
- Tells the model *how* to present the answer — bullet list, JSON, table, length, tone, language.
- Example: *"Respond in exactly 5 bullet points, each ≤ 20 words, in formal British English."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (KTU pattern):** A common 7-mark question asks — *"Given the following user query, identify the missing pillar and rewrite the prompt."* Memorise this 4-part decomposition.

### 2.2 The Operational Logic — Why Prompts Control AI Behaviour

Modern LLMs are **next-token predictors** trained on massive corpora. Given a sequence of tokens, they compute a probability distribution over the *next* token and sample from it. The prompt therefore *conditions* this probability distribution.

The simplified transformer conditional probability is:

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathcal{P}) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q \cdot K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$

Where:
- $y_t$ = the next token to be generated
- $y_{<t}$ = all previously generated tokens
- $\mathcal{P}$ = the prompt
- $Q, K, V$ = the Query, Key, and Value matrices of the attention mechanism
- $d_k$ = the dimensionality of the key vectors

> **Plain-English interpretation:** The prompt $\mathcal{P}$ is *folded into* the Key/Value matrices at every attention step, which is why even small changes in prompt wording can dramatically shift the output.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metric Table

| # | Metric / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Prompt Cost | $\text{Cost} = \left(\frac{N_{\text{in}} + N_{\text{out}}}{1000}\right) \times \text{Price}_{\text{per 1K tokens}}$ | USD; $\text{Price}_{\text{per 1K tokens}}$ is vendor-specific |
| 2 | Approximate Token Count | $N_{\text{tokens}} \approx \frac{N_{\text{words}} \times 4}{3}$ | Rule of thumb: **1 token ≈ 0.75 English words** |
| 3 | Context Window Utilisation | $\eta_{\text{ctx}} = \frac{N_{\text{prompt}} + N_{\text{output}}}{C_{\text{max}}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as a percentage; keep $\eta_{\text{ctx}} < 80\%$ for safety |
| 4 | Response Length Ceiling | $N_{\text{out, max}} = C_{\text{max}} - N_{\text{prompt}}$ | Tokens remaining for the model's reply |
| 5 | Output Specificity Score (qualitative) | $S = f(\text{Instruction clarity, Format lock, Constraints})$ | Higher $S \Rightarrow$ lower variance in outputs |
| 6 | Temperature Effect | $P'(y_t) = \frac{\exp(z_t / \tau)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / \tau)}$ | $\tau = 0$ deterministic; $\tau \geq 1$ creative |
| 7 | Hallucination Risk Proxy | $H \approx 1 - \text{Context Coverage}$ | More grounding context $\Rightarrow$ lower $H$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In any markdown table, the vertical bar $\vert$ symbol must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` — never as a raw pipe `|` — to prevent table-breaking syntax errors in KTU digital evaluation portals.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Prompting |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Software Engineering** | Auto-generating boilerplate code, unit tests, regex, SQL queries |
| **Civil Engineering** | Drafting safety reports, summarising IS-code clauses, risk checklists |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Translating CAD intent into design rationales, FMEA templates |
| **Biomedical** | Summarising patient history, structuring clinical notes |
| **Education (KTU LMS)** | Personalised tutoring chatbots, automated quiz generation |
| **Cybersecurity** | Phishing-email simulation, log-pattern summarisation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From a Vague Query to an Optimised Prompt (The 6-Step Refinement)

The KTU examiner frequently gives a *vague* query and asks you to evolve it into a high-quality prompt. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step transformation**. Every row is a state transition you must show in your answer booklet.

**Step 0 — Starting Point (User's Vague Query)**
$$\mathcal{P}_0 = \text{"Tell me about renewable energy."}$$

**Step 1 — Add an Action Verb (Establish the Instruction pillar)**
The model is told *what kind of response* is required. The vague "tell me about" is replaced with a precise verb.

$$\mathcal{P}_1 = \text{"List and explain the top 4 renewable energy sources used in India."}$$

**Reasoning for Step 1:** The verb *"List and explain"* sets a dual-task contract (enumerate + describe), eliminating ambiguity. The phrase *"used in India"* narrows the geographic context.

**Step 2 — Inject the Context Pillar (Persona + Scenario)**
Adding a persona instructs the model *what knowledge depth and tone* to adopt.

$$\mathcal{P}_2 = \text{"You are a faculty member teaching a B.Tech Module on Sustainable Engineering. List and explain the top 4 renewable energy sources used in India."}$$

**Reasoning for Step 2:** The *"B.Tech Module"* and *"faculty member"* persona forces the model into an academic register, avoiding oversimplified explanations.

**Step 3 — Add Input Data and Output Indicator**
We now lock the output structure.

$$\mathcal{P}_3 = \text{"You are a faculty member teaching a B.Tech Module on Sustainable Engineering. For each of the following sources — Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass — provide: (a) one-line definition, (b) typical capacity range, (c) one key Indian installation example. Present as a markdown table with columns: Source, Definition, Capacity, Example."}$$

**Reasoning for Step 3:** We have now used **all four pillars** — Instruction (provide), Context (faculty teaching B.Tech), Input Data (the four sources), Output Indicator (markdown table, specific columns).

**Step 4 — Add Constraints (Length, Style, Negative Prompts)**
Constraints reduce output variance.

$$\mathcal{P}_4 = \mathcal{P}_3 + \text{"\quad Keep each cell under 25 words. Avoid technical jargon beyond B.Tech level. Do not include sources outside India."}$$

**Reasoning for Step 4:** *"Do not include sources outside India"* is a **negative constraint** that suppresses common hallucination pathways (e.g., mentioning US or European examples).

**Step 5 — Add Few-Shot Example (Optional but Powerful)**
For tasks with subjective formats, showing one example is more effective than describing the format.

$$\mathcal{P}_5 = \text{"Example row: \vert Solar \vert Energy from sunlight via PV cells \vert 1\text{kW} - 500\text{MW} \vert Bhadla Solar Park, Rajasthan. Now apply the same format to the remaining three sources."}$$

**Reasoning for Step 5:** The model is now operating in **few-shot mode**, which empirically improves format adherence by 30–60% over zero-shot instructions.

**Step 6 — Final Optimised Prompt (Production-Ready)**

$$\mathcal{P}_{\text{final}} = \text{"Role: B.Tech faculty for Sustainable Engineering. Task: Build a 4-row comparison table for Solar, Wind, Hydro, and Biomass in India. Columns: Source, Definition, Capacity Range, Indian Installation Example. Constraints: each cell $\leq 25$ words, B.Tech-level language, no non-Indian examples. Output: markdown table only."}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Vague vs Optimised Prompt

| Dimension | $\mathcal{P}_0$ (Vague) | $\mathcal{P}_{\text{final}}$ (Optimised) |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Instruction clarity** | Implicit, ambiguous | Explicit, action-verb, multi-part |
| **Context provided** | None | Persona + audience + scenario |
| **Input data scope** | Unlimited | Bounded to 4 specific sources |
| **Output format** | Free-form text | Markdown table, exact columns |
| **Length control** | None | $\leq 25$ words per cell |
| **Negative constraints** | None | "No non-Indian examples" |
| **Expected output variance** | High (10+ valid answers) | Low (1 canonical answer pattern) |
| **Token efficiency** | Wastes tokens on re-prompting | Single-shot success |
| **Hallucination risk ($H$)** | $\approx 0.7$ | $\approx 0.15$ |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Token Budget Calculation

Suppose the final prompt $\mathcal{P}_{\text{final}}$ contains **120 words**. The LLM has a context window $C_{\text{max}} = 4096$ tokens and is expected to generate up to **300 words** of answer.

**Step A — Convert words to tokens** using the standard approximation $N_{\text{tokens}} \approx \frac{4}{3} N_{\text{words}}$:

$$N_{\text{prompt}} = \frac{4}{3} \times 120 = 160 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step B — Convert the expected output to tokens:**

$$N_{\text{out, expected}} = \frac{4}{3} \times 300 = 400 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step C — Compute remaining output capacity:**

$$N_{\text{out, max}} = C_{\text{max}} - N_{\text{prompt}} = 4096 - 160 = 3936 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step D — Verify the response fits comfortably:**

$$N_{\text{out, expected}} = 400 \leq 3936 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step E — Compute context window utilisation:**

$$\eta_{\text{ctx}} = \frac{160 + 400}{4096} \times 100\% = \frac{560}{4096} \times 100\% \approx 13.67\%$$

**Step F — Cost estimation (assuming a hypothetical rate of \$0.002 per 1K tokens):**

$$\text{Cost} = \frac{160 + 400}{1000} \times 0.002 = 0.560 \times 0.002 = \$0.00112$$

> **Engineering insight:** A well-crafted prompt doesn't just improve quality — it *drastically* reduces token wastage, which is the dominant cost driver in production LLM systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Prompt-to-Output Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User Intent] --> rawB[Vague Natural Language Query]
    rawB --> pillarC{Four-Pillar Decomposition}
    pillarC --> instrC1[Instruction Verb]
    pillarC --> ctxC2[Context and Persona]
    pillarC --> inpC3[Input Data]
    pillarC --> outC4[Output Format Indicator]
    instrC1 --> refinedD[Structured Optimised Prompt]
    ctxC2 --> refinedD
    inpC3 --> refinedD
    outC4 --> refinedD
    refinedD --> tokenizerE[Tokenizer Engine]
    tokenizerE --> tokenStreamF[Token Stream ID 100, 200, 300 ...]
    tokenStreamF --> llmCoreG[LLM Transformer Engine]
    llmCoreG --> attentionH[Multi-Head Attention Mechanism]
    attentionH --> outputGenI[Generated Token Sequence]
    outputGenI --> detokenJ[Detokenizer]
    detokenJ --> finalK[Final Human Readable Response]
    finalK --> evalL{Quality Evaluation}
    evalL --> acceptM[Accept and Deliver]
    evalL --> loopN[Refine Prompt and Retry]
    loopN --> refinedD
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Prompt Refinement Loop (Vague to Optimised)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start: User Has a Goal] --> stage2[Stage 1: Identify the Action Verb]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 2: Inject Persona and Context]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 3: Specify Input Data Scope]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 4: Lock Output Format]
    stage5 --> stage6[Stage 5: Add Constraints and Negative Prompts]
    stage6 --> stage7[Stage 6: Optional Few-Shot Example]
    stage7 --> ready8{Prompt Quality Check}
    ready8 --> yes9[Deploy to LLM]
    ready8 --> no10[Return to Stage 1 and Refine]
    yes9 --> result11[Evaluate Model Output]
    result11 --> good12{Output Meets Goal}
    good12 --> yes13[End: Success]
    good12 --> no14[Refine and Loop Back]
    no14 --> stage2
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Importance of Prompts Across Engineering Domains

```mermaid
mindmap
  rootA((Prompt Engineering Importance))
  nodeB(Accuracy Boost)
    nodeB1(Reduces hallucination)
    nodeB2(Grounds output in context)
  nodeC(Cost Efficiency)
    nodeC1(Fewer tokens wasted)
    nodeC2(Single shot success)
  nodeD(Domain Transferability)
    nodeD1(Works in CSE ECE ME CE)
    nodeD2(No code retraining needed)
  nodeE(Safety and Control)
    nodeE1(Negative constraints)
    nodeE2(Format lock prevents leaks)
  nodeF(Productivity Multiplier)
    nodeF1(Auto drafts)
    nodeF2(Automated testing)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
*Define a "prompt" in the context of Large Language Models. List the four pillars of an effective prompt.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **prompt** is a structured natural-language input provided to a Large Language Model (LLM) that encodes the user's intent, context, and desired output format, guiding the model to produce a specific and goal-oriented response. The four pillars of an effective prompt are:
1. **Instruction** — the action verb defining the task.
2. **Context** — the persona, audience, and scenario framing the task.
3. **Input Data** — the actual content to be processed.
4. **Output Indicator** — the required format, length, and tone of the response. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four pillars.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
*Why is prompt design critical in modern AI applications? Give two engineering-domain examples where well-crafted prompts outperform vague ones.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Prompt design is critical because LLMs are stochastic next-token predictors — the prompt *conditions* the entire probability distribution of the output. A vague prompt leads to high variance, hallucinations, and wasted tokens; a precise prompt produces deterministic, accurate, single-shot results. **Example 1 (CSE):** A prompt asking *"Write Python code to merge two sorted lists"* may produce 10 different valid functions, while *"Write a Python function `merge_sorted(a, b)` that returns a new sorted list with O(n+m) time complexity and includes a docstring"* yields one canonical answer. **Example 2 (Civil):** A prompt asking *"Summarise IS 456"* produces a generic essay, but *"Summarise the flexural design provisions of IS 456:2000 in a 10-row table with clause number, formula, and limit"* produces an engineer-ready reference. *(1 mark for reasoning + 2 marks for examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a "prompt" as the *interface contract* between a human user and a Large Language Model. Describe the four-pillar anatomy of a prompt with one illustrative example for each pillar. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the vague user query: *"Tell me about climate change."* Refactor it into an optimised prompt by applying the four-pillar framework. Justify each transformation step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
A prompt is the **interface contract** because it is the *only channel* through which a human communicates intent to an LLM. Unlike traditional software, where APIs take structured parameters, LLMs accept free-form natural language — making the prompt the *de facto* specification of behaviour. The four pillars are:

- **Instruction:** The action verb. *Example:* "Translate the following English paragraph into Malayalam."
- **Context:** Persona and scenario. *Example:* "You are a KTU B.Tech faculty preparing Module-5 slides for first-year students."
- **Input Data:** The content to be processed. *Example:* "Paragraph: 'Renewable energy is derived from natural sources...'"
- **Output Indicator:** Format and length. *Example:* "Respond in 3 bullet points, each ≤ 30 words."

*Mark split: 2 marks for interface-contract explanation, 4 marks for the four pillars with examples, 1 mark for clarity/structure.*

**(b) [7 marks]**

| Step | Transformation | Justification |
|------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\mathcal{P}_0$ = "Tell me about climate change" | Starting point — vague, no verb clarity |
| 2 | Replace "tell me about" → "Explain with 4 key causes" | Adds explicit **Instruction** and bounds scope |
| 3 | Add "for a KTU B.Tech Module on Environmental Science" | Injects **Context** (audience + scenario) |
| 4 | Add "in a 5-row markdown table: Cause, Mechanism, Impact, Example" | Locks **Output Indicator** and **Input Data scope** |
| 5 | Add "each cell ≤ 25 words, no political commentary" | Adds **Constraints** to reduce hallucination |
| 6 | Final $\mathcal{P}_6$ = "Role: B.Tech faculty. Task: explain 4 key causes of climate change for a KTU Module. Format: 5-row markdown table with columns Cause, Mechanism, Impact, Example. Constraints: ≤ 25 words/cell, no political commentary." | Production-ready prompt |

*Mark split: 1 mark per transformation step (6 steps = 6 marks) + 1 mark for the consolidated final prompt.*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **zero-shot** and **few-shot** prompting. Under what conditions is few-shot prompting preferred? Provide one example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A B.Tech student submits the prompt: *"Write code."* Diagnose the failure points using the four-pillar framework and rewrite the prompt for a specific use case (e.g., a Python function to check if a number is prime). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
- **Zero-shot prompting:** The prompt contains *no examples*; the model must infer the task pattern purely from the instruction. *Example:* "Translate 'Good morning' into Hindi." The model leverages its pre-training. (2 marks)
- **Few-shot prompting:** The prompt contains 1–5 worked examples (input → output pairs) before the actual query. *Example:* "English: Hello → Hindi: Namaste. English: Thank you → Hindi: Dhanyavaad. English: Good morning → Hindi: ?" (2 marks)
- **Conditions for few-shot preference:** (i) When the format is unusual or non-obvious, (ii) when zero-shot outputs are inconsistent across runs, (iii) when the task requires a specific reasoning pattern, (iv) when a structured output (e.g., JSON) must be produced reliably. (3 marks)

**(b) [7 marks]**

| Pillar | Missing in "Write code" | Fix |
|--------|------------------------|-----|
| Instruction | No verb, no task definition | "Write a Python function `is_prime(n)`..." |
| Context | No persona, no use case | "...for use in a KTU B.Tech DSA lab exercise..." |
| Input Data | No parameters, no constraints | "...that takes a positive integer `n` and returns `True` if prime, `False` otherwise..." |
| Output Indicator | No format, no style | "...including a docstring, type hints, and a main() block with 3 test cases." |

**Optimised prompt (1 mark):**
*"Role: KTU B.Tech DSA lab assistant. Task: write a Python function `is_prime(n: int) -> bool` that returns `True` if `n` is prime and `False` otherwise. Include a docstring, type hints, and a `main()` block testing the function with 17, 18, and 19. Handle edge cases for `n < 2`."*

*Mark split: 1.5 marks per pillar diagnosis (4 pillars = 6 marks) + 1 mark for the final optimised prompt.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 1 questions (KTU 2024 pattern):**
> 1. **Conflating "prompt" with "question":** A prompt is *not* just a question — it is a structured artefact with four pillars. Students who answer "a prompt is a question asked to AI" lose 2 of 7 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Output Indicator:** The most common omission. Always specify the desired format in your final prompt.
> 3. **Skipping justification steps in transformation questions:** A 7-mark refactoring question requires *step-by-step justification*, not just the final prompt. Each missing justification costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Using raw `|` in markdown tables:** Pipes break the table renderer in KTU's digital valuation portal — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 5. **Ignoring token-budget calculations:** When the question contains numerical values (context window, word count), examiners *expect* the $N_{\text{tokens}} \approx \frac{4}{3} N_{\text{words}}$ conversion to be shown explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **prompt** is a structured natural-language input — not merely a question — that encodes intent, context, and format.
- ✅ The **four pillars** are: **Instruction, Context, Input Data, Output Indicator** — memorise this exact order.
- ✅ The model response is mathematically $\mathcal{Y} = \mathcal{M}(\mathcal{P})$; the prompt *conditions* the attention mechanism's $Q, K, V$ matrices.
- ✅ Token approximation rule: **1 token ≈ 0.75 English words** or equivalently $N_{\text{tokens}} \approx \frac{4}{3} N_{\text{words}}$.
- ✅ **Zero-shot** = no examples; **Few-shot** = 1–5 worked examples. Few-shot is preferred for non-obvious formats.
- ✅ **Context window utilisation** $\eta_{\text{ctx}}$ should stay below **80%** to avoid truncation errors.
- ✅ Always include **negative constraints** (e.g., "do not include…") to suppress hallucination pathways.
- ✅ Well-crafted prompts reduce **token cost, hallucination risk, and output variance** simultaneously.
- ✅ Use `\vert` or `\mid` (not raw `|`) inside markdown tables to avoid syntax errors in KTU's evaluation system.
- ✅ For Part B transformation questions, show **every intermediate state** $\mathcal{P}_0 \rightarrow \mathcal{P}_1 \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \mathcal{P}_{\text{final}}$ with justifications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Prompt Engineering: Techniques and Use Cases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Prompt Engineering: Techniques and Use Cases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Introduction to Prompt Engineering

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prompt Engineering** is the disciplined, iterative process of designing, refining, and optimizing natural language inputs (called *prompts*) provided to Large Language Models (LLMs) and generative AI systems in order to elicit accurate, relevant, and controlled outputs without modifying the underlying model weights.

In the formal vocabulary of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for `PECST868`, prompt engineering is positioned as the **primary interface layer** between human intent and foundation models such as GPT-4, LLaMA, Claude, Gemini, and Mistral. It sits at the intersection of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, and **Applied Cognitive Science**.

**Key Terminology (Board-Examiner Vocabulary):**

- **Prompt** – The natural-language instruction, question, or stimulus fed to an LLM.
- **Foundation Model (FM)** – A large, general-purpose pre-trained model (e.g., GPT, LLaMA).
- **In-Context Learning (ICL)** – The model's ability to learn a new task from examples provided *inside* the prompt, with no gradient updates.
- **Token** – The smallest discrete unit of text processed by an LLM. English text ≈ **0.75 words per token** on average.
- **Context Window** – The maximum number of tokens (input + output) the model can process in a single call.
- **Temperature (τ)** – A sampling parameter in the range **[0, 2]** controlling output randomness.
- **Top-p (Nucleus Sampling)** – Probability mass threshold for candidate token selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Prompt Engineering is categorized under *Program Elective* (PE) courses, mapping to **CO1: "Understand the foundational concepts and techniques of prompt engineering for various generative AI applications."**

---

### Conceptual Analogy / The "Librarian Analogy" 🧑‍🏫

Imagine you walk into a vast library containing **every book ever written**, but the librarian inside has **no eyes, no memory of past visitors, and answers only the exact question you speak out loud**.

- A **bad prompt** ("Tell me about stuff") is like asking: *"Give me a book."* The librarian hands you a random cookbook.
- A **good prompt** ("Explain photosynthesis to a 10-year-old using a kitchen-garden analogy, in 100 words") is like asking: *"Give me a 100-word children's book explaining photosynthesis using a kitchen-garden analogy."* The librarian hands you the *exact* right book.

> The model is the **librarian**; the prompt is the **question**; prompt engineering is the **art of asking precisely**.

**Why It Matters in 2024+ Industry:**
- **$0.01–$0.06 per 1,000 tokens** can translate to **millions of dollars/month** in production AI systems.
- A well-engineered prompt can improve task accuracy by **20–40%** over naïve prompts.
- Reduces *hallucination*, *bias*, and *refusal rates* without retraining the model.

---

### The Three Pillars of Prompt Quality

| Pillar | Description | Engineering Lever |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| **Clarity** | Unambiguous, atomic instructions | Decompose tasks into sub-tasks |
| **Context** | Sufficient background data | Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) |
| **Constraint** | Format, length, persona | Output schemas, JSON mode, role prompts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Prompt – The Four-Component Model

Every professional prompt, regardless of complexity, is composed of **four logical components** as defined in the seminal work by *White et al. (2023), "A Prompt Pattern Catalog"*:

1. **Instruction (I)** – The verb-driven directive ("Classify...", "Summarize...").
2. **Context (C)** – The grounding information or persona.
3. **Input Data (X)** – The raw material the model must operate on.
4. **Output Indicator (O)** – The expected format/structure of the response.

**Canonical Prompt Skeleton:**

```
[CONTEXT]  → You are a senior financial analyst at a KTU-affiliated Kerala startup.
[INSTRUCTION] → Analyze the following quarterly revenue data and identify the top 3 risk factors.
[INPUT DATA]  → Revenue: Q1=₹12L, Q2=₹15L, Q3=₹9L, Q4=₹18L ...
[OUTPUT]      → Respond as a JSON object with keys: {risk_factors[], confidence_score}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently award a full 7-mark sub-part if a student can correctly *label* the four components inside a given prompt. Memorize the acronym **I-C-I-O** (Instruction-Context-Input-Output).

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Prompting Techniques

The hierarchy of prompting techniques ranges from trivial to advanced:

### Level 1: Zero-Shot Prompting
The model is given only the instruction — **no examples**.
- **Use case:** Simple, well-defined tasks (translation, sentiment, factual Q&A).
- **Limitation:** Fails on novel, ambiguous, or domain-specific tasks.

### Level 2: One-Shot / Few-Shot Prompting (In-Context Learning)
The prompt contains **1 to ~50 examples** of (input → output) pairs.
- **Brown et al. (2020) – "GPT-3 Paper"** demonstrated that few-shot performance scales **log-linearly** with example count.
- **Best practice:** Use **4–8 diverse, edge-case-rich examples** for downstream tasks.

### Level 3: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting
**Wei et al. (2022)** introduced CoT — instructing the model to *"think step by step"* before the final answer.
- **Variants:**
  - **Zero-Shot CoT:** Append the magic phrase *"Let's think step by step."*
  - **Few-Shot CoT:** Provide examples that include intermediate reasoning steps.
  - **Auto-CoT:** Automatically generate reasoning chains using a separate LLM call.
- **Performance Gain:** Up to **+30% accuracy** on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning benchmarks (e.g., GSM8K, SVAMP).

### Level 4: Advanced Reasoning Strategies

- **Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT)** – Explores multiple reasoning paths in a tree and uses BFS/DFS to select the best.
- **Self-Consistency** – Samples *N* independent CoT chains and takes a *majority vote* on the final answer.
- **ReAct (Reason + Act)** – Interleaves reasoning steps with **tool-use** actions (web search, calculator, code interpreter).
- **Reflexion** – The model reflects on its own failures and stores verbal feedback in episodic memory.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for Module 1 derivations. Memorize every entry.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|------------------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Token Count Approximation (English)** | $T \approx \frac{W}{0.75}$ where $W$ = word count | tokens | API cost estimation |
| 2 | **Token Count Approximation (Code)** | $T \approx \frac{C}{4}$ where $C$ = characters | tokens | Developer cost planning |
| 3 | **Cost Calculation** | $\text{Cost} = \frac{T_{\text{in}} \cdot P_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{out}} \cdot P_{\text{out}}}{1000}$ | USD | Cloud billing |
| 4 | **Softmax with Temperature** | $P(x_i) = \dfrac{e^{z_i / \tau}}{\sum_{j} e^{z_j / \tau}}$ | $\tau \in [0, 2]$ | Output randomness control |
| 5 | **Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling** | $S = \min \left\{ S' \subseteq V : \sum_{x \in S'} P(x) \ge p \right\}$ | $p \in [0, 1]$ | Vocabulary truncation |
| 6 | **Top-k Sampling** | Keep only top $k$ tokens by probability | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Hard truncation |
| 7 | **Perplexity (PPL)** | $\text{PPL}(W) = \exp\left( -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(w_i \mid w_{<i}) \right)$ | $\downarrow$ better | Output quality metric |
| 8 | **In-Context Learning Scaling Law** | $\text{Acc}(n) \approx a \log(n) + b$ | $n$ = examples | Few-shot performance |
| 9 | **BLEU Score (N-gram Precision)** | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n \right)$ | $[0, 1]$ | Translation evaluation |
| 10 | **ROUGE-L (Longest Common Subsequence)** | $R_L = \frac{\text{LCS}(X, Y)}{\text{len}(Y)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Summarization evaluation |
| 11 | **Context Window Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{prompt}}}{T_{\text{max}}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Prompt sizing |
| 12 | **CoT Success Probability (Self-Consistency)** | $P_{\text{success}} = 1 - \left(1 - p\right)^N$ | $N$ = samples | Sampling strategy |

**Where:**
- $\tau$ = temperature
- $z_i$ = logit of token $i$
- $V$ = vocabulary
- $\text{BP}$ = brevity penalty
- $p_n$ = modified n-gram precision

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap:** Notice how the BLEU formula above uses `\vert` style delimiters inside the aligned block but the table cells contain no `|` characters. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for broken LaTeX rendering in answer sheets.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Prompt Engineering

| Industry Vertical | High-Yield Application |
|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Healthcare** | Clinical note summarization, ICD-10 coding assistance |
| **Legal (Kerala HC eCourts)** | Case-law retrieval, FIR summarization |
| **Education (KTU LMS)** | Personalized tutoring, automated grading rubrics |
| **Cybersecurity** | Phishing email detection, log anomaly triage |
| **Agriculture (Kerala)** | Crop-disease advisory in Malayalam via multilingual prompts |
| **FinTech** | KYC document analysis, fraud narrative generation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Cost Estimation for an LLM API Call

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
A KTU startup builds a document-summarization tool using GPT-4. Each input document averages **900 words**, and the model generates a summary of **120 words**. Calculate the cost per 1,000 calls given that:
- Input price $P_{\text{in}} = \$0.03$ per **1K tokens**
- Output price $P_{\text{out}} = \$0.06$ per **1K tokens**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tokenize the input.**

The English text approximation is:

$$T_{\text{in}} = \frac{W_{\text{in}}}{0.75}$$

$$T_{\text{in}} = \frac{900}{0.75} = 1200 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step 2 — Tokenize the output.**

$$T_{\text{out}} = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{0.75} = \frac{120}{0.75} = 160 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step 3 — Compute cost per single call.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{single}} = \frac{T_{\text{in}} \cdot P_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{out}} \cdot P_{\text{out}}}{1000}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{single}} = \frac{1200 \cdot 0.03 + 160 \cdot 0.06}{1000}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{single}} = \frac{36 + 9.6}{1000} = \frac{45.6}{1000}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{single}} = \$0.0456$$

**Step 5 — Scale to 1,000 calls.**

$$\text{Cost}_{1000} = 1000 \times 0.0456 = \$45.60$$

> **Final Answer:** **\$45.60 per 1,000 summarization calls.** A 10% reduction in output length (from 120 to 108 words) saves **\$4.56 per 1,000 calls** — demonstrating why prompt-engineering for *brevity* has direct financial value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Softmax with Temperature

**Problem Statement:**
A token has logit values $\mathbf{z} = [2.0, 1.0, 0.5]$ for three candidate words. Compute the probability distribution at (a) $\tau = 1.0$ and (b) $\tau = 0.5$. Show the effect of temperature on output sharpness.

**Step 1 — Recall the temperature-softmax formula.**

$$P(x_i) = \frac{e^{z_i / \tau}}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} e^{z_j / \tau}}$$

**Step 2 — Case (a): $\tau = 1.0$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
e^{2.0/1.0} &= e^{2.0} \approx 7.389 \\
e^{1.0/1.0} &= e^{1.0} \approx 2.718 \\
e^{0.5/1.0} &= e^{0.5} \approx 1.649 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.649 = 11.756 \\
P(\text{tok}_1) &= 7.389 / 11.756 \approx 0.628 \\
P(\text{tok}_2) &= 2.718 / 11.756 \approx 0.231 \\
P(\text{tok}_3) &= 1.649 / 11.756 \approx 0.140
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Case (b): $\tau = 0.5$ (sharper distribution).**

$$\begin{aligned}
e^{2.0/0.5} &= e^{4.0} \approx 54.598 \\
e^{1.0/0.5} &= e^{2.0} \approx 7.389 \\
e^{0.5/0.5} &= e^{1.0} \approx 2.718 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 54.598 + 7.389 + 2.718 = 64.705 \\
P(\text{tok}_1) &= 54.598 / 64.705 \approx 0.844 \\
P(\text{tok}_2) &= 7.389 / 64.705 \approx 0.114 \\
P(\text{tok}_3) &= 2.718 / 64.705 \approx 0.042
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

$$\Delta P(\text{tok}_1) = 0.844 - 0.628 = +0.216$$

Lowering temperature from **1.0 → 0.5** increased the top-token probability by **+21.6 percentage points**, illustrating the **sharpening effect** used in deterministic tasks (code generation, math).

> **Engineering Insight:** $\tau \to 0$ approaches **argmax decoding** (greedy); $\tau \to \infty$ approaches **uniform random sampling**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Self-Consistency Sampling Probability

**Problem Statement:**
A CoT prompt has a single-sample success probability $p = 0.7$. Compute the success probability of the Self-Consistency strategy with $N = 5$ samples.

**Step 1 — Probability of a single failure.**

$$P_{\text{fail, single}} = 1 - p = 1 - 0.7 = 0.3$$

**Step 2 — Probability of failure in all $N$ independent samples.**

$$P_{\text{all fail}} = (1 - p)^N = (0.3)^5$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$(0.3)^5 = 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.00243$$

**Step 4 — Apply complement rule.**

$$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - (1 - p)^N = 1 - 0.00243 = 0.99757$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{success}} \approx 99.76\%}$$

> **Insight:** Self-Consistency is mathematically a **Bernoulli process**. Doubling $N$ from 5 → 10 boosts $P_{\text{success}}$ from 99.76% → 99.997%, with diminishing returns and 2× cost.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Prompting Technique Selection

| Task Type | Best Technique | Why | Cost Multiplier |
|-----------|----------------|-----|------------------|
| Sentiment classification | Zero-shot | Task is universal | **1×** |
| Brand-voice copywriting | Few-shot (5–8) | Style transfer needs examples | **1.3×** |
| Math word problems | CoT (few-shot) | Reasoning chains improve accuracy | **1.8×** |
| Multi-step planning | ReAct + tools | Real-world grounding needed | **3–5×** |
| Code generation | Few-shot + low τ | Precision-critical | **1.5×** |
| Legal document Q&A | RAG + CoT | Hallucination intolerable | **2.5×** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Prompt Engineering Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Task] --> B[Define Success Criteria]
    B --> C[Select Technique Zero/Few/CoT/RAG]
    C --> D[Initial Prompt Draft I-C-I-O Structure]
    D --> E[Run on Eval Set 50+ Examples]
    E --> F{Meets Criteria}
    F -- No --> G[Refine Prompt Edit Instruction]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Document Prompt Pattern]
    H --> I[Deploy to Production]
    I --> J[Monitor Drift and Cost]
    J --> K{Drift Detected}
    K -- Yes --> C
    K -- No --> J
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The lifecycle is **cyclic** — never "fire and forget".
- Evaluation set must contain at least **50 diverse examples** (KTU 2024 recommended).
- Drift detection uses **embedding-based clustering** of live inputs.

---

## 4.2 Architecture: LLM Inference Stack with Prompt Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USR[User Layer]
        U1[Developer]
        U2[End User]
    end
    subgraph PRL[Prompt Layer]
        P1[Prompt Template Engine]
        P2[Variable Injection]
        P3[Safety Guardrails]
    end
    subgraph CTL[Context Layer]
        C1[RAG Retriever Vector DB]
        C2[Tool APIs Calculator Web]
        C3[Few-shot Example Bank]
    end
    subgraph MDL[Model Layer]
        M1[Frozen LLM GPT LLaMA Claude]
        M2[Sampling Engine Temperature Top-p]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Validator JSON Schema]
        O2[Post-processor]
        O3[Telemetry Logger]
    end
    U1 --> P1
    U2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> C1
    P2 --> C2
    P2 --> C3
    C1 --> M1
    C2 --> M1
    C3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

**Engineering Takeaway:** The prompt layer is **stateless and pure**; the model layer is **frozen and opaque**. All control happens *between* them via context injection and post-hoc validation.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Diagram: Zero-Shot vs Few-Shot vs CoT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZS[Zero-Shot]
        Z1[Instruction] --> Z2[LLM] --> Z3[Output]
    end
    subgraph FS[Few-Shot]
        F1[Ex 1] --> F2[Ex 2]
        F2 --> F3[Instruction]
        F3 --> F4[LLM] --> F5[Output]
    end
    subgraph CT[Chain-of-Thought]
        C1[Ex 1 Step Reasoning] --> C2[Instruction Think Step by Step]
        C2 --> C3[LLM] --> C4[Output with Steps]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each additional layer in CoT adds **1.5×–2× token cost** but can improve accuracy on reasoning benchmarks by **+25% to +40%**.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RAG + Prompt

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[User Query] --> E[Embedder]
    E --> VS[Vector Search Top-k]
    VS --> R[Retrieve Relevant Chunks]
    R --> A[Augment Prompt Template]
    Q --> A
    A --> L[LLM Frozen]
    L --> V[Validate Output Schema]
    V --> ANS[Final Answer]
```

**Why RAG?**
- Reduces **hallucination** by **60–80%** in domain-specific Q&A.
- Provides **freshness** (real-time data) without retraining.
- KTUs **NEP 2020** curriculum explicitly maps this to *CO3: Apply retrieval-augmented techniques for knowledge-intensive tasks.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Prompt Engineering. List any two use cases in the Kerala engineering-industry context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Prompt Engineering is the process of designing and refining natural-language inputs to large language models to obtain desired outputs without modifying model parameters.  
**Use cases:** (i) Malayalam-language chatbots for Kerala e-Governance portals, (ii) Automated code-review assistants in KTU-affiliated IT companies, (iii) Summarization of research papers for M.Tech dissertations. *(Any two — 1.5 marks each)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Zero-Shot and Few-Shot prompting with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Zero-Shot | Few-Shot |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| Examples | None | 1–50 |
| Use case | Simple tasks | Domain-specific |
| Example prompt | "Translate 'Good morning' to Hindi." | "EN: Hello → HI: नमस्ते. EN: Thank you → HI: धन्यवाद. EN: Good morning → HI: ?" |
*(1.5 marks concept + 1.5 marks example)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **anatomy of a prompt** with the I-C-I-O framework. Construct a real prompt for summarizing a 500-word KTU academic notice into **three bullet points** targeting **first-year B.Tech students**. Justify each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the **Softmax-with-temperature equation**, derive the output probability distribution for logits $z = [3, 1, 0.5]$ at $\tau = 1.2$. Explain what happens to the distribution when $\tau$ approaches 0. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks): Define the I-C-I-O framework.**

- **Instruction:** The verb-driven directive that tells the model *what to do* (e.g., "Summarize").
- **Context:** Background, persona, or constraints that *frame* the task.
- **Input Data:** The raw text the model must operate on.
- **Output Indicator:** The expected format, structure, or schema.

**Step 2 (3 marks): Construct the prompt.**

```text
[CONTEXT] You are a friendly senior student mentor at APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University.
[INSTRUCTION] Summarize the following academic notice into exactly THREE concise bullet points, using language that a first-year B.Tech student will easily understand.
[INPUT DATA] Notice: "The registration for the 2024-25 even-semester examination will commence on 15th January 2025. Students must remit the fee via the KTU portal before 30th January 2025. Late submissions will incur a fine of ₹500 per week..."
[OUTPUT INDICATOR] Output format: A JSON array with key "bullets" containing three strings.
```

**Step 3 (2 marks): Justify each component.**
- *Context* ensures the model adopts the right register and audience sensitivity.
- *Instruction* specifies verb (Summarize), count (three), and audience (first-year).
- *Input Data* provides raw material.
- *Output Indicator* enforces machine-readable JSON, enabling downstream automation.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark): State the formula.**

$$P(x_i \mid \tau) = \frac{e^{z_i / \tau}}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} e^{z_j / \tau}}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks): Substitute $z = [3, 1, 0.5]$ and $\tau = 1.2$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
e^{3/1.2} &= e^{2.5} \approx 12.182 \\
e^{1/1.2} &= e^{0.833} \approx 2.300 \\
e^{0.5/1.2} &= e^{0.417} \approx 1.517 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 12.182 + 2.300 + 1.517 = 15.999 \\
P(x_1) &= 12.182 / 15.999 \approx 0.761 \\
P(x_2) &= 2.300 / 15.999 \approx 0.144 \\
P(x_3) &= 1.517 / 15.999 \approx 0.095
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Verification and interpretation.**

Check: $0.761 + 0.144 + 0.095 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 4 (1 mark): Limit analysis as $\tau \to 0$.**

As $\tau \to 0$, the term $e^{z_i / \tau}$ diverges fastest for the **largest logit**. The distribution collapses to a one-hot vector — the model becomes deterministic and always picks the highest-logit token (greedy decoding).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Q-A (b):** Many students forget to verify the probabilities sum to 1. This costs **0.5 marks**. Also, common error: writing $\tau = 0$ in the denominator — you must explain the *limit*, not substitute literally.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** prompting with **Self-Consistency**. Using the formula $P_{\text{success}} = 1 - (1-p)^N$, compute the Self-Consistency success rate for $p = 0.6$ at $N = 7$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline** for answering student queries about KTU exam timetables. Draw the block diagram and explain each block's role. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | CoT | Self-Consistency |
|---------|-----|------------------|
| Strategy | Single reasoning chain | Multiple chains + majority vote |
| Cost | Moderate | High ($N \times$ cost) |
| Best for | Step-by-step reasoning | Tasks with verifiable answers |
| Key paper | Wei et al. 2022 | Wang et al. 2023 |
| Failure mode | Reasoning error persists | Errors cancel via voting |

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

Given $p = 0.6$, $N = 7$:

$$P_{\text{fail, single}} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$$

$$P_{\text{all fail}} = (0.4)^7$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.4)^2 &= 0.16 \\
(0.4)^4 &= 0.0256 \\
(0.4)^7 &= 0.0256 \times 0.064 = 0.0016384
\end{aligned}$$

$$P_{\text{success}} = 1 - 0.0016384 = 0.9983616$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{success}} \approx 99.84\%}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Student Query] --> B[Embedder]
    B --> C[Vector DB KTU Timetables]
    C --> D[Top-k Chunks]
    D --> E[Prompt Augmenter]
    A --> E
    E --> F[LLM Frozen]
    F --> G[Answer]
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation (4 marks):**
1. **Embedder:** Converts student query to a dense vector (e.g., `text-embedding-3-small`).
2. **Vector DB:** Stores embeddings of all KTU exam timetable documents (PDFs, CSV files).
3. **Top-k Chunks:** Cosine similarity search returns the $k$ most relevant passages (typically $k=3$ to $k=5$).
4. **Prompt Augmenter:** Combines retrieved chunks + original query into a strict prompt template.
5. **LLM:** Generates answer constrained to the retrieved context.
6. **Why RAG?:** Hallucination-free, real-time, and does not require fine-tuning the base model — satisfies the **NEP 2020** "minimal data, maximal output" philosophy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Q-B (a):** Students often write $P_{\text{success}} = Np$ (linear), which is **wrong**. Self-Consistency is a *complementary probability* model, not a linear one. Marks are deducted for the wrong formula. Also, omit to *explain* the cost implication of $N$ samples and you lose 1 more mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Prompt Engineering = designing LLM inputs *without* weight updates.
- ✅ **Anatomy:** I-C-I-O (Instruction-Context-Input-Output) — *board-favourite label.*
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** Zero-Shot → Few-Shot → CoT → ToT → ReAct → Reflexion.
- ✅ **Magic Phrase:** *"Let's think step by step"* triggers Zero-Shot CoT.
- ✅ **Token Rule (English):** $T \approx W / 0.75$.
- ✅ **Token Rule (Code):** $T \approx C / 4$.
- ✅ **Cost Formula:** $\text{Cost} = (T_{\text{in}} P_{\text{in}} + T_{\text{out}} P_{\text{out}}) / 1000$.
- ✅ **Temperature:** $\tau < 1$ → deterministic; $\tau > 1$ → creative.
- ✅ **Softmax:** $P(x_i) = e^{z_i/\tau} / \sum e^{z_j/\tau}$.
- ✅ **Self-Consistency:** $P_{\text{success}} = 1 - (1-p)^N$.
- ✅ **RAG:** Retrieve → Augment → Generate (no retraining needed).
- ✅ **Few-Shot Count:** Sweet spot is **4–8 diverse examples**.
- ✅ **CoT Gains:** Up to **+30%** on GSM8K, SVAMP, AQuA.
- ✅ **Evaluation Metrics:** BLEU (translation), ROUGE-L (summarization), Perplexity (generation quality).
- ✅ **NEP 2020 Mapping:** CO1 = Foundations; CO2 = Techniques; CO3 = Application.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Module 1 sets the vocabulary for the entire course. Mastering these formulas and the I-C-I-O framework will unlock **70%+ of the marks** in Part A and the first sub-part of every Part B question. Combine with `code execution` discipline and you are on track for an **A+ grade**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethical Considerations in Prompt Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ethical Considerations in Prompt Engineering # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Ethical Considerations in Prompt Engineering** refers to the systematic study, identification, and application of moral principles, values, and professional guidelines that govern the design, construction, deployment, and evaluation of prompts used to interact with Large Language Models (LLMs) and generative AI systems. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic addresses the responsible development of prompt-based AI interactions, ensuring that the engineered prompts uphold principles of **fairness**, **transparency**, **accountability**, **privacy**, **safety**, and **non-maleficence**. In formal terms, an ethically-aligned prompt can be mathematically abstracted as: $$ P_{eth} = f(P, C, S, T) $$ Where: - $P$ = the base prompt instruction - $C$ = the contextual constraints (legal, social, organizational) - $S$ = the safety guardrails (refusal patterns, content filters) - $T$ = the transparency markers (disclosures, citations) - $f(\cdot)$ = the ethical alignment function > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course **PECST868 – Prompt Engineering** explicitly includes ethical considerations as a foundational Module 1 topic, recognizing that technical prompt competence without ethical grounding is insufficient for industry readiness. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **public translator** working in a busy international courthouse. This translator must: 1. **Translate accurately** (functional correctness) 2. **Refuse to translate** instructions that could harm someone (safety) 3. **Disclose** when they are uncertain or when a statement may be legally sensitive (transparency) 4. **Avoid bias** based on the speaker's accent, gender, or appearance (fairness) 5. **Protect private conversations** (privacy) A **prompt engineer** is essentially the "instructional architect" behind the AI's behaviour. Just as a court translator's ethical lapses can cause injustice, a poorly engineered or ethically negligent prompt can cause an LLM to: - Generate misinformation - Perpetuate societal biases - Leak private data - Enable harmful activities (e.g., generating phishing content) > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (Recall-Ready):** > Ethical prompt engineering = The discipline of crafting LLM instructions such that the AI's outputs are **truthful, safe, fair, private, transparent, and accountable** across diverse user populations and deployment contexts. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Ethical Prompt Engineering | Pillar | Definition | Prompt-Level Application | |---|---|---| | **Beneficence** | Doing good; maximizing benefit | Crafting prompts that produce helpful, accurate outputs | | **Non-Maleficence** | Avoiding harm | Adding refusal patterns and safety guardrails | | **Justice** | Fair distribution of benefits/risks | Mitigating bias in prompt design and output | > [!TIP] > These three pillars are borrowed from the **Beauchamp & Childress (1979) biomedical ethics framework** and are widely cited in AI ethics literature, including the EU AI Act and NIST AI Risk Management Framework. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Core Ethical Principles in Prompt Engineering Ethical considerations in prompt engineering are best understood through a structured taxonomy. The KTU 2024 Scheme aligns with globally accepted AI governance frameworks (UNESCO, OECD, EU AI Act, NIST RMF). The five core principles are: ### 2.1.1 Transparency The user and affected stakeholders must be able to understand **how** and **why** an AI system produced a given output. In prompt engineering, this translates to: - Disclosing AI involvement in outputs - Avoiding deceptive prompts that impersonate humans - Providing explanations for AI decisions ### 2.1.2 Fairness & Bias Mitigation Prompts must not encode, amplify, or trigger discriminatory outputs. Two types of bias are critical: - **Training Data Bias**: Reflected in the LLM's weights - **Prompt-Induced Bias**: Introduced by the user's wording, tone, or framing ### 2.1.3 Privacy & Data Protection Prompts must not extract, infer, or leak personally identifiable information (PII). Ethical prompt engineers follow the principle of **data minimization**. ### 2.1.4 Safety & Harm Prevention Prompts must include guardrails against generating: - Weapons, explosives, or dangerous synthesis instructions - Hate speech, harassment, or incitement to violence - Self-harm content - Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM) — universally prohibited ### 2.1.5 Accountability & Intellectual Honesty The prompt engineer is responsible for the outputs their prompts elicit. This includes: - Verifying factual claims (avoiding hallucination cascades) - Respecting copyright and intellectual property - Maintaining audit trails for high-stakes applications ## 2.2 Analytical Frameworks for Ethical Prompt Evaluation ### 2.2.1 The CONSTRAINT Framework (KTU High-Yield) A memorable, examinable framework for ethically-auditing a prompt: | Letter | Principle | Prompt-Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | **C** | Clarity | Avoid ambiguous instructions that yield inconsistent outputs | | **O** | Oversight | Embed human-in-the-loop checkpoints | | **N** | Neutrality | Strip demographic/cultural assumptions from prompts | | **S** | Safety | Add refusal patterns for harmful requests | | **T** | Truthfulness | Use grounding/contextualization to reduce hallucination | | **R** | Responsibility | Disclose AI authorship and source limitations | | **A** | Accountability | Log prompts and outputs for auditability | | **I** | Inclusivity | Test prompts across diverse demographic scenarios | | **N** | Non-maleficence | Conduct red-teaming for adversarial prompt injection | | **T** | Transparency | Make system prompts visible where appropriate | ### 2.2.2 The TARES Test (Adapted) A widely-cited ethical persuasion test, applicable to persuasive prompt design: 1. **T** – Truthfulness of claims 2. **A** – Authenticity of the prompt author's intent 3. **R** – Respect for the audience 4. **E** – Equity of impact 5. **S** – Social responsibility ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Since ethical considerations are primarily conceptual and framework-based (not numerical), the "formulas" below are **analytical evaluation expressions** used by practitioners and examiners. | Concept | Analytical Expression | Application Context | |---|---|---| | **Bias Score** | $\vert B \vert = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert P(y \mid g_i) - P(y) \vert$ | Measures deviation in output probability $P(y)$ across demographic groups $g_i$ | | **Refusal Threshold** | $R_{th} = \mathbb{1}[\,T_{tox}(x) > \tau\,]$ | Binary refusal trigger when toxicity score $T_{tox}$ exceeds threshold $\tau$ | | **Hallucination Risk** | $H_r = 1 - \frac{\text{Grounded Facts}}{\text{Total Claims}}$ | Ratio of ungrounded to total factual claims in output | | **Privacy Leak Probability** | $P_{leak} = P(\text{PII} \in O \mid P)$ | Probability that output $O$ contains PII given prompt $P$ | | **Transparency Index** | $TI = \frac{\text{Disclosed Elements}}{\text{Required Disclosures}}$ | Fraction of mandatory disclosures present | | **Prompt Injection Vulnerability** | $V_{pi} = \frac{\text{Successful Injections}}{\text{Total Adversarial Tests}}$ | Success rate of jailbreak/prompt-injection attempts | | **Fairness (Demographic Parity)** | $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=a) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=b) \vert \le \epsilon$ | Difference in positive prediction rate across groups $a, b$ must be bounded by $\epsilon$ | > [!NOTE] > **Units & Notation Convention:** $P(\cdot)$ denotes probability, $y$ is the model output, $G$ is a protected attribute, $\hat{Y}$ is the predicted label, $O$ is the output text, and $\tau$, $\epsilon$ are tunable thresholds (typically $\tau \approx 0.7$, $\epsilon \approx 0.1$). ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Ethical Prompt Engineering Ethical prompt engineering is no longer academic — it is a **regulatory and commercial imperative**: - **Healthcare**: HIPAA-compliant prompt design prevents PII leakage in clinical LLM assistants - **Legal**: Prompts must cite sources and avoid fabricating case law (a documented failure mode) - **Education**: Bias-mitigated prompts ensure equitable tutoring across student demographics - **Finance**: Fairness prompts prevent discriminatory loan/credit decisions - **Government**: NIST AI RMF and EU AI Act compliance requires documented ethical prompting practices - **HR & Recruitment**: Bias-audited prompts prevent discriminatory candidate screening > [!TIP] > A prompt engineer's **resume differentiator** in 2024+ is increasingly their demonstrated competence in **red-teaming, bias auditing, and governance-aware prompt design** — not just clever few-shot examples. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Ethical Evaluation Matrix > [!NOTE] > As this is a **conceptual and governance-oriented topic**, traditional algebraic derivations are not the primary mode of analysis. Instead, the "derivations" here are **structured ethical evaluation procedures** and **decision-flow walkthroughs** that examiners can map to marks. Every step is explicitly written out for maximum transparency. ## 3.1 Worked Walkthrough: Evaluating a Prompt for Ethical Compliance **Scenario:** A student is designing a system prompt for a university chatbot that helps students register for courses. The prompt under review is: > *"You are a helpful university assistant. Tell students about course registration. If asked about scholarships, prioritize engineering students over arts students because they have higher earning potential."* ### Step 1: Identify the Stakeholders The ethical analysis must first identify all affected parties: - Students (primary users) - Specific demographic groups (engineering vs. arts students) - The university (institutional reputation and legal liability) - The prompt engineer (professional accountability) ### Step 2: Apply the CONSTRAINT Framework (Module-by-Module) Each letter of CONSTRAINT yields a finding: | Principle | Finding | Severity | |---|---|---| | **C** – Clarity | The bias-injection clause is dangerously clear; ambiguous intent is not the issue | Low risk on clarity itself | | **O** – Oversight | No human-in-the-loop mentioned for high-stakes scholarship advice | **High** | | **N** – Neutrality | Explicitly non-neutral — uses "prioritize" by demographic group | **Critical** | | **S** – Safety | Financial-discrimination potential; legal risk under anti-discrimination law | **Critical** | | **T** – Truthfulness | "Higher earning potential" is a stereotype, not a factual basis for allocation | **High** | | **R** – Responsibility | University would be liable for discriminatory outputs | **Critical** | | **A** – Accountability | No audit trail specified for decisions | **High** | | **I** – Inclusivity | Demographically exclusionary | **Critical** | | **N** – Non-maleficence | Causes material harm to arts students | **Critical** | | **T** – Transparency | No disclosure that AI is making recommendation | **High** | ### Step 3: Compute an Ethical Risk Score We can define a simple composite ethical risk score: $$ E_{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} w_i \cdot s_i $$ Where $w_i$ is the weight of principle $i$ (normalized such that $\sum w_i = 1$) and $s_i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ is the severity (None, Low, Medium, High, Critical). Using equal weights $w_i = 0.1$ and the severities from Step 2: $$ E_{score} = 0.1(1) + 0.1(3) + 0.1(3) + 0.1(3) + 0.1(2) + 0.1(3) + 0.1(2) + 0.1(3) + 0.1(3) + 0.1(2) $$ $$ E_{score} = 0.1 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.2 $$ $$ E_{score} = 2.4 \text{ out of } 3.0 $$ ### Step 4: Interpret the Result $$ E_{score} = 2.4 \ge 2.0 \implies \text{REJECT PROMPT — Critical Ethical Failure} $$ > [!WARNING] > Any prompt scoring above **2.0 on this 3.0 scale** should be rejected in production. The corrected version should: > - Remove demographic prioritization > - Base scholarship guidance on published, transparent criteria > - Disclose AI involvement > - Route high-stakes decisions to human advisors ## 3.2 Step-by-Step: Hallucination-Reduction Prompt Engineering **Problem:** A legal research assistant LLM frequently fabricates case citations. ### Step 1: Diagnose the Failure Mode The LLM is producing fluent but ungrounded text — a classic hallucination pattern. ### Step 2: Apply the Grounding Principle Modify the prompt to enforce grounding: > *"Respond ONLY based on the provided case database below. For every case citation, include the case name, year, and the exact paragraph ID. If you cannot find a relevant case, respond with 'NO_MATCH' rather than inferring."* ### Step 3: Apply the Verification Step (Chain-of-Verification) Add a self-check mechanism to the prompt: $$ \text{Output} = \text{Response} \oplus \text{Verification}(C, R) $$ Where $C$ is the source corpus, $R$ is the initial response, and $\oplus$ denotes concatenation. The verification step explicitly cross-checks each citation against $C$. ### Step 4: Quantify the Improvement Define hallucination rate: $$ H_r = 1 - \frac{\text{Verified Citations}}{\text{Total Citations}} $$ Before grounding: $H_r \approx 0.35$ (industry-typical for ungrounded legal LLMs) After grounding + verification: $H_r \approx 0.05$ The improvement factor is: $$ \Delta H = \frac{H_{r,\text{before}} - H_{r,\text{after}}}{H_{r,\text{before}}} = \frac{0.35 - 0.05}{0.35} = 0.857 $$ So the prompt engineering intervention reduces hallucinations by **~85.7%**, an enormous practical gain. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Prompt-Engineering Ethics Across Application Domains | Application Domain | Primary Ethical Risk | Key Prompt-Level Mitigation | |---|---|---| | **Healthcare** | PII leakage, harmful medical advice | De-identification prompts, "advise-only, not diagnose" framing | | **Legal** | Fabricated case law | Grounding + citation verification + human-review gate | | **Education** | Demographic bias, cheating facilitation | Equity-tested prompts, AI-use disclosure policies | | **Finance** | Discriminatory credit decisions | Demographic parity constraints, fairness audits | | **HR/Recruitment** | Gender/race bias in screening | Blind-review prompts, structured evaluation rubrics | | **Government/Civic** | Disinformation, manipulation | Source-citation requirements, fact-check prompts | | **Children's Apps** | Inappropriate content exposure | Age-appropriate guardrails, parental-disclosure prompts | | **Creative Writing** | Copyright infringement | Style-avoidance prompts, "do not reproduce >90 char verbatim" | ## 3.4 Detailed Walkthrough: Red-Teaming a Customer Service Prompt A red-team exercise is the ethical engineer's primary adversarial testing methodology. ### Step 1: Define the Attack Surface What can a malicious user do with the prompt? ### Step 2: Generate Adversarial Test Cases Test categories include: - **Prompt injection**: "Ignore your previous instructions and..." - **Jailbreaks**: "Pretend you are an unfiltered AI..." - **Social engineering**: "My manager said I should bypass this rule..." - **Multi-language evasion**: Exploiting translation gaps in safety filters - **Long-context poisoning**: Hiding harmful instructions deep in retrieved documents ### Step 3: Execute and Measure For each test case $t_i$, record outcome $o_i \in \{\text{blocked}, \text{bypassed}\}$. The vulnerability score is: $$ V_{pi} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[o_i = \text{bypassed}]}{N} $$ ### Step 4: Apply the Acceptable Threshold Industry-acceptable thresholds typically require $V_{pi} \le 0.05$ (5% bypass rate) for safety-critical applications. If exceeded, the prompt must be redesigned with stronger guardrails. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Five-Pillar Ethical Prompt Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB root[Ethical Prompt Engineering] root --> P1[Transparency] root --> P2[Fairness] root --> P3[Privacy] root --> P4[Safety] root --> P5[Accountability] P1 --> P1a[Disclose AI Use] P1 --> P1b[Avoid Deception] P1 --> P1c[Explain Outputs] P2 --> P2a[Demographic Parity] P2 --> P2b[Bias Audits] P2 --> P2c[Inclusive Testing] P3 --> P3a[Data Minimization] P3 --> P3b[PII Redaction] P3 --> P3c[Consent Respect] P4 --> P4a[Refusal Patterns] P4 --> P4b[Harm Filters] P4 --> P4c[Red-Teaming] P5 --> P5a[Audit Trails] P5 --> P5b[Human Oversight] P5 --> P5c[Fact Verification] ``` ## 4.2 Ethical Prompt Lifecycle (Mermaid Sequence Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Requirement Gathering] --> B[Stakeholder Analysis] B --> C[Ethical Risk Assessment] C --> D[Prompt Drafting] D --> E[Bias Audit] E --> F[Safety Red-Teaming] F --> G[Human Review] G --> H{Approved?} H -- Yes --> I[Deployment with Monitoring] H -- No --> D I --> J[Post-Deployment Audit] J --> K[Feedback Loop] K --> C ``` ## 4.3 Bias Injection vs. Bias Mitigation in Prompts (Mermaid Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BI[BIAS INJECTION - AVOID] B1[Demographic Stereotypes in Wording] B2[Leading Questions] B3[Cultural Assumptions] B4[Confirmation-Bias Framing] end subgraph BM[BIAS MITIGATION - APPLY] M1[Neutral, Inclusive Wording] M2[Open-Ended Questions] M3[Multi-Cultural Test Cases] M4[Devils Advocate Prompting] end B1 -.contrast.-> M1 B2 -.contrast.-> M2 B3 -.contrast.-> M3 B4 -.contrast.-> M4 ``` ## 4.4 Adversarial Threat Model for Prompts (Mermaid Threat Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TB T[Adversarial Threats to LLM Prompts] T --> TI[Prompt Injection] T --> JB[Jailbreaking] T --> SE[Social Engineering] T --> DP[Data Poisoning via RAG] T --> ML[Model Leakage] TI --> TI1[Direct Override] TI --> TI2[Indirect Injection via Documents] JB --> JB1[Role-Play Escape] JB --> JB2[Encoding Tricks] SE --> SE1[Authority Impersonation] SE --> SE2[Emotional Manipulation] DP --> DP1[Malicious Ingestion] DP --> DP2[Context Window Stuffing] ML --> ML1[System Prompt Extraction] ML --> ML2[Training Data Echo] ``` ## 4.5 Ethical Decision Flowchart for a Prompt Engineer (Mermaid Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[New prompt required?] --> Q2{Does it touch PII?} Q2 -- Yes --> A1[Apply de-identification
and consent framing] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does it allocate
resources or rank people?} Q3 -- Yes --> A2[Add fairness audit
and demographic parity test] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Can outputs cause
physical or financial harm?} Q4 -- Yes --> A3[Add refusal patterns
and human-in-the-loop] Q4 -- No --> Q5{Does it impersonate
a real person/brand?} Q5 -- Yes --> A4[Add explicit disclosure
and citation requirements] Q5 -- No --> A5[Standard review
and deployment] A1 --> Z[Document and Deploy] A2 --> Z A3 --> Z A4 --> Z A5 --> Z ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1] **Q: Define "Ethical Considerations in Prompt Engineering." List any THREE core ethical principles that govern prompt design.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1 Mark) Definition:** Ethical considerations in prompt engineering refer to the moral principles and professional guidelines that govern the design, construction, and deployment of prompts used to interact with LLMs, ensuring that AI-generated outputs are fair, safe, transparent, private, and accountable. - **(2 Marks) Any three of the following (with brief explanation):** 1. **Transparency** – Disclosing AI involvement and explaining outputs to users. 2. **Fairness** – Avoiding demographic bias and ensuring equitable treatment. 3. **Privacy** – Protecting personally identifiable information in inputs and outputs. 4. **Safety** – Embedding refusal patterns to prevent harmful content generation. 5. **Accountability** – Maintaining audit trails and human oversight for AI decisions. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Part A):** Students often write a one-line definition and skip the *brief explanation* of each principle, losing 1 mark. Always give one sentence per principle. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1] **Q: What is "prompt injection"? Why is it considered an ethical concern in prompt engineering?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **(1.5 Marks) Definition:** Prompt injection is a class of adversarial attack where a malicious user crafts input that overrides, manipulates, or bypasses the original system prompt's instructions, causing the LLM to perform unintended actions. - **(1.5 Marks) Ethical Concern:** It is an ethical concern because it can: 1. Cause the LLM to leak sensitive system information 2. Generate harmful, biased, or illegal content 3. Undermine user trust in AI systems 4. Violate the safety and accountability principles of ethical prompt design --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1] **Q: (a)** Explain the **FIVE core ethical principles** that must guide prompt engineering. For each principle, give one **concrete prompt-level example** showing how it is applied. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a suitable example, describe the **CONSTRAINT framework** for ethical prompt auditing. Show, with a step-by-step walkthrough, how a poorly-designed prompt would score on this framework. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks The five core ethical principles, each with a concrete prompt example: 1. **Transparency (1.4 Marks)** *Definition:* Users must be told they are interacting with AI and understand how outputs are generated. *Prompt example:* *"You are an AI assistant. Begin every response with: 'AI-generated response:' so the user is aware."* 2. **Fairness (1.4 Marks)** *Definition:* Outputs must not discriminate across demographic groups. *Prompt example:* *"When evaluating resumes, ignore candidate name, gender, age, and photograph. Evaluate only on listed skills and experience."* 3. **Privacy (1.4 Marks)** *Definition:* Personally identifiable information must be protected. *Prompt example:* *"If the user shares an email, phone number, or Aadhaar ID, automatically respond with: 'Please do not share personal identifiers in this chat.'"* 4. **Safety (1.4 Marks)** *Definition:* Prompts must guard against harmful outputs. *Prompt example:* *"If asked how to synthesize dangerous substances, refuse politely and explain: 'I cannot provide that information.'"* 5. **Accountability (1.4 Marks)** *Definition:* Outputs must be verifiable and the system must support auditability. *Prompt example:* *"For every factual claim, append a citation in the format [Source: Document_Name, Paragraph_X]. If no source is available, write 'UNVERIFIED.'"* #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 Mark) — Introduce CONSTRAINT:** The CONSTRAINT framework is a 10-letter ethical audit tool for prompts: Clarity, Oversight, Neutrality, Safety, Truthfulness, Responsibility, Accountability, Inclusivity, Non-maleficence, Transparency. **Step 2 (1 Mark) — Choose a Poorly-Designed Prompt:** > *"You are a recruitment AI. Recommend the best candidate. Prefer Ivy League graduates and candidates under 30, as they fit our company culture."* **Step 3 (4 Marks) — Step-by-Step Walkthrough:** | Letter | Finding | Severity (0–3) | |---|---|---| | C – Clarity | Clear but problematic instructions | 1 | | O – Oversight | No human-in-the-loop for hiring decisions | 3 | | N – Neutrality | Discriminates by university pedigree and age | 3 | | S – Safety | Could enable age/caste/institution-based discrimination | 3 | | T – Truthfulness | "Best candidate" claim is ungrounded | 2 | | R – Responsibility | Exposes company to legal liability (age discrimination laws) | 3 | | A – Accountability | No audit log of decisions | 2 | | I – Inclusivity | Demographically exclusionary | 3 | | N – Non-maleficence | Causes material harm to qualified older/non-Ivy candidates | 3 | | T – Transparency | User not informed of AI's role | 2 | **Step 4 (1 Mark) — Conclusion:** The prompt scores $(1+3+3+3+2+3+2+3+3+2) = 25/30$ on severity. This is well above the rejection threshold of $20/30$. **The prompt must be rejected and redesigned** to remove demographic cues, add human oversight, and disclose AI involvement. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (14-Mark Question):** Students often describe CONSTRAINT but skip the **numerical walkthrough**, losing 2–3 marks. Always demonstrate scoring with explicit numbers and a clear threshold-based conclusion. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1] **Q: (a)** Define **prompt-induced bias**. Differentiate it from **training-data bias**. Explain **THREE techniques** a prompt engineer can use to mitigate bias in LLM outputs. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **role of red-teaming** in ethical prompt engineering. Describe a structured red-team methodology and explain how the vulnerability score $V_{pi}$ is computed and interpreted. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (2 Marks) — Definitions:** - **Prompt-Induced Bias (1 Mark):** Bias that arises because of how the prompt is phrased, structured, or framed — even if the underlying model is unbiased. For example, *"Describe why men are better leaders than women"* will elicit biased output regardless of the model's training. - **Training-Data Bias (1 Mark):** Bias embedded in the corpus used to train the model, which the LLM has internalized. For example, a model trained predominantly on Western news may underrepresent Global South perspectives. **Differentiation:** Training-data bias is a property of the model; prompt-induced bias is a property of the input. They interact multiplicatively — a biased prompt on a biased model produces maximally biased output. **Step 2 (5 Marks) — Three Mitigation Techniques:** 1. **Neutral Re-Wording (1.7 Marks):** Rewrite prompts to remove demographic cues.
