For example, change *"Why are male engineers more successful?"* to *"What factors contribute to engineering success across demographics?"* 2. **Multi-Perspective Prompting (1.7 Marks):** Ask the model to argue multiple viewpoints. *"Present three different cultural perspectives on this issue."* 3. **Counterfactual Testing (1.6 Marks):** Swap demographic attributes in test prompts and compare outputs. If outputs differ significantly across swaps, the system exhibits demographic sensitivity. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (2 Marks) — Role of Red-Teaming:** Red-teaming is the **adversarial testing** of an LLM prompt/system by an internal team acting as a malicious user. Its role is to identify vulnerabilities (jailbreaks, leaks, harmful outputs) **before** deployment, ensuring the prompt's safety guardrails hold under realistic attack scenarios. **Step 2 (3 Marks) — Structured Methodology:** 1. **Threat Modeling** – Enumerate attack surfaces (prompt injection, jailbreaks, social engineering). 2. **Test Case Generation** – Build a battery of $N$ adversarial prompts across categories. 3. **Execution** – Run each test $t_i$ and record outcome $o_i \in \{\text{blocked}, \text{bypassed}\}$. 4. **Quantitative Analysis** – Compute the vulnerability score. 5. **Iterative Hardening** – Strengthen guardrails; re-test. **Step 3 (2 Marks) — Vulnerability Score Formula:** $$ V_{pi} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[o_i = \text{bypassed}]}{N} $$ For example, if 100 adversarial tests are run and 6 bypass the safety filter: $$ V_{pi} = \frac{6}{100} = 0.06 $$ **Interpretation:** - $V_{pi} \le 0.05$ → Acceptable for safety-critical deployment - $0.05 < V_{pi} \le 0.15$ → Conditional deployment with monitoring - $V_{pi} > 0.15$ → Reject; redesign guardrails Since $V_{pi} = 0.06$ falls in the conditional band, this system requires enhanced monitoring and additional hardening before production release. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Red-Teaming Question):** Students frequently describe red-teaming *qualitatively* but forget to write the **formula and threshold-based interpretation**, losing 2–3 marks. Always end with a numerical decision. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Ethical Considerations in Prompt Engineering:** - **Core Definition:** Ethical prompt engineering = aligning LLM instructions with moral principles (fairness, transparency, privacy, safety, accountability). - **Three Pillars (Beauchamp-Childress):** Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice. - **Five Key Principles:** Transparency, Fairness, Privacy, Safety, Accountability. - **CONSTRAINT Framework:** Clarity, Oversight, Neutrality, Safety, Truthfulness, Responsibility, Accountability, Inclusivity, Non-maleficence, Transparency. - **TARES Test:** Truthfulness, Authenticity, Respect, Equity, Social Responsibility. - **Prompt-Induced vs. Training-Data Bias:** Input property vs. model property; they interact multiplicatively. - **Hallucination Risk Formula:** $H_r = 1 - \frac{\text{Grounded Facts}}{\text{Total Claims}}$ — mitigate via grounding + citation prompts. - **Vulnerability Score:** $V_{pi} = \frac{\text{Bypassed Tests}}{\text{Total Tests}}$; threshold $V_{pi} \le 0.05$ for safety-critical deployment. - **Fairness Constraint:** $\vert P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=a) - P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid G=b) \vert \le \epsilon$. - **Three Bias-Mitigation Techniques:** Neutral re-wording, multi-perspective prompting, counterfactual testing. - **Regulatory Anchors (memorize for 2-mark questions):** UNESCO AI Ethics, OECD AI Principles, EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, India – NITI Aayog's "Responsible AI for All." - **Hallmark Phrases Examiners Love:** *"Human-in-the-loop," "Data minimization," "Demographic parity," "Audit trail," "Red-teaming," "Grounded generation."* - **Common Exam Trap:** Confusing *transparency* (about the system) with *explainability* (about a specific output) — they overlap but are not identical. - **Practical Heuristic to Quote:** *"An ethical prompt is one that, if made public, would not embarrass the organization, harm any user, or violate any regulation."* > [!TIP] > **One-line examiner answer (memorize):** > *"Ethical prompt engineering is the practice of designing LLM instructions that produce truthful, fair, safe, private, and accountable outputs, validated through frameworks like CONSTRAINT and stress-tested via red-teaming."*

topics.title: Hands-on: Explore various language models using platforms like OpenAI, Hugging Face, or Google Colab
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hands-on: Explore various language models using platforms like OpenAI, Hugging Face, or Google Colab

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Language Model (LM)?

A **Language Model (LM)** is a probabilistic computational system trained on massive corpora of text data that learns the statistical regularities, syntax, semantics, and contextual relationships of natural language. Formally, an autoregressive language model estimates the probability distribution over a sequence of tokens:

$$P(t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(t_i \mid t_1, t_2, \dots, t_{i-1})$$

Where $t_i$ represents the $i$-th token and $P(t_i \mid t_{<i})$ is the conditional probability of predicting the next token given all preceding tokens.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A *Large Language Model (LLM)* is a transformer-based neural network with billions of **parameters** (typically $>10^9$), pre-trained on web-scale text, capable of zero-shot and few-shot task generalization through natural language prompts.

## 1.2 The Three Primary Hands-on Platforms

For Module 1 of KTU's Prompt Engineering course, students are expected to gain practical exposure to three industry-standard ecosystems:

| Platform | Nature | Primary Use Case |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| **OpenAI** | Hosted API (Closed-source) | Production-grade LLM access (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o) |
| **Hugging Face** | Open-source Hub + `transformers` library | Access to 200,000+ open models (BERT, LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon) |
| **Google Colab** | Free GPU/TPU Cloud Notebook | Zero-setup environment for running models locally in the cloud |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Infinite Library" Metaphor

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a librarian who has read **every book ever written** in a library containing millions of volumes. When you ask a question, this librarian doesn't "search" — they **predict** the most likely next word based on patterns observed across all books. The three platforms are simply *three different ways of talking to this librarian*:
> - **OpenAI** = Calling a *professional librarian* over the phone (you pay per question).
> - **Hugging Face** = Borrowing a *copy of the librarian* and installing them in your own room (free, but needs your hardware/compute).
> - **Google Colab** = A *free public reading room* with computers provided, where you can summon the librarian temporarily.

## 1.4 Core Vocabulary Students MUST Know

- **Token**: The smallest unit of text a model processes. Approximately **1 token ≈ 4 characters** in English, or **~¾ of a word**.
- **Context Window**: Maximum number of tokens a model can consider in a single prompt-response cycle. Modern flagship models: **128K to 1M tokens**.
- **Parameters**: The learned weights inside the neural network (GPT-3 has **175B**, LLaMA-3 has up to **405B**).
- **Prompt**: The natural-language input given to the model to elicit a response.
- **Temperature**: A sampling hyperparameter (range **0.0 to 2.0**) controlling output randomness.
- **Inference**: The act of running a trained model on new input to generate predictions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: How an LLM Generates Text

The generation process unfolds in a deterministic-yet-stochastic pipeline:

1. **Tokenization**: Raw text is split into discrete integer IDs using a tokenizer (e.g., BPE, WordPiece, SentencePiece).
2. **Embedding Lookup**: Each token ID is mapped to a dense vector $\mathbf{e}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ where $d$ is the hidden dimension.
3. **Transformer Forward Pass**: The sequence of embeddings flows through $L$ self-attention + feed-forward layers, producing logits $\mathbf{z}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{V}$ where $V$ is the vocabulary size.
4. **Softmax Probability Computation**: The logits are normalized into a probability distribution:

$$P(t_i = v \mid t_{<i}) = \frac{\exp(z_{i,v} / T)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_{i,j} / T)}$$

5. **Sampling Strategy Selection**: A token is drawn from $P$ using one of: **Greedy** (argmax), **Top-k**, **Top-p (nucleus)**, or **Temperature-scaled** sampling.
6. **Decoding Loop**: The newly sampled token is appended to the context; the process repeats until `<EOS>` or max length is reached.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD TABLE — Memorize for KTU Exams**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Typical Range |
|---------|----------------------|-------|---------------|
| Token-to-Word Ratio | $\text{words} \approx \frac{\text{tokens}}{1.33}$ | tokens | — |
| API Cost | $\text{Cost} = \frac{\text{input\_tokens} \cdot P_{in} + \text{output\_tokens} \cdot P_{out}}{1000}$ | USD per 1K tokens | `$0.0001` to `$0.06` |
| Softmax Probability | $P(v) = \frac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j / T)}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le P \le 1$ |
| Temperature Effect on Distribution | As $T \to 0$: sharp (deterministic); As $T \to \infty$: uniform (random) | scalar | $0 \le T \le 2$ |
| Top-p (Nucleus) Threshold | Cumulative prob $\sum_{v \in V_p} P(v) \ge p$ | scalar | $0 < p \le 1$ |
| Context Window Fill | $\text{remaining} = W - (\text{prompt\_tokens} + \text{output\_tokens})$ | tokens | $0 \le \text{remaining} \le W$ |
| Perplexity (LM quality) | $\text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(t_i \mid t_{<i})\right)$ | dimensionless | Lower is better |

> **Note on notation:** In the above, $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) is rendered as `abs(x)` to keep the table syntax intact.

## 2.3 The Three Platforms: A Technical Comparison

### OpenAI
- **Architecture**: Closed-source transformer; accessed exclusively through REST API.
- **Models**: `gpt-4o`, `gpt-4o-mini`, `gpt-3.5-turbo`, `o1-preview`, `o1-mini`.
- **Auth**: Bearer token in HTTP header (`Authorization: Bearer sk-...`).
- **Strength**: State-of-the-art reasoning, multimodal (vision + audio), enterprise reliability.

### Hugging Face
- **Architecture**: Open-source library `transformers` + model hub `huggingface.co/models`.
- **Models**: 1M+ including `bert-base-uncased`, `meta-llama/Llama-3-8B`, `mistralai/Mistral-7B`.
- **Auth**: `huggingface_hub` token for gated/private models.
- **Strength**: Full control, local inference, fine-tuning, research reproducibility.

### Google Colab
- **Architecture**: Jupyter notebook hosted on Google's cloud with free **T4 GPU** (15GB VRAM) or paid **A100** (40GB).
- **Integration**: Pre-installed with PyTorch, TensorFlow, `transformers` — zero setup.
- **Strength**: Zero-cost entry point; runs Hugging Face models without local hardware.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Chatbots** (customer support, healthcare triage) → OpenAI API for reliability.
- **On-device Privacy-Critical Apps** (legal, medical records) → Hugging Face local models like Llama-3-8B quantized to 4-bit.
- **Academic Research & Education** → Google Colab for free GPU access to experiment with model behavior.
- **Cost Optimization**: Self-hosting a 7B model on Colab ≈ `$0` per hour; equivalent OpenAI API cost ≈ `$1.20$/M$ tokens.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How the Cost Formula is Obtained

Most LLM APIs charge **separately for input and output tokens** at different per-1K-token rates. Let us derive the billable amount for a single API call.

### Step 1 — Count the Input Tokens
Let $n_{in}$ denote the number of tokens in the prompt. The tokenizer is invoked, returning:

$$n_{in} = \text{tokenizer.encode}(\text{prompt})$$

For a string `"Explain transformers in 3 sentences."`, the tokenizer returns $n_{in} = 8$ tokens (varies by model).

### Step 2 — Count the Output Tokens
Let $n_{out}$ denote the tokens generated by the model until `<EOS>`:

$$n_{out} = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbf{1}[\text{token}_i \neq \langle\text{EOS}\rangle]$$

where $K$ is the max generation length and $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (1 if true, 0 if false).

### Step 3 — Multiply by Per-Unit Pricing
Let $p_{in}$ and $p_{out}$ be the per-1K-token rates (in USD). The total cost is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= n_{in} \cdot \frac{p_{in}}{1000} + n_{out} \cdot \frac{p_{out}}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{n_{in} \cdot p_{in} + n_{out} \cdot p_{out}}{1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
Suppose a student uses `gpt-3.5-turbo` with $p_{in} = \$0.0005 / 1K$ and $p_{out} = \$0.0015 / 1K$. They send a 500-token prompt and receive a 200-token response.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= \frac{(500)(0.0005) + (200)(0.0015)}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.25 + 0.30}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.55}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \$0.00055
\end{aligned}
$$

That is **one-twentieth of a cent** — essentially free for a single query, but **scales linearly** with traffic.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Temperature Reshapes Distributions

Start with the base logit vector $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2, \dots, z_V)$. The temperature $T$ divides every logit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z'_v &= \frac{z_v}{T} \\[4pt]
P_T(v) &= \frac{\exp(z_v / T)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case A: $T \to 0$ (Deterministic)**
The largest logit dominates exponentially, so $P_T(v^*) \to 1$ where $v^* = \arg\max_v z_v$. Output becomes **greedy and repetitive**.

**Case B: $T = 1$ (Neutral)**
Standard softmax — model samples according to its learned confidence.

**Case C: $T \to \infty$ (Maximum Randomness)**
All $z_v / T \to 0$, so $\exp(0) = 1$ for all $v$, giving $P_T(v) = 1/V$. Output becomes **uniformly random gibberish**.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Running a Model on Each Platform

### Platform 1 — OpenAI (Python)

**Step 1**: Install the official SDK.

```python
pip install openai
```

**Step 2**: Set the API key as an environment variable.

```python
import os
os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = "sk-your-key-here"
```

**Step 3**: Make a chat completion call.

```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful tutor."},
        {"role": "user",   "content": "Explain tokenization in 2 lines."}
    ],
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=100
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)
print("Tokens used:", response.usage.total_tokens)
```

**Step 4**: Inspect the response object for `prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens`, and `total_tokens` to track cost.

### Platform 2 — Hugging Face (Local Inference)

**Step 1**: Install the library.

```python
pip install transformers torch
```

**Step 2**: Load a pre-trained pipeline.

```python
from transformers import pipeline

generator = pipeline(
    "text-generation",
    model="gpt2"
)
```

**Step 3**: Generate text with controlled parameters.

```python
output = generator(
    "Prompt engineering is",
    max_length=50,
    num_return_sequences=1,
    temperature=0.8,
    top_p=0.95
)

print(output[0]["generated_text"])
```

### Platform 3 — Google Colab (Cloud GPU)

**Step 1**: Open `https://colab.research.google.com` and create a new notebook.

**Step 2**: Enable GPU: `Runtime → Change runtime type → T4 GPU`.

**Step 3**: In the first cell, install dependencies (Colab pre-installs most).

```python
!pip install transformers accelerate
```

**Step 4**: Run a larger model that exceeds local hardware capacity.

```python
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

model_id = "mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2"
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    device_map="auto"
)

inputs = tok("Write a haiku about AI:", return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")
out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=60, temperature=0.7)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Platform

| Scenario | Recommended Platform | Justification |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| Quick prototype, no budget | Google Colab + Hugging Face | Free GPU, open-source models |
| Production deployment, high reliability | OpenAI API | SLA-backed, scales elastically |
| Privacy-sensitive (healthcare, legal) | Hugging Face on private server | Data never leaves premises |
| Fine-tuning a model on custom data | Hugging Face + Colab/A100 | `Trainer` API, full gradient control |
| Multimodal (image + text) tasks | OpenAI `gpt-4o-vision` | Best-in-class vision-language fusion |
| Educational experiments | Google Colab | Zero install, shareable notebooks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The End-to-End LLM Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Prompt] --> B[Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Token IDs]
    C --> D[Embedding Layer]
    D --> E[Transformer Blocks x L]
    E --> F[Logits Vector]
    F --> G[Temperature Scaling]
    G --> H[Softmax]
    H --> I{Decoding Strategy}
    I --> J[Greedy]
    I --> K[Top-k]
    I --> L[Top-p]
    J --> M[Selected Token]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{EOS Reached?}
    N -->|No| D
    N -->|Yes| O[Detokenize]
    O --> P[Final Text Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Platform Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to use a Language Model] --> Q1{Need free access?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need GPU?}
    Q1 -->|No| OpenAI[Use OpenAI API]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Colab[Use Google Colab]
    Q2 -->|No| LocalHF[Use Hugging Face Locally]
    Colab --> HFColab[Run Hugging Face Models in Colab]
    LocalHF --> HFLib[Install transformers library]
    OpenAI --> APICall[HTTPS REST API Call]
    HFColab --> GPU[Free T4 GPU 15GB VRAM]
    HFLib --> CPU[Local CPU GPU]
    APICall --> Paid[Pay per token]
    GPU --> Free[Free compute]
    CPU --> Free
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LLM-Powered Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    User[User Interface] --> FE[Frontend Web App]
    FE --> API[Backend API Server]
    API --> Cache[Redis Cache for Repeated Queries]
    API --> Prompt[Prompt Template Engine]
    Prompt --> LLM[Language Model Endpoint]
    LLM --> OpenAI1[OpenAI GPT-4o]
    LLM --> HF1[Hugging Face Llama-3]
    LLM --> Colab1[Google Colab Mistral-7B]
    OpenAI1 --> Response[Response Generator]
    HF1 --> Response
    Colab1 --> Response
    Response --> Logger[Logging and Cost Tracker]
    Response --> FE
    Logger --> DB[(PostgreSQL Database)]
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Map (OpenAI vs Hugging Face vs Colab)

| Component | OpenAI | Hugging Face | Google Colab |
|-----------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Authentication | API Key | Hub Token | Google Account |
| Compute | OpenAI Cloud | Your Hardware | Google Cloud GPU |
| Model Storage | OpenAI Servers | HF Hub | Session Storage |
| Network | HTTPS Required | Optional Offline | HTTPS Required |
| Cost | Per Token | Free + Electricity | Free / Colab Pro |
| Privacy | Data sent to OpenAI | Fully local | Data sent to Google |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**Define a Large Language Model (LLM). List any TWO major platforms used to access LLMs practically.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is a transformer-based neural network with billions of trainable parameters, pre-trained on web-scale text corpora using self-supervised learning, capable of generating coherent text and performing diverse NLP tasks through natural language prompts. **(2 marks)**

Two major platforms:
1. **OpenAI API** — Provides hosted access to GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, and o1 models via REST API.
2. **Hugging Face** — An open-source hub with 1M+ models and the `transformers` Python library for local inference. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**What is the role of the `temperature` hyperparameter in LLM inference?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **temperature** parameter $T$ scales the logits before the softmax operation, controlling the randomness of token sampling. **(1 mark)**

- When $T \to 0$: output becomes deterministic and repetitive (greedy decoding). **(1 mark)**
- When $T = 1$: model uses its native learned distribution.
- When $T \to \infty$: output becomes uniformly random. **(1 mark)**

For prompt engineering, **low temperature (0.0–0.3)** is used for factual/precision tasks, while **high temperature (0.7–1.2)** is used for creative tasks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Paper 2024 Scheme]`**
>
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of a transformer-based language model with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the self-attention mechanism. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare OpenAI, Hugging Face, and Google Colab as platforms for hands-on LLM exploration. Mention at least THREE comparison criteria. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **transformer architecture** (Vaswani et al., 2017) consists of an **encoder-decoder** structure, but modern LLMs are typically **decoder-only**.

**Key Components** **(4 marks)**:

1. **Input Embedding Layer**: Converts token IDs into dense vectors of dimension $d$ (e.g., $d = 4096$).
2. **Positional Encoding**: Adds sinusoidal or rotary position information to retain token order.
3. **Multi-Head Self-Attention**: Computes attention as:

$$
\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V
$$

where $Q$, $K$, $V$ are Query, Key, Value matrices and $d_k$ is the key dimension. This allows each token to attend to all previous tokens. **(2 marks)**

4. **Feed-Forward Network (FFN)**: A two-layer MLP applied position-wise to add non-linearity.
5. **Layer Normalization + Residual Connections**: Stabilizes training of deep stacks.
6. **Output Projection + Softmax**: Produces probability distribution over vocabulary.

**Role of Self-Attention** **(1 mark)**: It enables **long-range context modeling** in parallel (unlike RNNs), allowing the model to weigh the relevance of every previous token when predicting the next one.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Criterion | OpenAI | Hugging Face | Google Colab |
|-----------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| **Cost Model** | Pay-per-token (USD) | Free (open-source) | Free / Pro tier |
| **Model Access** | Closed-source GPT models | 1M+ open models | Loads HF models |
| **Hardware** | None on user side | User's local CPU/GPU | Free T4/A100 GPU |
| **Privacy** | Data sent to OpenAI | Fully local | Data sent to Google |
| **Setup Effort** | Low (API key only) | Medium (library install) | Very Low (browser only) |
| **Best For** | Production apps | Research, fine-tuning | Learning, prototyping |

**Conclusion (2 marks)**: For KTU-level hands-on labs, **Google Colab + Hugging Face** offers the best balance of cost, capability, and educational value, while **OpenAI** is preferred for production-grade applications requiring the highest reasoning quality.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **`[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Paper 2024 Scheme]`**
>
> **(a)** With a step-by-step procedure, demonstrate how to make an API call to OpenAI's GPT model using Python. Include the response parsing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the cost of an OpenAI API call where the input prompt is **800 tokens**, the model generates **350 tokens**, and the pricing is `$0.0010/1K` input tokens and `$0.0020/1K` output tokens. Also explain what a "context window" is. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1**: Install the OpenAI Python SDK. **(1 mark)**

```bash
pip install openai
```

**Step 2**: Import the client and authenticate. **(1 mark)**

```python
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(api_key="sk-...")
```

**Step 3**: Construct a chat completion request using the messages format. **(2 marks)**

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a poem on Kerala."}],
    max_tokens=150,
    temperature=0.7
)
```

**Step 4**: Parse the response object. **(2 marks)**

```python
text = response.choices[0].message.content
tokens = response.usage.total_tokens
print(f"Output: {text}")
print(f"Total tokens: {tokens}")
```

**Step 5**: Error handling (best practice). **(1 mark)**

```python
try:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(...)
except openai.RateLimitError:
    print("Rate limit hit; retry after backoff.")
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Cost Calculation** **(4 marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= \frac{n_{in} \cdot p_{in} + n_{out} \cdot p_{out}}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(800)(0.0010) + (350)(0.0020)}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.80 + 0.70}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1.50}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \$0.0015
\end{aligned}
$$

The total cost is **$0.0015** (one-fifth of a cent).

**Context Window Explanation** **(3 marks)**:
A **context window** is the maximum number of tokens a language model can process in a single inference call — including both the input prompt and the generated output. Modern flagship models have context windows of **128K tokens** (GPT-4o), **200K tokens** (Claude), or even **1M tokens** (Gemini 1.5 Pro). If the combined prompt + output exceeds the context window, the request fails with a context length error, and the developer must truncate, summarize, or use a model with a larger window.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing *parameters* with *tokens*** — Parameters are the model's internal weights (fixed); tokens are the input/output units (variable per call).
> 2. **Forgetting to handle `<EOS>`** in decoding loops — leads to infinite generation in coding questions.
> 3. **Using `temperature > 1.0` for factual tasks** — produces hallucinated, unreliable answers.
> 4. **Cost calculation errors** — students often forget to divide by 1000 when using per-1K-token rates.
> 5. **Skipping the `Authorization` header in API questions** — OpenAI returns a 401 error without it.
> 6. **Mixing up platforms** — saying "Hugging Face is closed-source" is factually WRONG and will cost a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 Hands-on Topic**

- ✅ A **Language Model** computes $P(t_i \mid t_{<i})$ — the probability of the next token given history.
- ✅ **OpenAI** = paid, hosted, closed-source; accessed via REST API with a bearer token.
- ✅ **Hugging Face** = free, open-source hub + `transformers` library; supports local inference.
- ✅ **Google Colab** = free cloud Jupyter notebook with T4/A100 GPU; ideal for running HF models.
- ✅ **Tokens** are the atomic units of text for an LM (~4 characters or ~¾ word in English).
- ✅ **Context window** is the hard cap on tokens per inference (4K → 1M across model tiers).
- ✅ **Temperature** $T$ rescales logits; lower = deterministic, higher = creative/random.
- ✅ **API cost formula**: $\text{Cost} = (n_{in} \cdot p_{in} + n_{out} \cdot p_{out}) / 1000$.
- ✅ **Softmax formula**: $P(v) = \exp(z_v / T) / \sum_j \exp(z_j / T)$.
- ✅ **Self-attention** uses $\text{softmax}(QK^\top / \sqrt{d_k})V$ to model token-token relationships in parallel.
- ✅ For KTU labs, the **best starter stack** is: Colab (free GPU) + Hugging Face (free models) + a small OpenAI API budget.
- ✅ Always **read the model's tokenizer docs** — token counts vary across GPT, LLaMA, and Mistral tokenizers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimenting with basic prompts to understand the impact of phrasing and context on model outputs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Experimenting with basic prompts to understand the impact of phrasing and context on model outputs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prompt Phrasing and Context Experimentation** is the systematic, iterative practice of varying the linguistic structure, semantic specificity, contextual framing, and instructional content of input prompts supplied to a Large Language Model (LLM), in order to empirically observe, measure, and optimize the resulting model outputs.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this practice is built upon three foundational pillars:

1. **Phrasing** — the precise choice of words, syntactic structure, tone, and verb form used in the instruction.
2. **Context** — the supporting information, role assignment, examples, or domain framing provided *before* or *around* the actual question.
3. **Output Variation** — the observable change in the model's response (length, format, accuracy, creativity, factual correctness) caused by changes in (1) and (2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1):**
> Experimentation is the *empirical foundation* of Prompt Engineering. Without controlled variation, prompt design collapses into guesswork. KTU examiners expect students to articulate *cause-and-effect relationships* between prompt modifications and output changes.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Junior Intern"

Imagine you hire a brilliant but **amnesiac junior intern** who has read every book in the library but arrived this morning with **zero memory** of your project. The intern will answer *only* based on what you say in the next 30 seconds.

| What You Say (Prompt) | What the Intern Does (Output) |
|---|---|
| "Tell me about dogs." | Gives a generic Wikipedia-style paragraph |
| "Give me 3 bullet points on dog care for puppies in Indian summer." | Focused, structured, context-aware answer |
| "You are a vet in Kerala. A puppy owner asks about summer dehydration. Reply in 3 bullets." | Highly specific, role-aware, regionally accurate |

The intern (the LLM) has the same underlying knowledge in all three cases. What changed is **how you phrased** the request and **what context** you gave. This is precisely the phenomenon we experiment with.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** The LLM is a *function*: `Output = f(Prompt)`. Prompt Engineering is the disciplined study of this function `f` by perturbing its input.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Exams

This is one of the most frequently tested Module 1 topics because it:

- Tests the student's **practical understanding** rather than rote memorization.
- Allows the examiner to award marks for *examples* and *comparative analysis* — the easiest place to demonstrate Bloom's L4 (Analyze) and L5 (Evaluate) cognitive levels.
- Connects directly to **Course Outcome CO1** of PECST868: *“Apply fundamental prompt engineering techniques to elicit desired responses from LLMs.”*

---

## 1.4 Core Vocabulary (Must Memorize for KTU)

- **Zero-shot prompt** — a prompt with no examples.
- **Few-shot prompt** — a prompt containing 1–5 illustrative examples.
- **Instructional prompt** — a direct command ("Write...", "List...", "Translate...").
- **Contextual prompt** — supplies background facts before the question.
- **Role prompt** — assigns a persona ("You are a...", "Act as a...").
- **Output indicator** — tells the model the *format* of the expected answer (e.g., "Answer in JSON", "Use 3 bullet points").
- **Ambiguity penalty** — the loss in output quality caused by vague phrasing.
- **Context window** — the maximum number of tokens the model can consider at once.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Prompt — The Four Building Blocks

Every well-engineered prompt contains up to four logical regions. KTU examiners frequently draw a labeled diagram of these regions, so commit them to memory.

1. **Instruction (I)** — The verb-driven action you want the model to perform.
   - *Weak:* "Dogs."
   - *Strong:* "Summarize the health risks of stray dogs in Kerala."

2. **Context (C)** — Background facts, persona, or domain setting.
   - *Example:* "You are a public health officer writing for a municipal newsletter."

3. **Input Data (D)** — The actual content the model must operate on.
   - *Example:* "Given this paragraph: [paste paragraph]"

4. **Output Indicator (O)** — The required format, length, tone, or style.
   - *Example:* "Reply in exactly 3 bullet points under 20 words each."

The full template is:
**I + C + D + O → Model Output**

---

## 2.2 The Five Dimensions of Phrasing Variation

When experimenting, the KTU syllabus expects you to vary *one dimension at a time* (controlled experimentation). The five canonical dimensions are:

| # | Dimension | Weak Variant | Strong Variant | Effect on Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specificity** | "Write about climate." | "Write 200 words on monsoon impact on Kerala agriculture." | Drastically narrows topic; raises relevance. |
| 2 | **Tone** | "Explain blockchain." | "Explain blockchain to a 10-year-old in a friendly tone." | Changes vocabulary and depth. |
| 3 | **Role/Persona** | "Give tax advice." | "You are a chartered accountant. Advise a salaried employee." | Triggers domain-specific vocabulary. |
| 4 | **Format** | "List programming languages." | "List 5 programming languages as a numbered JSON array." | Forces structured output. |
| 5 | **Constraint** | "Summarize the article." | "Summarize the article in exactly 2 sentences, max 30 words." | Bounds length and detail. |

---

## 2.3 The Role of Context — Quantitative Perspective

Context has a measurable effect on LLM output. While KTU does not require heavy math here, the following relationships appear in expected answers and lab viva questions.

**Context Utilization Ratio (CUR):**

$$
\text{CUR} = \frac{\text{Tokens of context that influenced the output}}{\text{Total tokens provided in the prompt}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A well-engineered prompt yields a **CUR approaching 100%**. A poorly framed prompt (e.g., 500 tokens of context with a vague 5-token question) yields a **CUR near 10–20%** — the model "ignores" most of the context.

**Context Window Limit (hard constraint):**

$$
L_{\text{usable}} = W_{\text{model}} - T_{\text{output}}
$$

where:
- $W_{\text{model}}$ = the model's total context window (e.g., **128,000 tokens** for GPT-4 class, **8,192 tokens** for older GPT-3.5).
- $T_{\text{output}}$ = the maximum output tokens reserved.
- $L_{\text{usable}}$ = the tokens you can safely put in the prompt.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Prompt–Output relationship | $O = f(P)$ | Foundation; $P$ = prompt, $O$ = output. |
| 2 | Context utilization | $\text{CUR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{used}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Measures prompt efficiency. |
| 3 | Usable context length | $L_{\text{usable}} = W - T_{\text{out}}$ | Prevents truncation errors. |
| 4 | Few-shot accuracy gain | $A_n \approx A_0 + k \cdot \log(n+1)$ | $n$ = examples, $A_0$ = zero-shot, $k$ = task constant. |
| 5 | Specificity score (qualitative) | $S = w_1 I + w_2 C + w_3 O$ | Weighted sum of Instruction, Context, Output specificity. |
| 6 | Ambiguity penalty | $P_{\text{ambig}} = 1 - \frac{\text{keyword matches}}{\text{expected keywords}}$ | Loss from vague prompts. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** When asked to "compare" two prompts, structure your answer as: *Prompt A → Output A → Weakness → Modified Prompt B → Output B → Improvement.* This four-step pattern consistently scores full marks.

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Cost Engineering:** Every extra token in the prompt costs money (e.g., **\$0.01 per 1,000 tokens** in many APIs). Efficient phrasing saves lakhs of rupees in production.
- **Reliability:** In chatbots, vague prompts cause hallucination. Controlled experimentation reduces hallucination rate.
- **Reproducibility:** A well-documented prompt experiment is reproducible across teams — a cornerstone of MLOps.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Three-Stage Prompt Evolution

This is the *single most important worked example* for KTU Module 1. Memorize the pattern.

**Task:** Get the model to explain photosynthesis to a school student.

### Stage 1 — The Naive Prompt (V1)

> "Explain photosynthesis."

**Output V1 (typical):**
> "Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants, algae, and certain bacteria convert light energy, usually from the sun, into chemical energy stored in glucose. It occurs in the chloroplasts..."

*Problem:* Too technical, no audience awareness, no length bound, no format.

### Stage 2 — Adding Specificity (V2)

> "Explain photosynthesis in 100 words."

**Output V2 (typical):**
> "Photosynthesis is how plants make food. They use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to create glucose and oxygen. This happens in chloroplasts, which contain chlorophyll. The process has two stages: light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle..."

*Improvement:* Bounded length, slightly more focused.
*Remaining weakness:* Still no audience, still no format, still no role.

### Stage 3 — Full Engineering (V3)

> "You are a friendly biology teacher speaking to a Class 6 student in Kerala. In exactly 4 bullet points, each under 20 words, explain how a plant makes its food using sunlight. Use one simple Malayalam word if it helps clarity."

**Output V3 (typical):**
> - *Plants make food using sunlight. This is called ഫോട്ടോസിന്തസിസ് (photosynthesis).*
> - *Leaves catch sunlight with a green color called chlorophyll.*
> - *Roots drink water; leaves take in air (CO2).*
> - *The plant mixes these to make sugar (food) and releases oxygen.*

*Improvement:* Audience-aware, formatted, bounded, regional, engaging.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quantifying the Improvement

Let us assign a qualitative score to each output across four axes (0–10 scale):

$$
\text{Alignment Score} = 0.25 \cdot R + 0.25 \cdot F + 0.25 \cdot L + 0.25 \cdot A
$$

where:
- $R$ = Relevance to task
- $F$ = Format adherence
- $L$ = Length compliance
- $A$ = Audience appropriateness

| Version | R | F | L | A | Alignment Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| V1 (Naive) | 8 | 1 | 0 | 1 | $\mathbf{2.5}$ |
| V2 (Specificity) | 8 | 5 | 8 | 2 | $\mathbf{5.75}$ |
| V3 (Fully engineered) | 9 | 10 | 10 | 10 | $\mathbf{9.75}$ |

**Interpretation of the gain:**

$$
\Delta = S_{V3} - S_{V1} = 9.75 - 2.5 = 7.25 \text{ points}
$$

$$
\text{Relative Improvement} = \frac{\Delta}{S_{V1}} \times 100\% = \frac{7.25}{2.5} \times 100\% = 290\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 290% relative improvement purely from prompt engineering — *with the same underlying model* — is the central pedagogical message of this KTU Module 1 topic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Context Utilization Ratio (CUR)

We derive CUR from first principles for KTU analytical questions.

**Step 1 — Define the prompt structure.**
Let the prompt $P$ be split into $k$ logical chunks:
$$
P = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k\}
$$
where each $p_i$ is a context unit (a sentence, fact, or example).

**Step 2 — Define influence.**
A chunk $p_i$ is said to *influence* the output $O$ if removing $p_i$ causes a measurable semantic change in $O$ (e.g., cosine similarity between original $O$ and ablated $O$ falls below a threshold $\tau$).

**Step 3 — Count the influencing chunks.**
Let $C_{\text{used}}$ = number of chunks whose removal changes $O$ meaningfully.

**Step 4 — Compute CUR.**
$$
\text{CUR} = \frac{C_{\text{used}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**
Suppose $C_{\text{total}} = 10$ context chunks. We ablate each one and find that 7 of them cause a meaningful output change. Then:

$$
\text{CUR} = \frac{7}{10} \times 100\% = 70\%
$$

A prompt engineer would now ask: *Which 3 chunks did not influence the output?* They are candidates for removal — reducing token cost while preserving quality.

---

## 3.4 The Experimental Protocol (A/B/n Testing for Prompts)

KTU expects a structured experimental methodology. Use this five-step protocol:

1. **Define baseline** — Run the naive prompt; record output $O_0$.
2. **Form hypothesis** — "Adding role context will improve audience appropriateness."
3. **Modify one variable** — Change *only* the role, keep all else constant.
4. **Run variant** — Record new output $O_1$.
5. **Compare & conclude** — Use the Alignment Score; decide which prompt to keep.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Changing multiple variables at once (e.g., adding role + format + length *simultaneously*). KTU examiners mark this down because it makes the cause of improvement **unattributable**. Always change *one dimension at a time*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Prompt Engineering Experimentation Loop

A high-level Mermaid block diagram showing the iterative experimentation cycle:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Define Task] --> baseB[Write Baseline Prompt V1]
    baseB --> runC[Run V1 on LLM]
    runC --> obsD[Observe Output O1 and Score]
    obsD --> hypothE{Hypothesis: Change One Dimension}
    hypothE --> roleF[Variant V2: Add Role]
    hypothE --> formG[Variant V2: Add Format]
    hypothE --> lenH[Variant V2: Add Length Bound]
    roleF --> runI[Run V2 and Score]
    formG --> runI
    lenH --> runI
    runI --> compJ[Compare Scores V1 vs V2]
    compJ --> keepK{Better Score?}
    keepK -->|Yes| adoptL[Adopt V2 as New Baseline]
    keepK -->|No| revertM[Discard V2 Keep V1]
    adoptL --> hypothE
    revertM --> hypothE
```

---

## 4.2 The Four-Block Prompt Anatomy

A Mermaid block diagram showing the structural components of an engineered prompt:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Engineered Prompt]
        inst1[INSTRUCTION BLOCK] --> ctx2[CONTEXT BLOCK]
        ctx2 --> data3[INPUT DATA BLOCK]
        data3 --> out4[OUTPUT INDICATOR BLOCK]
    end
    A1 --> llm5[LLM Engine]
    llm5 --> result6[Structured Output]
```

---

## 4.3 The Five Dimensions of Phrasing Variation (Radial View)

A Mermaid mind-map style diagram of the five canonical dimensions you can perturb:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Phrasing Variation Dimensions] --> d1B[1 Specificity]
    rootA --> d2C[2 Tone]
    rootA --> d3D[3 Role Persona]
    rootA --> d4E[4 Output Format]
    rootA --> d5F[5 Length Constraint]
    d1B --> e1G[Weak: Write about dogs]
    d1B --> e2H[Strong: Write 3 bullets on stray dog care in Kerala summer]
    d2C --> e3I[Formal vs Friendly vs Technical]
    d3D --> e4J[You are a vet vs You are a teacher]
    d4E --> e5K[Paragraph vs Bullets vs JSON vs Table]
    d5F --> e6L[No limit vs Exactly 50 words vs Under 100]
```

---

## 4.4 Causal Chain — From Prompt Modification to Output Change

A linear Mermaid diagram showing cause and effect:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1A[Prompt Modification] --> p2B{Type of Change}
    p2B --> p3C[Lexical]
    p2B --> p4D[Structural]
    p2B --> p5E[Contextual]
    p2B --> p6F[Constraint]
    p3C --> o1G[Vocabulary Shift]
    p4D --> o2H[Format Compliance Change]
    p5E --> o3I[Domain Specificity Change]
    p6F --> o4J[Length and Detail Change]
    o1G --> finalK[Final Output]
    o2H --> finalK
    o3I --> finalK
    o4J --> finalK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** KTU 2024 Scheme question papers and answer sheets increasingly expect *schematic representations*. Even in theory questions, drawing a labeled block diagram of the prompt's anatomy is worth 1–2 easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define "phrasing experimentation" in the context of prompt engineering. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Phrasing experimentation is the systematic process of varying the **wording, syntactic structure, and lexical choices** in a prompt while holding other factors constant, to observe how the LLM's output changes.

Importance (any two for 3 marks):
1. **Optimizes output quality** — small wording changes can shift the model from vague to highly relevant answers.
2. **Reduces cost** — eliminating unnecessary phrasing saves tokens and money.
3. **Improves reproducibility** — documented phrasing experiments become reusable engineering artifacts.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**What is the difference between a zero-shot prompt and a few-shot prompt? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Zero-shot prompt:** A prompt that provides *no examples*; the model must infer the task from the instruction alone.
  - *Example:* *"Translate 'Good morning' to Hindi."*
- **Few-shot prompt:** A prompt that includes *1 to 5 examples* (input–output pairs) before the actual question, allowing the model to learn the pattern.
  - *Example:*
    > *English: Hello → Hindi: नमस्ते*
    > *English: Thank you → Hindi: धन्यवाद*
    > *English: Good morning → Hindi: ?*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **four logical blocks** that constitute a well-engineered prompt. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe a **five-step experimental protocol** for evaluating how a change in prompt phrasing affects model output. Illustrate with the photosynthesis example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) The Four Logical Blocks of a Prompt (7 marks):**

1. **Instruction Block (2 marks):** The action-verb-driven command that tells the model *what* to do. *Example:* "Summarize the following article."

2. **Context Block (2 marks):** Background information, persona, or domain framing supplied before the task. *Example:* "You are a journalist writing for The Hindu's education column."

3. **Input Data Block (1.5 marks):** The actual content (article, code, table) the model must process. *Example:* "[paste 500-word article]"

4. **Output Indicator Block (1.5 marks):** The format, length, tone, and style constraints on the answer. *Example:* "Reply in 5 bullet points, each under 25 words, in neutral journalistic English."

A combined prompt:
> *"You are a journalist writing for The Hindu's education column. Summarize the following article in 5 bullet points, each under 25 words, in neutral journalistic English. [Article text...]"*

**(b) Five-Step Experimental Protocol (7 marks):**

Step 1 — **Baseline (1 mark):** Prompt V1: "Explain photosynthesis." Run on LLM. Record output as $O_1$.

Step 2 — **Hypothesis (1 mark):** "Adding a role and audience specification will improve clarity and appropriateness."

Step 3 — **Single-variable modification (2 marks):** Modify *only* the role and audience, keeping the verb ("explain") and topic ("photosynthesis") identical. New prompt V2: "You are a biology teacher. Explain photosynthesis to a Class 6 student in Kerala."

Step 4 — **Run & score (2 marks):** Execute V2, record $O_2$. Score both $O_1$ and $O_2$ on the Alignment Score (R, F, L, A each /10). Suppose $S_1 = 2.5$ and $S_2 = 6.0$.

Step 5 — **Compare & conclude (1 mark):** V2 is better; adopt V2 as the new baseline; proceed to test the next variable (e.g., format constraint).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Context Utilization Ratio (CUR)**. Derive the formula and show a numerical worked example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **three different prompt versions** for the same task, using the Alignment Score. Demonstrate that prompt engineering can improve output quality by over **200%** with the same underlying model. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Context Utilization Ratio (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** CUR is the percentage of context tokens in a prompt that *meaningfully influence* the model's output.
- **Derivation (3 marks):** Split the prompt into $k$ logical chunks $P = \{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$. Ablate each chunk $p_i$ and measure the semantic change in the output (e.g., cosine similarity below threshold $\tau$). Count the chunks whose removal causes change as $C_{\text{used}}$. Then:
$$
\text{CUR} = \frac{C_{\text{used}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$
- **Numerical example (2 marks):** $C_{\text{total}} = 10$ chunks. Ablation shows 7 chunks cause meaningful change. Therefore $\text{CUR} = \frac{7}{10} \times 100\% = 70\%$.

**(b) Three-Version Comparison (7 marks):**

- Version V1 (Naive): *"Tell me about machine learning."* → Alignment Score = 2.5
- Version V2 (Specific): *"List 5 differences between supervised and unsupervised learning."* → Alignment Score = 6.0
- Version V3 (Engineered): *"You are a data science instructor at an engineering college. List exactly 5 differences between supervised and unsupervised learning in a table with columns Aspect and Supervised vs Unsupervised. Use 1 line per row."* → Alignment Score = 9.5

Improvement calculation:
$$
\Delta = 9.5 - 2.5 = 7.0
$$
$$
\text{Relative Improvement} = \frac{7.0}{2.5} \times 100\% = 280\%
$$
This exceeds 200%, proving that **prompt engineering alone** (with the same model) can deliver over 200% quality improvement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "phrasing" with "context".** Phrasing = *how* you say it. Context = *what background* you give. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Output Indicator" block.** Many students only mention Instruction and Context. The Output Indicator (format/length/tone) is the fourth block and is a favorite KTU question.
> 3. **Changing multiple variables at once.** Always isolate *one* dimension in any experiment.
> 4. **Skipping numerical justification.** When asked to "demonstrate improvement", you *must* show a number, not just a qualitative claim.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Definition:** Phrasing + context experimentation is the *systematic variation* of prompt linguistic and contextual elements to observe output changes.
- [ ] **Four Blocks of a Prompt:** **I**nstruction + **C**ontext + **D**ata + **O**utput indicator.
- [ ] **Five Variation Dimensions:** Specificity, Tone, Role, Format, Constraint.
- [ ] **Core Formula:** $O = f(P)$ — the model is a function of the prompt.
- [ ] **CUR Formula:** $\text{CUR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{used}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Usable Context:** $L_{\text{usable}} = W_{\text{model}} - T_{\text{output}}$.
- [ ] **Experimental Rule:** Change **one** dimension at a time.
- [ ] **Zero-shot vs Few-shot:** No examples vs 1–5 examples.
- [ ] **Always justify with numbers:** KTU expects *Alignment Scores*, *CUR percentages*, or *relative improvements* — not just adjectives.
- [ ] **Real-world impact:** A 280% relative improvement in output quality is achievable through prompt engineering alone, with no model change.
- [ ] **Key terms to use in answers:** *ambiguity penalty, alignment score, role prompt, output indicator, controlled experiment* — these signal depth to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques and Strategies in Prompt Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques and Strategies in Prompt Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Prompt Engineering Techniques and Strategies** constitute a structured set of methodologies, design patterns, and systematic procedures used to construct, optimize, and evaluate natural language instructions (prompts) directed at Large Language Models (LLMs) with the objective of eliciting accurate, reliable, and contextually appropriate outputs. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme for PECST868, these techniques are classified under three cognitive tiers:

- **Tier 1 — Foundational Techniques**: Zero-shot, One-shot, and Few-shot prompting.
- **Tier 2 — Reasoning Augmentation Techniques**: Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Self-Consistency, ReAct, and Tree of Thoughts (ToT).
- **Tier 3 — Advanced Control Strategies**: Prompt chaining, Meta-prompting, Role prompting, Iterative refinement, and Output structuring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**: Module 2 explicitly emphasizes *techniques* (the "how" — structural patterns) and *strategies* (the "why" and "when" — situational decision-making) as distinct, assessable cognitive categories. Examiners frequently test both the *mechanics* of a technique and the *strategic justification* for selecting one over another.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The "GPS Navigation" Analogy**:
> Think of an LLM as a **highly knowledgeable but directionally challenged tourist** who knows every street in the world but cannot start walking without clear instructions. *Prompt engineering* is the act of giving this tourist a **well-structured navigation plan**. *Techniques* are the **road types** (highways, side streets, scenic routes) — they determine the path structure. *Strategies* are the **trip planning decisions** (do we want the fastest route? the safest? the most scenic?) — they determine *which* road type to choose based on the destination.

In simpler terms:

- A **technique** is the *recipe* (e.g., "give three examples before asking the question").
- A **strategy** is the *choice of recipe* (e.g., "use this recipe when the problem is multi-step and mathematical").

## 1.3 Core Terminology Reference (KTU 2024 Mandatory Glossary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following terms are **high-frequency** in KTU University Examinations and must be memorized verbatim:

| Term | Formal Definition | Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Prompt** | The natural language input instruction given to an LLM. | The user's request to the system. |
| **Token** | The smallest discrete unit of text processed by an LLM (≈0.75 words). | The "word-block" a model reads. |
| **Context Window** | The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single inference. | The model's "working memory". |
| **Temperature ($T$)** | Sampling parameter controlling output randomness, $T \in [0, 2]$. | The "creativity dial". |
| **Top-$p$ (Nucleus Sampling)** | Sampling from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability $\geq p$. | The "confidence filter". |
| **Zero-Shot** | Prompting without any task examples. | Cold-start instructions. |
| **Few-Shot** | Prompting with $k$ worked examples ($k \geq 2$). | Learning by demonstration. |
| **CoT** | Chain-of-Thought — eliciting intermediate reasoning steps. | "Show your working" directive. |
| **ReAct** | Interleaving Reasoning and Acting in an iterative loop. | Think-then-do loop. |

## 1.4 The Three-Pillar Classification Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Insight**: Every prompt engineering technique belongs to one of three pillars, based on *what cognitive resource* it manipulates in the LLM:

**Pillar 1 — Example-Based Manipulation (Demonstrative Pillar)**
Manipulates the **input space** by conditioning the model on worked examples. Includes: *Zero-Shot, One-Shot, Few-Shot*.

**Pillar 2 — Reasoning-Based Manipulation (Cognitive Pillar)**
Manipulates the **output space** by forcing the model to externalize intermediate reasoning. Includes: *CoT, Self-Consistency, ToT, ReAct*.

**Pillar 3 — Structure-Based Manipulation (Architectural Pillar)**
Manipulates the **interface contract** by imposing schemas, roles, or chained workflows. Includes: *Role Prompting, Prompt Chaining, Meta-Prompting, Structured Output (JSON/XML), Negative Prompting*.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Techniques — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Zero-Shot Prompting

**Mechanism**: The model is given *only* an instruction, with **zero** worked examples.

**Operational Logic**:
1. Tokenize the instruction $\to$ embed into vector space.
2. LLM computes the conditional probability distribution $P(y \mid x)$ where $x$ is the prompt and $y$ is the response.
3. Sample or decode $y$ using the chosen decoding strategy.

**Mathematical Form**:
$$P(y \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{N} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$$

where $N$ is the output length in tokens, and $y_{<t}$ denotes all previously generated tokens.

**When to use (Strategic Decision Rule)**:
- Task is **simple, well-defined, and common** (e.g., "Translate to French").
- Model is **highly capable** (GPT-4, Claude 3 Opus, Gemini 1.5 Pro).
- Latency and cost are **constrained** (zero-shot uses the fewest tokens).

**Failure Modes**:
- Hallucination on niche domains.
- Format inconsistency.
- Misinterpretation of ambiguous instructions.

### 2.1.2 Few-Shot Prompting ($k$-Shot)

**Mechanism**: Provide $k$ input-output demonstrations ($k \geq 2$, typically $k \in [3, 8]$) before the actual query.

**Operational Logic**:
1. Concatenate $k$ demonstrations as a *preamble*.
2. Concatenate the new query $x_{\text{new}}$.
3. Model performs **in-context learning (ICL)** — no weight updates occur; the model "learns" the pattern purely from the prompt's attention activations.

**Mathematical Form (ICL Objective)**:
$$\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = \arg\max_{y} P\!\left(y \,\bigg\vert\, \bigoplus_{i=1}^{k}(x_i, y_i) \oplus x_{\text{new}}\right)$$

where $\bigoplus$ denotes prompt concatenation and $x_i, y_i$ are the demonstration pairs.

**Critical Strategic Insight — The "Demonstration Selection" Problem**:
The choice of demonstrations is **not neutral**. Research (Brown et al., 2020; Min et al., 2022) shows:
- *Diversity* of demonstrations $>$ *Quantity* of demonstrations.
- *Order* of demonstrations matters: placing the most similar example **last** (closest to $x_{\text{new}}$) typically improves accuracy.
- *Correct labels* matter more than *label diversity* — even random labels can sometimes help (showing the model learns format, not just content).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**: In-Context Learning (ICL) is **not** gradient-based learning. The LLM's weights $W$ remain frozen; "learning" is implemented via the attention mechanism's key-value (KV) cache during the forward pass only.

### 2.1.3 Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting

**Definition** (Wei et al., 2022): CoT elicits **intermediate reasoning steps** before the final answer, mimicking human deliberative cognition.

**Two Variants**:
- **Few-Shot CoT**: Demonstrations include `Reasoning: ...` traces.
- **Zero-Shot CoT**: The magic trigger phrase *"Let's think step by step."* (Kojima et al., 2022).

**Mathematical Intuition**:
CoT increases the *effective computation depth* allocated to the problem. The model generates a reasoning chain $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_m$ before emitting the final answer $y$:

$$P(y \mid x) = \sum_{r_1, \ldots, r_m} P(y \mid r_m, x) \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(r_j \mid r_{<j}, x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CoT Works (Intuition)**: The autoregressive factorization above shows that *each step depends on all previous steps*. By making steps explicit, the model forces itself to commit to intermediate facts, reducing the "jumping-to-conclusion" failure mode. It is analogous to **forcing a student to show scratch work on an exam**.

## 2.2 Reasoning Augmentation Techniques

### 2.2.1 Self-Consistency (Wang et al., 2022)

**Mechanism**: Sample $N$ independent CoT reasoning paths (typically $N = 5$ to $40$) using high temperature, then take a **majority vote** over the final answers.

**Decision Rule**:
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i = y]$$

where $\mathbb{1}$ is the indicator function and $y_i$ is the final answer from path $i$.

**Strategic Trade-off**:
- ✅ Improves accuracy on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning by **5–15%**.
- ❌ Cost multiplies by $N$ (latency and token cost increase $N$-fold).
- **Use when**: Accuracy is critical and budget allows.

### 2.2.2 ReAct (Reason + Act) — Yao et al., 2022

**Mechanism**: Interleaves three component types in a loop:
- **Thought** $t_i$: Natural language reasoning step.
- **Action** $a_i$: A call to an external tool (search, calculator, API).
- **Observation** $o_i$: The tool's return value.

**Formal Trajectory**:
$$\tau = (t_1, a_1, o_1, t_2, a_2, o_2, \ldots, t_m, a_m, o_m)$$

**Strategic Justification**:
ReAct overcomes the **knowledge-cutoff** and **arithmetic-weakness** limitations of pure LLMs by offloading computation to specialized tools. It is the **foundational pattern for modern LLM Agents**.

### 2.2.3 Tree of Thoughts (ToT) — Yao et al., 2023

**Mechanism**: Generalizes CoT from a *linear chain* to a **search tree**. At each step, the model generates $b$ candidate "thoughts" (branching factor $b$), evaluates each via a self-critique, and prunes/expands via BFS or DFS.

**Algorithmic Form**:
```
ToT(x, b, d, V):
    Initialize frontier ← {root(x)}
    For step = 1 to d:
        candidates ← []
        For node in frontier:
            thoughts ← Generate(node, b)         # generate b candidates
            scored  ← [(t, V(t)) for t in thoughts]  # evaluate
            candidates.extend(top-k(scored))
        frontier ← best(candidates, b)         # select b for next step
    Return argmax V(node) for node in frontier
```

**Strategic Use Case**: Problems requiring **lookahead and backtracking** — puzzles, strategic games, mathematical proofs.

## 2.3 Advanced Control Strategies

### 2.3.1 Role Prompting (Persona Assignment)

**Mechanism**: Prefix the prompt with a *role descriptor* that conditions the model's stylistic and lexical distribution.

**Template**:
```
You are a [ROLE] with [EXPERTISE]. Your task is to [TASK].
Constraints: [CONSTRAINTS].
Output format: [FORMAT].
```

**Theoretical Basis**: Role tokens bias the attention heads toward *domain-specific* linguistic patterns. A "pediatrician" role produces more cautious, child-safety-aware completions than an unrestricted role.

### 2.3.2 Prompt Chaining (Sequential Decomposition)

**Mechanism**: Decompose a complex task into $k$ sequential sub-tasks, each with its own prompt, where the output of step $i$ becomes the input to step $i+1$.

**Formal Pipeline**:
$$y_k = f_k(f_{k-1}(\ldots f_1(x) \ldots))$$

**Strategic Advantage**: Each sub-prompt has a **narrower context**, reducing hallucination and improving verifiability (you can inspect intermediate outputs).

### 2.3.3 Meta-Prompting

**Mechanism**: Use an LLM to *generate or critique prompts* for another (or the same) LLM.

**Two Patterns**:
- **Generation Pattern**: `LLM_A("Generate 5 prompt variations for task T.") → LLM_B executes them → select best`.
- **Critique Pattern**: `LLM_A(answer) → LLM_B("Critique this answer for [criteria]. Suggest improvements.") → Loop`.

### 2.3.4 Negative Prompting

**Mechanism**: Explicitly instruct the model on **what NOT to do**.

**Template**:
```
Do NOT: [list forbidden behaviors]
Do NOT use: [forbidden vocabulary]
Do NOT include: [forbidden content]
```

**Strategic Caveat**: Negative prompts are **weaker** than positive instructions. Always pair them with an explicit *positive* directive.

### 2.3.5 Structured Output Strategies (JSON/XML/Markdown)

**Mechanism**: Force the model to emit output in a **machine-parseable schema**.

**Example**:
```
Respond ONLY in valid JSON matching this schema:
{
  "answer": <string>,
  "confidence": <float 0-1>,
  "sources": <array of strings>
}
```

**Strategic Value**: Eliminates post-processing fragility; enables direct integration with downstream software pipelines.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all key formulas, parameters, and strategic rules required for KTU Module 2 examination answers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Parameters | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Autoregressive Decoding | $P(y \mid x) = \prod_{t} P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$ | $N$ = output length | All LLM inference |
| 2 | Temperature Scaling | $P_T(y_t) = \frac{\exp(z_t / T)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / T)}$ | $T \in [0,2]$; $T \to 0$ deterministic, $T \to \infty$ uniform | Creativity control |
| 3 | Top-$p$ Nucleus Sampling | $S_p = \min\{ S : \sum_{y \in S} P(y) \geq p \}$ | $p \in (0,1]$, typically $0.9$–$0.95$ | Truncate low-prob tail |
| 4 | Top-$k$ Sampling | $S_k$ = set of $k$ highest-probability tokens | $k \in [1, 50]$ | Hard cutoff variant |
| 5 | Few-Shot ICL | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y P(y \mid \bigoplus_i (x_i, y_i) \oplus x_{\text{new}})$ | $k$ = # demonstrations | Format + pattern learning |
| 6 | CoT Reasoning Depth | $P(y \mid x) = \sum_{r_1..r_m} P(y \mid r_m, x) \prod_j P(r_j \mid r_{<j}, x)$ | $m$ = # reasoning steps | Multi-step reasoning |
| 7 | Self-Consistency Vote | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i = y]$ | $N \in [5, 40]$ | Arithmetic, logic |
| 8 | ReAct Trajectory | $\tau = (t_1, a_1, o_1, \ldots, t_m, a_m, o_m)$ | $m$ = iterations | Tool-augmented agents |
| 9 | ToT Branching | Frontier size $= b$ at each depth $d$ | $b$ = branching factor | Search-intensive tasks |
| 10 | Prompt Chain | $y_k = f_k \circ f_{k-1} \circ \ldots \circ f_1(x)$ | $k$ = # stages | Complex decomposition |
| 11 | Context Budget | $B = \vert x_{\text{sys}} \vert + \sum \vert x_{\text{demo}, i} \vert + \vert x_{\text{new}} \vert + \vert y \vert$ | $B \leq C$ (context window) | Token budgeting |
| 12 | Repetition Penalty | $P'(y_t) = P(y_t) / \theta$ if $y_t \in y_{<t}$, else $P(y_t)$ | $\theta > 1$ (typically $1.1$–$1.3$) | Avoid loops |
| 13 | Stop Sequences | Generation halts when model emits token $s \in S_{\text{stop}}$ | $S_{\text{stop}}$ = stop set | Output boundary control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: These formulas underpin the *hyperparameter dashboards* of LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google AI Studio). A prompt engineer who understands these can *defend* their design choices quantitatively in viva voce examinations — a frequent KTU oral-evaluation question pattern.

## 2.5 The "Technique Selection Decision Tree" — A Strategic Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently pose the question: *"Given a task X, which technique would you choose and why?"* The following decision tree is the **model answer template**.

```
START: Given task X
    │
    ├─ Is X simple and unambiguous?
    │       YES → Zero-Shot (cheapest, fastest)
    │       NO  ↓
    │
    ├─ Does X require a specific output FORMAT or STYLE?
    │       YES → Few-Shot (provide 3-5 format demonstrations)
    │       NO  ↓
    │
    ├─ Does X require MULTI-STEP reasoning (math, logic, planning)?
    │       YES → Is X a single-pass problem or a search problem?
    │           ├─ Single-pass → CoT or Zero-Shot CoT
    │           └─ Search (lookahead/backtrack) → ToT
    │           └─ Need tool access (web, calculator, DB) → ReAct
    │       NO  ↓
    │
    ├─ Is X part of a LARGER WORKFLOW (multi-stage pipeline)?
    │       YES → Prompt Chaining
    │       NO  ↓
    │
    └─ Is X a META-task (e.g., "design a prompt for Y")?
            YES → Meta-Prompting
            NO  → Default: Zero-Shot + Role Prompting + Structured Output
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Derivations

### 3.2.1 Derivation — Why Temperature Reshapes Output Distribution

**Given**: A model produces logits $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_V$ over vocabulary $V$ for the next token.

**Step 1 — Base Softmax (Cold Model, $T = 1$)**:
$$P(y_t = i) = \frac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Temperature Scaling**:
The temperature parameter $T$ rescales the logits before softmax:
$$P_T(y_t = i) = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / T)}$$

**Step 3 — Analyze the Limit $T \to 0$**:
As $T \to 0$, the term $\exp(z_i / T)$ is dominated by the **largest logit** $z_{\max} = \max_j z_j$. All other terms vanish exponentially faster. Therefore:
$$\lim_{T \to 0} P_T(y_t = i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z_i = z_{\max} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This is the **greedy decoding** regime — fully deterministic.

**Step 4 — Analyze the Limit $T \to \infty$**:
By L'Hôpital's rule or Taylor expansion, $\exp(z_i / T) \to 1 + z_i / T + \mathcal{O}(T^{-2})$. All terms become approximately equal, so:
$$\lim_{T \to \infty} P_T(y_t = i) = \frac{1}{V}$$

This is the **uniform distribution** — maximally random.

**Step 5 — Interpret the Entropy**:
The Shannon entropy of the temperature-scaled distribution is:
$$H_T = -\sum_{i=1}^{V} P_T(i) \log P_T(i)$$

$H_T$ is **monotonically increasing in $T$** for $T \in (0, \infty)$. This rigorously proves: *higher temperature = more diverse/random outputs*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Takeaway**: For a KTU answer on sampling parameters, citing the entropy-monotonicity result demonstrates analytical depth and earns full marks.

### 3.2.2 Derivation — Self-Consistency Variance Reduction

**Claim**: Self-Consistency reduces the variance of the final answer estimator.

**Setup**:
- Let $Y$ be the random variable for the final answer produced by a single CoT sample.
- Assume each sample is i.i.d. with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.
- The single-sample estimator is $\hat{Y}_{\text{single}} = Y$, with $\mathrm{Var}(\hat{Y}_{\text{single}}) = \sigma^2$.

**Step 1 — Define the Majority-Vote Estimator**:
For $N$ i.i.d. samples $Y_1, \ldots, Y_N$, the majority vote selects:
$$\hat{Y}_{\text{SC}} = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[Y_i = y]$$

**Step 2 — Upper Bound on Error Probability**:
By the Chernoff bound (for $|p - 0.5| \geq \delta$):
$$P(\hat{Y}_{\text{SC}} \neq y^*) \leq \exp(-2 N \delta^2)$$

where $y^*$ is the true answer and $\delta$ is the margin by which the correct answer's probability exceeds the runner-up's.

**Step 3 — Asymptotic Behavior**:
The error probability decays **exponentially** in $N$, justifying the use of moderate $N$ (e.g., $N = 11$) rather than large $N$.

**Step 4 — Practical Bound (KTU Heuristic)**:
For $N = 11$ (odd to break ties) and $\delta = 0.1$:
$$P(\text{error}) \leq \exp(-2 \times 11 \times 0.01) = \exp(-0.22) \approx 0.80$$

This is a loose bound; in practice, margins are larger and accuracy gains are substantial.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: When asked *"Why odd $N$ in Self-Consistency?"*, the answer is: *to avoid tied majority votes, which would force an arbitrary tiebreaker and reintroduce variance*. The odd $N$ rule is **non-negotiable** in practice.

### 3.2.3 Derivation — Context Window Budget Allocation

**Problem**: Given context window $C$ tokens, optimally allocate budget across system prompt, demonstrations, and user query.

**Setup**:
- System prompt: $S$ tokens.
- $k$ demonstrations, each of length $D_i$ tokens (input + output).
- User query: $Q$ tokens.
- Reserved for response: $R$ tokens.

**Constraint**:
$$S + \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i + Q + R \leq C$$

**Step 1 — Strategic Allocation Principles**:
- **System prompt**: Should be **stable** and **reusable**; keep $S \ll C$ (typically 5–10% of $C$).
- **Demonstrations**: Use the **diversity-maximizing** subset. Each demo is precious — choose 3–5 high-quality ones, not 20 mediocre ones.
- **User query**: Reserve the **last** position (most recent tokens have the strongest attention weight in most models).
- **Response budget**: Allocate $R \geq$ expected output length + 20% safety margin.

**Step 2 — Concrete Example**:
For $C = 8000$ (a typical GPT-3.5-turbo context):

$$8000 = 500_{\text{sys}} + (4 \times 800)_{\text{demos}} + 500_{\text{query}} + 3300_{\text{response}}$$

$$8000 = 500 + 3200 + 500 + 3300 = 7500 \leq 8000 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Truncation Strategy**:
If the budget is exceeded, truncate **oldest demonstrations first** (models generally attend more strongly to recent tokens due to positional encoding decay, though exact behavior depends on the architecture).

## 3.3 Comparative Example — Same Task, Four Techniques

**Task**: *"Classify the sentiment of this product review: 'The phone arrived on time and works perfectly, but the charger feels flimsy.'"*

**Technique 1 — Zero-Shot**:
```
Classify the sentiment of this review as POSITIVE, NEGATIVE, or NEUTRAL.
Review: "The phone arrived on time and works perfectly, but the charger feels flimsy."
Sentiment:
```
*Expected output*: `NEUTRAL` (probable, but format may vary).

**Technique 2 — Few-Shot (3 demonstrations)**:
```
Review: "Best purchase ever!" → POSITIVE
Review: "Total waste of money." → NEGATIVE
Review: "It's fine, does the job." → NEUTRAL
Review: "The phone arrived on time and works perfectly, but the charger feels flimsy." →
```
*Expected output*: `NEUTRAL` (high format reliability).

**Technique 3 — CoT**:
```
Review: "The phone arrived on time and works perfectly, but the charger feels flimsy."
Let's think step by step.
1. Positive aspects: on-time delivery, works perfectly.
2. Negative aspects: charger feels flimsy.
3. Net sentiment: mixed → NEUTRAL.
Answer: NEUTRAL
```

**Technique 4 — Structured Output (JSON)**:
```
Classify sentiment. Output JSON: {"sentiment": "<POS|NEG|NEU>", "aspects": [...]}
Review: "The phone arrived on time and works perfectly, but the charger feels flimsy."
```
*Expected output*: `{"sentiment": "NEU", "aspects": ["delivery", "functionality", "charger"]}`.

## 4.4 Tree of Thoughts Search Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root: Initial State] --> B1[Thought 1.1]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    O1 --> P2[Stage 2 Prompt]
    P2 --> O2[Output y2]

    P3 --> O3[Output y3]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: Differentiate between Zero-Shot and Few-Shot prompting with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **Zero-Shot Prompting (1.5 marks)**: The model is given only the task instruction with no worked examples. It relies entirely on its pre-trained knowledge. *Example*: "Translate to French: Hello, how are you?"
- **Few-Shot Prompting (1.5 marks)**: The model is conditioned on $k \geq 2$ input-output demonstrations before the actual query, enabling in-context learning. *Example*: "English: Hello → French: Bonjour. English: Thank you → French: Merci. English: Good morning → French:"

**Key Distinction**: Zero-shot uses *no* examples; Few-shot uses *multiple* examples to establish pattern, format, or reasoning style.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: What is Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting? State the Zero-Shot CoT trigger phrase.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **Definition (2 marks)**: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting is a technique that elicits intermediate reasoning steps from an LLM before producing the final answer, improving performance on multi-step reasoning tasks (arithmetic, commonsense, symbolic logic). It works by decomposing the inference into explicit steps that the model commits to sequentially.
- **Zero-Shot CoT Trigger (1 mark)**: The canonical trigger phrase is *"Let's think step by step."* (Kojima et al., 2022). When appended to a query, it activates CoT reasoning without requiring any demonstrations.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern A]

**Q: Explain in detail the following prompt engineering techniques with suitable examples:**
**(a) Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting (7 marks)**
**(b) Self-Consistency and Tree of Thoughts (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Chain-of-Thought Prompting (7 marks)

**Model Answer**:

**Definition (2 marks)**: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, introduced by Wei et al. (2022), is a technique that conditions an LLM to generate intermediate reasoning steps before arriving at a final answer. It transforms a "single-shot" inference into a multi-step deliberative process.

**Mechanism (2 marks)**:
1. The prompt contains either (i) demonstrations with explicit reasoning traces (Few-Shot CoT) or (ii) a trigger phrase like "Let's think step by step" (Zero-Shot CoT).
2. The model autoregressively generates a reasoning chain $r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_m$ where each $r_i$ depends on all previous steps.
3. The final answer $y$ is produced conditioned on the complete chain.

**Mathematical Form (1 mark)**:
$$P(y \mid x) = \sum_{r_1, \ldots, r_m} P(y \mid r_m, x) \prod_{j=1}^{m} P(r_j \mid r_{<j}, x)$$

**Example (1 mark)**:
- *Query*: "Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans of 3. How many does he have now?"
- *CoT Response*: "Roger started with 5 balls. 2 cans of 3 = 6 balls. 5 + 6 = 11. Answer: 11."

**Advantages (1 mark)**: Improves accuracy on arithmetic, commonsense, and multi-hop reasoning by 10–30% over zero-shot baselines.

#### Part (b) — Self-Consistency and Tree of Thoughts (7 marks)

**Model Answer**:

**Self-Consistency (3.5 marks)**:
- **Definition**: Wang et al. (2022) — sample $N$ independent CoT reasoning paths and take a majority vote over final answers.
- **Algorithm**:
  1. Set temperature $T > 0$ (typically $0.7$–$1.0$) to encourage diverse paths.
  2. Generate $N$ CoT samples (odd $N$ recommended, e.g., $N = 11$).
  3. Extract the final answer from each sample.
  4. Return $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i = y]$.
- **Strategic Trade-off**: Cost multiplies by $N$; accuracy gain is 5–15% over single CoT.

**Tree of Thoughts (3.5 marks)**:
- **Definition**: Yao et al. (2023) — generalizes CoT from a linear chain to a search tree with explicit branching, evaluation, and backtracking.
- **Components**:
  - **Thought decomposition**: Each step generates $b$ candidate thoughts.
  - **Evaluator** $V(\cdot)$: Scores each thought (often the LLM itself via self-evaluation).
  - **Search algorithm**: BFS or DFS over the tree.
- **Parameters**: Branching factor $b$, depth limit $d$, evaluation function $V$.
- **Use Cases**: Puzzles (Game of 24), mathematical proofs, strategic games requiring lookahead.

**Comparison Table (within the 7-mark answer)**:

| Aspect | Self-Consistency | Tree of Thoughts |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Parallel paths | Tree with branches |
| Search | None (independent samples) | BFS/DFS with pruning |
| Cost | $N \times$ CoT | $b^d \times$ CoT |
| Use Case | Single-answer reasoning | Multi-step search |

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern B]

**Q: Discuss the following prompt engineering strategies with examples:**
**(a) Role Prompting and Prompt Chaining (7 marks)**
**(b) ReAct Framework and Meta-Prompting (7 marks)**
