**With guardrails** (assuming guardrails catch 80% of failures and trigger retry with 95% per-retry success):

$$ P_{\text{per-step, with guardrail}} = 0.95 + (1 - 0.95) \times 0.80 \times 0.95 \approx 0.988 $$

$$ P_{\text{end-to-end, with guardrails}} = 0.988^4 \approx 0.952 \;\; (95.2\%) $$

**Conclusion:** Guardrails improve end-to-end reliability from 73% to 95% — a **22 percentage-point uplift** — while only adding **2 extra LLM calls on average** per failed step. The ROI is strongly positive for any production system.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> *Explain Iterative Prompting with a worked example. Discuss (a) the critique-mutate loop, and (b) convergence criteria, including how to prevent "evaluator overfitting" in LLM-as-a-Judge setups.*

#### **Part (a) — The Critique-Mutate Loop (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Iterative prompting is the process of repeatedly invoking an LLM on a task, **modifying the prompt** based on the quality of prior outputs, until a stop condition is met.

**The Loop (4 marks):**

1. **Initialize** $P^{(0)}$ with a baseline prompt.
2. **Invoke** $Y^{(k)} = \text{LLM}(P^{(k)})$.
3. **Critique** using one of: (i) human review, (ii) LLM-as-a-Judge, (iii) programmatic checks.
4. **Mutate** $P^{(k+1)} = \Phi(P^{(k)}, \text{Critique}(Y^{(k)}))$ — typically adding constraints, few-shot examples, or persona.
5. **Repeat** until stop condition.

**Worked Example (2 marks):** Generating a *100-word* product tagline for a smartwatch targeting seniors.

- $P^{(0)} =$ *"Write a tagline for our senior-friendly smartwatch."*
- $Y^{(0)} =$ *"The smartwatch that does everything!"* — **Critique:** too generic, 5 words, no senior-specificity. $Q=0.2$.
- $P^{(1)} =$ *"Write a 100-word tagline for a smartwatch with large buttons, fall detection, and heart-rate alerts, targeting users 60+."*
- $Y^{(1)} =$ *"Stay connected, stay safe..."* (98 words, senior-specific). $Q=0.85$.
- $P^{(2)} = P^{(1)} \oplus$ *"Tone: warm, reassuring. Avoid: technical jargon, exclamation marks."* + 2 few-shot examples.
- $Y^{(2)} =$ (99 words, warm tone, no jargon). $Q=0.96 \geq 0.9$. **STOP.**

#### **Part (b) — Convergence Criteria & Evaluator Overfitting (7 marks)**

**Convergence Criteria (3 marks):** A robust iterative loop uses **one or both** of:

1. **Quality Threshold:** $\text{Score}(Y^{(k)}) \geq \tau$
2. **Diminishing Returns:** $\vert Q^{(k)} - Q^{(k-1)} \vert < \epsilon$
3. **Hard Cap:** $k \geq K_{\max}$ (safety net)
4. **Cost Budget:** $C_{\text{accumulated}} \leq C_{\max}$

**Evaluator Overfitting — The Hidden Trap (4 marks):**

When using an **LLM-as-a-Judge** (a second LLM scoring the first), a subtle failure mode emerges: the prompts converge to *what the judge rewards* rather than *what is genuinely good*. Symptoms:

- Outputs become **verbally polished but factually shallow** (judges reward fluency).
- Outputs **systematically share biases** with the judge (e.g., both prefer longer answers).
- Improvement plateaus at a *locally optimal* point that is globally mediocre.

**Mitigations:**
1. **Multiple independent judges** — average scores from $J \geq 3$ LLMs of different families.
2. **Human spot-checks** on a $5$–$10\%$ sample of iterations.
3. **Diverse evaluation rubrics** — score on *factuality, brevity, helpfulness* independently, then take a weighted sum.
4. **Held-out test set** — never let the judge see the test cases during mutation.
5. **Refreshing the judge prompt** — periodically rotate the rubric to prevent the writer from exploiting a static weakness.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** "Evaluator overfitting" is a **favorite 7-mark sub-question** in Module 2 because it tests both *technical* (iterative loops) and *ethical/robustness* awareness — exactly the NEP 2020 outcome-based emphasis.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Prompt Chaining** = DAG of distinct LLM calls; **Iterative Prompting** = loop on one call. *Always state the topology first in exams.*
- ✅ A chain **must** have: (1) explicit step decomposition, (2) state-passing mechanism, (3) at least one **guardrail**, (4) termination.
- ✅ **Error propagation in a linear chain:** $P_{\text{success}} = \prod p_i$ (NOT $1 - \prod(1-p_i)$).
- ✅ Iterative loop termination: quality threshold $\tau$, diminishing returns $\epsilon$, or max iterations $K_{\max}$.
- ✅ **ReAct** is a cyclic chain: $(Thought, Action, Observation)$ loop.
- ✅ The **mutation function** $\Phi$ can adjust: few-shot examples, constraints, persona, sampling parameters.
- ✅ Diminishing returns typically appear after $K=3$–$5$ iterations — do not over-iterate.
- ✅ **Evaluator overfitting** is the #1 risk in LLM-as-a-Judge iterative setups — use multiple judges, human spot-checks, and diverse rubrics.
- ✅ Chains can **contain** iterative loops inside individual steps — this hybrid is the production-standard architecture.
- ✅ KTU 2024 ethical principle: **"AI augments, humans authorize"** — every production prompt pipeline should have a human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hands-on: Crafting and optimizing prompts for specific tasks (e.g., text generation, summarization, Q&A)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hands-on: Crafting and optimizing prompts for specific tasks (e.g., text generation, summarization, Q&A) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Prompt Engineering (Hands-on Application Layer)** is the iterative, empirical discipline of designing, structuring, and refining natural-language instructions (prompts) directed at Large Language Models (LLMs) to elicit high-fidelity outputs for **task-specific objectives** such as text generation, abstractive/extractive summarization, and question-answering (Q&A) systems. In the KTU 2024 framework, hands-on prompt engineering emphasizes the **closed-loop optimization cycle** — Design → Execute → Measure → Refine — aligning prompt architecture with measurable model parameters and task constraints. A **task-specific prompt** is a structured object composed of four canonical components, often called the **R-I-I-O** schema: 1. **R**ole — Persona / system context assigned to the model. 2. **I**nstruction — The explicit directive or task description. 3. **I**nput — The data, context, or query payload. 4. **O**utput Indicator — The format, schema, or stopping criteria. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The hands-on component explicitly requires students to demonstrate competence in *crafting* and *optimizing* prompts across **three task archetypes** — text generation, summarization, and Q&A. Memorizing abstract theory is insufficient; you must be able to show iterative refinement with measurable improvement. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Think of prompting an LLM like **briefing a brilliant but extremely literal new intern on their very first day**. - The intern has read every book in the library, but has **no common sense** about *your* office, *your* preferences, or *what "good" looks like for you today*. - If you say *"Summarize this report,"* the intern will hand back *something* — but it might be 5 lines, 50 lines, a bullet list, or a paragraph. You wanted bullets, under 100 words, focused on financial risk. - The better the **briefing** (prompt) — context, examples, constraints, format — the closer the output matches your actual need. **The "crafting" stage** is the briefing. **The "optimization" stage** is the second-day conversation: *"Your summary was too long, missed the risks, and used passive voice — try again with these tweaks."* The intern (LLM) doesn't *learn* permanently, but with a sharper brief, the third attempt is usually publishable. > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** LLMs are stateless function approximators. Each call is a *fresh intern*. Optimization is therefore applied to the *prompt artifact*, not to the model. This is why prompt engineering is engineering, not conversation. ## 1.3 Core Metrics (Bolded for Recall) The three **first-class metrics** that govern task-specific prompt quality in KTU assessments are: - **Faithfulness** — degree to which output is grounded in the provided input/context. - **Conciseness** — information density per output token (target ratio ≈ **0.6–0.8** for summarization). - **Task Fidelity** — percentage of generations that satisfy the explicit instruction on first pass (target ≥ **85%** for production prompts). The three **generation-control parameters** (model-side knobs) you must know are: - **Temperature (T)** ∈ [0, 2] — controls output randomness. - **Top-p (nucleus sampling)** ∈ [0, 1] — cumulative probability mass cutoff. - **Top-k** ∈ ℕ — restricts sampling to the k highest-probability tokens. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Task Archetypes — Structural Anatomy ### 2.1.1 Text Generation Prompts Text generation is **open-ended continuation**. The prompt must constrain an otherwise unbounded output space along the axes of *topic, tone, length, and structure*. **Optimization levers specific to text generation:** - **Temperature tuning**: $T \downarrow$ (e.g., $T=0.2$) → deterministic, conservative. $T \uparrow$ (e.g., $T=1.0$) → creative, diverse. - **Top-p narrowing**: $p = 0.9$ typically produces fluent-yet-varied prose. - **Length budget**: explicit token or sentence ceiling in the prompt. - **Few-shot exemplars**: 2–3 worked examples dramatically improve stylistic alignment. ### 2.1.2 Summarization Prompts Summarization is **input-constrained compression**. The prompt must specify: - **Compression ratio target** (e.g., 1:10). - **Focus axis** (e.g., financial, technical, action items). - **Extractive vs. abstractive** preference. - **Output schema** (prose, bullets, JSON). **Faithfulness pitfall:** abstractive summarization without source-pinning can hallucinate. Mitigation = instruct the model to *"quote or paraphrase ONLY from the provided text; if not present, write 'Not in source.'"* ### 2.1.3 Question-Answering (Q&A) Prompts Q&A is **retrieval-then-synthesis**. The prompt architecture is: 1. **Context block** (retrieved passages). 2. **Question block** (user query). 3. **Answer-format block** (e.g., "Respond in 1–2 sentences. Cite the source paragraph number."). 4. **Refusal clause** (e.g., "If answer is not in the context, reply: 'I don't know.'"). **Q&A optimization rule of thumb:** Place the question *after* the context, not before — modern instruction-tuned models are trained on this ordering and lose ~15–20% accuracy when reversed. ## 2.2 The Optimization Loop (Design → Measure → Refine) A prompt is **never finished on the first draft**. The KTU-mandated optimization loop is: 1. **Design** — write initial prompt using R-I-I-O schema. 2. **Execute** — run on a held-out evaluation set of **N ≥ 10** test cases. 3. **Measure** — compute Faithfulness, Conciseness, Task Fidelity scores. 4. **Diagnose** — bucket failures (omission, hallucination, format, tone). 5. **Refine** — apply targeted edit (add example, tighten constraint, adjust T). 6. **Re-execute** — re-measure. Stop when target metric reached OR diminishing returns (Δ < 2% over 2 iterations). ## 2.3 KTU Formula / Parameter Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. Every cell is a potential short-answer or full-mark question. | Parameter / Concept | Symbol | Formula or Range | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Token estimate (English) | $\hat{t}$ | $\hat{t} \approx 1.33 \times w$ | $w$ = word count | | Token estimate (Code) | $\hat{t}_c$ | $\hat{t}_c \approx 0.75 \times c$ | $c$ = characters | | Context window (typical) | $C_w$ | $C_w \in \{8\text{K}, 32\text{K}, 128\text{K}, 1\text{M}\}$ | tokens | | Effective context | $C_{eff}$ | $C_{eff} \approx 0.8 \times C_w$ | tokens (safety margin) | | Temperature | $T$ | $T \in [0, 2]$ | dimensionless | | Top-p (nucleus) | $p$ | $p \in [0, 1]$, default $0.9$ | dimensionless | | Top-k | $k$ | $k \in \mathbb{N}$, default $40$ | tokens | | Compression ratio | $R_c$ | $R_c = \dfrac{\vert O \vert}{\vert I \vert}$ | $\vert O \vert$ = output length, $\vert I \vert$ = input length | | Target ratio (summarization) | $R_c^{\ast}$ | $R_c^{\ast} \in [0.05, 0.20]$ | typical abstractive summary | | Per-call cost | $\mathcal{C}$ | $\mathcal{C} = \dfrac{(t_{in} \cdot p_{in} + t_{out} \cdot p_{out})}{1000}$ | USD | | Task Fidelity | $\mathcal{F}$ | $\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{N_{pass}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | percent | | Hallucination rate | $\mathcal{H}$ | $\mathcal{H} = \dfrac{N_{halluc}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (lower is better) | | Information density | $\rho$ | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{unique facts}}{t_{out}}$ | facts / token | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | Critical Prompt Feature | |---|---|---| | Healthcare documentation | Clinical note summarization | Faithfulness constraint + refusal clause | | Legal tech | Contract clause Q&A | Citation requirement + scope delimiter | | Software engineering | Code generation | Few-shot examples + test-case self-check | | Customer support | Ticket classification | JSON output schema + low temperature | | Education | Adaptive tutoring | Role prompt + chain-of-thought | | Finance | Earnings-call summarization | Numeric preservation + ratio constraint | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU valuation, **showing you can defend a parameter choice** (e.g., *"I set $T=0.3$ because the task is summarization, which requires high faithfulness — high $T$ would risk hallucination"*) scores higher than just listing parameter values. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Token Budget Derivation for a Q&A Prompt **Problem.** A legal Q&A system must ingest a 4,200-word contract. The system uses an LLM with $C_w = 8{,}000$ tokens. We need to derive the **maximum safe word count of the user question** after reserving space for: (a) system prompt (180 tokens), (b) answer buffer (250 tokens), and (c) 20% safety margin. **Step 1 — Convert input words to tokens.** Using the English estimate $\hat{t} \approx 1.33 \times w$: $$ \hat{t}_{contract} = 1.33 \times 4200 = 5586 \text{ tokens} $$ **Step 2 — Subtract non-input reservations.** $$ t_{available} = C_w - t_{system} - t_{answer} = 8000 - 180 - 250 = 7570 \text{ tokens} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the 20% safety margin (so we operate at $C_{eff}$).** $$ t_{usable} = 0.80 \times t_{available} = 0.80 \times 7570 = 6056 \text{ tokens} $$ **Step 4 — Derive remaining headroom for the question.** $$ t_{question}^{max} = t_{usable} - \hat{t}_{contract} = 6056 - 5586 = 470 \text{ tokens} $$ **Step 5 — Convert back to words.** $$ w_{question}^{max} = \dfrac{t_{question}^{max}}{1.33} = \dfrac{470}{1.33} \approx 353 \text{ words} $$ > **Conclusion.** The user's question may be **at most ~353 words** in this configuration. If longer questions are anticipated, either upgrade to a 32K-token model, perform **retrieval chunking** first, or **summarize the contract** in a pre-pass and feed the summary instead. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost Calculation for a Summarization Pipeline **Problem.** A news-aggregation service summarizes 10,000 articles/day. Average article = 800 words. Target summary length = 60 words. Input price $p_{in} = \$0.0005 / 1K$ tokens, output price $p_{out} = \$0.0015 / 1K$ tokens. Derive daily and monthly cost. **Step 1 — Tokens per article (input).** $$ t_{in} = 1.33 \times 800 = 1064 \text{ tokens/article} $$ **Step 2 — Tokens per summary (output).** $$ t_{out} = 1.33 \times 60 = 79.8 \approx 80 \text{ tokens} $$ **Step 3 — Cost per article.** $$ \mathcal{C}_{article} = \dfrac{(1064 \times 0.0005 + 80 \times 0.0015)}{1} = 0.000532 + 0.000120 = \$0.000652 $$ **Step 4 — Daily cost.** $$ \mathcal{C}_{daily} = 10000 \times 0.000652 = \$6.52 $$ **Step 5 — Monthly cost (30 days).** $$ \mathcal{C}_{monthly} = 30 \times 6.52 = \$195.60 $$ **Step 6 — Optimization insight.** If we cache the article embeddings and use **extractive-then-abstractive** two-pass summarization, we can cut input tokens by ~60% (send only the top-3 relevant paragraphs, not the full article). New daily cost: $$ \mathcal{C}_{daily}^{opt} = 10000 \times \left( \dfrac{0.40 \times 1064 \times 0.0005 + 80 \times 0.0015}{1} \right) \approx \$3.35 $$ > **Savings ≈ 48.6% per day** — a figure you should *always* present in KTU optimization answers to show engineering maturity. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Iterative Prompt Refinement (Summarization) We will now walk through **three iterations** of a summarization prompt, showing measurable improvement at each step. ### Iteration 0 — Naïve prompt ``` Summarize this article. ``` **Observed failures** (measured on N=10 test articles): | Failure Type | Count | Rate | |---|---|---| | Too long (>250 words) | 7 | 70% | | Missing key fact | 4 | 40% | | Hallucinated detail | 3 | 30% | | Wrong format (prose vs. bullets) | 6 | 60% | $\mathcal{F} = 20\%$. ### Iteration 1 — Add constraints ``` Summarize the following article in exactly 3 bullet points, each ≤25 words. Focus on the financial implications. ARTICLE: {input} ``` **Result:** $\mathcal{F} = 60\%$. Length and format fixed. Hallucination still present. ### Iteration 2 — Add faithfulness clause + role ``` You are a financial-analyst assistant. Summarize the following article in exactly 3 bullet points, each ≤25 words. Focus on financial implications. Rules: - Use ONLY facts stated in the article. - If a financial figure is not mentioned, write "N/A". - Do not add external context. ARTICLE: {input} SUMMARY: ``` **Result:** $\mathcal{F} = 90\%$, $\mathcal{H} = 0\%$. **Acceptable for production.** > [!NOTE] > Notice the **3 changes** that drove the final jump: (1) role assignment, (2) explicit refusal/uncertainty clause, (3) separator token (`SUMMARY:`) that primes the model for output. KTU examiners reward this kind of *post-mortem diagnosis* in 14-mark answers. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Technique for Which Task?" | Task | Primary Technique | Temperature | Few-Shot? | CoT? | Format Pinning | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Creative story generation | Open-ended + role | $0.8$–$1.0$ | Optional | No | Weak | | Email drafting | Role + tone constraint | $0.5$–$0.7$ | 1 example | No | Medium | | Extractive summary | "Quote 3 sentences" | $0.2$ | No | No | Strong | | Abstractive summary | Role + ratio + faithfulness | $0.3$ | 1 example | No | Strong | | Factual Q&A (closed-book) | System prompt + low $T$ | $0.1$–$0.2$ | No | Sometimes | Strong | | Factual Q&A (RAG) | Context-pinned + refusal | $0.0$–$0.2$ | No | Optional | Strong | | Multi-step reasoning Q&A | Chain-of-thought | $0.0$ | 2–3 examples | **Yes** | Medium | | Code generation | Few-shot + test cases | $0.0$–$0.2$ | **2–3 examples** | No | Strong | | Classification | JSON schema + low $T$ | $0.0$ | 3–5 examples | No | **Very strong** | **Key reading rule:** As **task determinism** increases (left → right in many rows), **temperature should decrease** and **format pinning should increase**. This single rule resolves ~60% of optimization decisions. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Canonical Prompt Anatomy (R-I-I-O Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[ROLE BLOCK
System Persona] --> B[INSTRUCTION BLOCK
Task Directive] B --> C[INPUT BLOCK
Data / Context / Query] C --> D[OUTPUT INDICATOR
Format and Stop Criteria] D --> E[LLM INFERENCE ENGINE] E --> F[POST PROCESSING
Parse Validate Filter] F --> G[MEASUREMENT LAYER
Faithfulness Conciseness Fidelity] G --> H{Refine Needed} H -- YES --> A H -- NO --> I[DEPLOY PROMPT ARTIFACT] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The feedback loop from `G` (Measurement) back to `A` (Role) is the **optimization cycle**. Once the metrics plateau (H = NO), the prompt is promoted to a versioned artifact (e.g., `prompt_v1.4.json`) and deployed. --- ## 4.2 Task-Specific Prompt Optimization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[TASK TEXT GENERATION] --> P1[Constrain Tone and Length] P1 --> S1[Sample Temperature Sweep 0.0 0.3 0.7 1.0] S1 --> M1[Score Diversity and Coherence] M1 --> O1[Lock T and Top-p] T2[TASK SUMMARIZATION] --> P2[Set Compression Ratio and Focus] P2 --> S2[Add Faithfulness Clause] S2 --> M2[Score Faithfulness and Coverage] M2 --> O2[Pin Output Schema] T3[TASK QA RAG] --> P3[Inject Retrieved Context First] P3 --> S3[Add Refusal Clause and Citation] S3 --> M3[Score Answer Accuracy and Hallucination] M3 --> O3[Lock Prompt Version] O1 --> Z[PRODUCTION PROMPT LIBRARY] O2 --> Z O3 --> Z ``` --- ## 4.3 Optimization Convergence Curve (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR I0[Iteration 0
F equals 20 percent] --> I1[Iteration 1
Add Constraints
F equals 60 percent] I1 --> I2[Iteration 2
Add Role and Faithfulness
F equals 90 percent] I2 --> I3[Iteration 3
Tune Temperature and Top-p
F equals 93 percent] I3 --> I4[Iteration 4
Diminishing Returns
F equals 93 percent STOP] ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Iteration 3 → 4 shows Δ < 2%. This is the KTU-recommended stopping criterion and must be explicitly stated in answers to demonstrate engineering rigor. --- ## 4.4 Hallucination Mitigation Architecture (Q&A) ```mermaid flowchart TD U[User Question] --> R[Retriever Top K Chunks] R --> CTX[Context Block Construction] CTX --> PROMPT[Prompt Assembly
Context then Question then Format] PROMPT --> LLM[LLM Call T equals 0.1] LLM --> VAL[Validator
Check Answer Is In Context] VAL -- GROUNDED --> OUT[Return Answer with Citation] VAL -- NOT GROUNDED --> REFUSE[Return I do not know] ``` > **Key insight:** The **Validator** is a cheap second LLM call (or even a regex/embedding-similarity check) that catches hallucinations before they reach the user. This is a standard production pattern and is *extremely high-yield* for KTU 14-mark design questions. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **Define a "task-specific prompt" and list its four canonical components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **task-specific prompt** is a natural-language instruction, structured for a particular LLM task archetype (generation, summarization, Q&A), that constrains the model's behavior along defined axes. The four canonical components — the **R-I-I-O schema** — are: 1. **Role** — assigns a persona or system context to the model (e.g., *"You are a tax consultant"*). 2. **Instruction** — the explicit task directive (e.g., *"Summarize the following"*). 3. **Input** — the data, context, or query payload to operate on. 4. **Output Indicator** — the format, schema, length, or stopping criterion (e.g., *"Respond in 3 bullets, ≤25 words each"*). *(Award 0.5 marks per component, 0.5 marks for the introductory sentence; 1 mark reserved for naming the schema as R-I-I-O.)* --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **State any three prompt-optimization techniques that reduce hallucination in a Q&A system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Context-pinning** — explicitly instruct the model to use *only* the provided context block. *(1 mark)* 2. **Refusal clause** — add: *"If the answer is not in the context, reply 'I don't know.'"* *(1 mark)* 3. **Low temperature + grounding validator** — set $T \leq 0.2$ for deterministic output, and run a second pass to verify the answer is semantically supported by the source. *(1 mark)* *(Alternative acceptable techniques: few-shot grounded exemplars, citation requirement, extractive-then-abstractive two-pass design.)* --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** You must answer **one** of the two choices. Each choice has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### Question A (14 Marks). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Sample 2024]` **You are building a customer-support summarization engine for a SaaS company. The system ingests 500-word support tickets and must produce a 50-word summary categorized as {Billing, Technical, Account, Other}.** **(a) [7 marks]** Design and justify a complete prompt for this task. Specify the role, instruction, input handling, output schema, and generation parameters. Justify *each* parameter choice. **(b) [7 marks]** Describe an iterative optimization procedure (at least 3 iterations) you would apply, with the metric you would track at each step. Show how you would know when to stop optimizing. #### Model Solution — (a) **Prompt Design (full marks = 7):** ``` ROLE: You are a senior customer-support triage specialist with 10 years of SaaS experience. You write concise, factual summaries. INSTRUCTION: Read the support ticket below. Produce a summary in EXACTLY this format: Category: {Billing | Technical | Account | Other} Summary: Key Action: RULES: - Use only facts explicitly stated in the ticket. - If category is ambiguous, choose "Other". - Do not invent order IDs, amounts, or dates. TICKET: {input} OUTPUT: ``` **Justifications (each worth 1 mark):** - **Role** assigned → improves tone consistency by ~20% on average. - **Categorical enum** in output → forces classification, reduces ambiguity. - **Length cap** "≤50 words" → directly controls $R_c \approx 0.10$. - **Faithfulness rules** → hallucination reduction. - **Temperature $T = 0.2$** → low randomness appropriate for factual classification + summary. - **Top-p = 0.9, Top-k = 40** → balance of determinism and fluency. - **Separator token** `OUTPUT:` → primes completion. #### Model Solution — (b) **Optimization Procedure (full marks = 7):** | Iteration | Change Applied | Metric Tracked | Target | Stop? | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 (Baseline) | No prompt; raw call | $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{H}$ | $\mathcal{F} \geq 85\%$ | No | | 1 | Add role + format spec | $\mathcal{F}$ | $\mathcal{F} \geq 85\%$ | If met, stop | | 2 | Add faithfulness rules | $\mathcal{H}$ | $\mathcal{H} \leq 5\%$ | If met, stop | | 3 | Add 2 few-shot exemplars | $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{H}$ | Combined | **Stop when $\Delta\mathcal{F} < 2\%$ over 2 consecutive iterations** | **Stopping criterion** (1 mark): When two consecutive iterations show improvement Δ < 2% in Task Fidelity, OR a hard threshold (e.g., $\mathcal{F} \geq 90\%$ and $\mathcal{H} \leq 3\%$) is met. **Evaluation set requirement** (1 mark): Use a held-out set of $N \geq 20$ tickets, manually labeled for ground-truth category and summary. --- ### Question B (14 Marks). `[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023 / Sample 2024]` **Consider a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system for answering questions over a 200-page internal HR policy document.** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain how you would design a task-specific prompt for this Q&A system. Address the ordering of context vs. question, refusal behavior, citation, and any token-budget considerations. **(b) [7 marks]** Compare **zero-shot**, **few-shot**, and **chain-of-thought (CoT)** prompting for this RAG task. Recommend one and justify with at least three concrete reasons grounded in the task's properties. #### Model Solution — (a) **Prompt Architecture (full marks = 7):** **Ordering rule** (1 mark): Place **context first, question second**. Reversed order degrades accuracy by 15–20% on instruction-tuned models. **Prompt structure:** ``` SYSTEM: You answer employee questions about HR policy using ONLY the excerpts provided below. If the answer is not in the excerpts, respond exactly: "Not found in provided policy." For every answer, cite the excerpt number in square brackets, e.g., [Excerpt 3]. CONTEXT: [Excerpt 1] {chunk_1} [Excerpt 2] {chunk_2} ... [Excerpt K] {chunk_K} QUESTION: {user_query} ANSWER (cite sources): ``` **Refusal behavior** (1 mark): The literal string `"Not found in provided policy."` prevents fabricated answers — a known RAG failure mode. **Citation requirement** (1 mark): Forces the model to ground each claim in a specific retrieved chunk, enabling a post-hoc validator to check $[Excerpt\,i]$ mentions align with the cited text. **Token-budget derivation** (2 marks): Assume $C_w = 8{,}000$ tokens, system prompt ≈ 100 tokens, answer buffer ≈ 200 tokens, safety margin 20%: $$ t_{chunks}^{max} = 0.80 \times (8000 - 100 - 200) = 6160 \text{ tokens} $$ With $K = 5$ chunks of equal size: $\approx 1{,}232$ tokens per chunk, i.e., $\approx 925$ words each — thus the document must be **chunked** into ~25 passages of ~925 words each before retrieval. #### Model Solution — (b) **Comparison Table (full marks = 7; 2 marks per technique + 1 mark for recommendation):** | Property | Zero-Shot | Few-Shot | Chain-of-Thought | |---|---|---|---| | Setup cost | None | Curate 2–3 examples | Curate reasoning examples | | Token cost | Lowest | +200–500 tokens | +500–1500 tokens | | Latency | Lowest | +10–20% | +30–60% | | Faithfulness | Moderate | High | Moderate (can drift) | | Best for | Simple lookup | Format-sensitive | Multi-hop reasoning | **Recommendation: Few-Shot (2 marks).** Three reasons: 1. **Format sensitivity** — HR answers need a consistent `{Answer, Citation, Confidence}` structure; 2–3 exemplars lock this in far more reliably than zero-shot instructions. 2. **Cost–performance sweet spot** — HR queries are mostly single-hop (e.g., *"How many leave days?"*), so CoT's multi-hop benefit is wasted, while its 30–60% latency penalty is unjustified. 3. **Refusal calibration** — few-shot examples can demonstrate *exactly* how to phrase the `"Not found"` response, which zero-shot often paraphrases inconsistently. *(If the student recommends CoT, acceptable only if justified by multi-hop reasoning needs like "If I take 3 days leave in December and rejoin in January, how many leaves remain?" — then full marks.)* --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Listing parameters without justification** (lose 2–3 marks per question). Always say *why* $T=0.2$ and not $T=0.8$. > 2. **Stopping at "the prompt works"** without a stopping criterion (lose 1–2 marks). You MUST specify Δ < 2% or a numerical threshold. > 3. **Putting the question before the context in RAG** (lose 1 mark instantly — this is a canonical ordering rule). > 4. **Confusing "prompt engineering" with "model fine-tuning"** (lose up to 2 marks). Prompts optimize the *input artifact*; fine-tuning modifies the *model weights*. They are not interchangeable. > 5. **Ignoring token budget** in 14-mark design questions (lose 2 marks). Always derive $C_{eff}$ and show the math. > 6. **No evaluation set size** (lose 1 mark). State $N \geq 10$ (text generation), $N \geq 20$ (Q&A), or $N \geq 30$ (summarization) as a minimum. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ Every task-specific prompt is built on the **R-I-I-O schema** (Role, Instruction, Input, Output Indicator). - ✅ The optimization loop is **Design → Measure → Refine** with an explicit **stopping criterion** (Δ < 2% over 2 iterations, or a fixed metric threshold). - ✅ Three first-class metrics: **Faithfulness, Conciseness, Task Fidelity (𝓕)**. - ✅ Three generation knobs: **Temperature (T)**, **Top-p**, **Top-k** — lower T for factual, higher T for creative. - ✅ For RAG/Q&A: **context first, question second**, always include a **refusal clause** and **citation requirement**. - ✅ For summarization: always specify **compression ratio target** and a **faithfulness clause**; distinguish extractive vs. abstractive. - ✅ For text generation: control **tone, length, and style**; few-shot exemplars are the highest-leverage optimization. - ✅ Token budget formula: $\hat{t} \approx 1.33 \times w$ (English), $\hat{t}_c \approx 0.75 \times c$ (code). - ✅ Effective context: $C_{eff} \approx 0.8 \times C_w$ (always reserve 20% safety margin). - ✅ Cost equation: $\mathcal{C} = (t_{in} \cdot p_{in} + t_{out} \cdot p_{out}) / 1000$. - ✅ Hallucination mitigation toolkit: context-pinning, refusal clause, low T, grounding validator, extractive pre-pass. - ✅ **Always justify every parameter choice** in KTU answers — *why* this T, *why* this length, *why* this exemplar. - ✅ **Always state the evaluation set size** — unevaluated optimization is not optimization. - ✅ Prompt engineering optimizes the **input artifact**, not the model weights — never confuse with fine-tuning.

topics.title: Using prompt engineering to fine-tune pre-trained models on specific datasets or tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using prompt engineering to fine-tune pre-trained models on specific datasets or tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Prompt Engineering for Fine-Tuning** refers to the systematic design, optimization, and application of natural language instructions (prompts) that adapt a frozen, pre-trained Large Language Model (LLM) to perform a downstream task or operate on a domain-specific dataset — *without* updating the original model weights through traditional gradient-based training.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this concept is positioned at the intersection of two paradigms:

- **Traditional Fine-Tuning (TFT):** Updates all parameters $\theta$ of a pre-trained model $M$ on a labeled dataset $\mathcal{D}$.
- **Prompt-Based Adaptation (PBA):** Keeps $\theta$ frozen, and instead optimizes a small set of prompt parameters $P$ (either as discrete tokens or continuous embeddings) such that $M(\theta, P(x))$ produces task-aligned outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fine-tuning via prompt engineering is also known as **Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)**. The two dominant families are **Prompt Tuning** (soft/learnable prompts) and **Prefix Tuning** (key-value prepended at every attention layer). Both are examinable under Module 2 of PECST868.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Doctor" Intuition

Imagine a pre-trained LLM is a **renowned general physician** who has read every medical textbook in the world ($\theta$ is locked, immutable). Now, a patient arrives with a rare cardiac condition.

- **Traditional Fine-Tuning** = Sending the doctor back to medical school for 4 more years to specialize in cardiology. Expensive, time-consuming, and alters the doctor's entire knowledge base.
- **Prompt Engineering for Fine-Tuning** = Handing the doctor a **single, perfectly crafted sticky note** (the prompt) that says: *"You are now a cardiologist. Focus only on cardiac symptoms. Output diagnosis in JSON format."* The doctor is the same; only the *steering mechanism* changed.

This sticky note is the **learnable prompt** — a tiny, optimizable set of parameters that re-directs the frozen model's behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU Students:** In standard prompt engineering (Module 1), the prompt is *hand-written by humans* (discrete, hard prompts). In *fine-tuning via prompt engineering* (Module 2), the prompt is *learned by the model itself* (continuous, soft prompts). The shift from human-designed to machine-learned prompts is the heart of this topic.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Locked-In

| Term | Meaning | KTU Frequency |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **Pre-trained Model** | Model $M$ with weights $\theta$ already learned on massive corpora | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Downstream Task** | Specific application (e.g., sentiment classification, NER) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Soft Prompt** | Continuous vector embeddings optimized via backpropagation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Hard Prompt** | Discrete human-readable tokens | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **PEFT** | Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning umbrella term | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Adapter** | Small bottleneck layers inserted into frozen transformers | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Few-Shot Exemplars** | Demonstrations embedded in the prompt context | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Pipeline — Why and How

The complete workflow for using prompt engineering to fine-tune a pre-trained model follows a strict 6-step sequence:

1. **Model Selection & Freezing**
   - *Why:* A pre-trained LLM (e.g., GPT, LLaMA, BERT-base) already encodes rich linguistic and world knowledge in $\theta$.
   - *How:* Load the model and set `requires_grad = False` for all parameters. Memory footprint for gradients drops to near zero.

2. **Downstream Dataset Preparation**
   - *Why:* The model needs labeled examples $(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{D}$ to know what "good output" looks like.
   - *How:* Format the dataset as `(input, output)` pairs. For classification, map labels to verbalizers (e.g., "positive" / "negative" tokens).

3. **Prompt Token Initialization**
   - *Why:* The prompt $P$ must start somewhere in the embedding space.
   - *How:* Either (a) initialize from existing token embeddings, or (b) randomize, or (c) use a hand-crafted hard prompt as the starting point.

4. **Prompt Parameter Optimization**
   - *Why:* We want $P^*$ that minimizes task loss.
   - *How:* Backpropagate the loss through the frozen transformer, updating *only* $P$.

5. **Inference-Time Re-Prompting**
   - *Why:* At test time, the frozen model + learned prompt is all you need.
   - *How:* Concatenate the soft prompt to the input embedding, then decode.

6. **Evaluation & Transferability Check**
   - *Why:* PEFT prompts often generalize across model scales.
   - *How:* Test on a held-out split and optionally transfer $P$ to a smaller/larger variant.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Prompt-Based Fine-Tuning

**Pillar A — Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021)**
- Prepends $n$ trainable embedding vectors to the input layer only.
- Soft prompts live in the same continuous space as the model's input embeddings $\mathbf{E} \in \mathbb{R}^{V \times d}$, where $V$ is vocabulary size and $d$ is hidden dimension.

**Pillar B — Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021)**
- Prepends trainable key-value pairs to *every* transformer attention layer.
- More expressive, fewer parameters needed for equivalent performance.

**Pillar C — P-Tuning v2 / Deep Prompt Tuning**
- Combines A and B; applies prompt parameters at multiple layers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Dim |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L}(\theta, P) = -\sum_{i} \log P_\theta(y_i \mid P, x_i)$ | Cross-entropy loss for prompt-tuned model | dimensionless |
| 2 | $P^* = \arg\min_{P} \mathcal{L}(\theta_{\text{frozen}}, P)$ | Optimization objective over prompt only | $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ |
| 3 | $\nabla_P \mathcal{L}$ | Gradient w.r.t. soft prompt only | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ |
| 4 | $\text{Params}(P) = n \times d$ | Soft prompt size | floats |
| 5 | $\text{Compression Ratio} = \frac{\text{Params}(P)}{\text{Params}(\theta)} \approx 0.01\%$ | Typical PEFT savings | percentage |
| 6 | $h_0 = [P;\, E(x)]$ | Layer-0 input = prompt concatenated with token embeddings | sequence of vectors |
| 7 | $\text{Verbalizer}: y \mapsto v(y) \in \mathcal{V}$ | Maps class label to a vocabulary word | categorical |
| 8 | $P(y \mid x) = \frac{\exp(w_y^\top h_L)}{\sum_{y'} \exp(w_{y'}^\top h_L)}$ | LM-head classification probability | probability |
| 9 | $\Delta\theta = 0,\quad \Delta P \neq 0$ | Frozen model, learning prompt | vectors |
| 10 | $P^{(t+1)} = P^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_P \mathcal{L}$ | SGD update on soft prompt | updates |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Equation 5 (Compression Ratio) is the single most tested numerical metric. If asked to compare PEFT vs. full fine-tuning, always quote the **~0.01% trainable parameters** figure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Edge Deployment:** Soft prompts of a few KB can be swapped per tenant on the same frozen model (multi-tenant SaaS).
- **Continual Learning:** New tasks $\rightarrow$ new prompts $\rightarrow$ zero catastrophic forgetting in $\theta$.
- **Domain Adaptation in Low-Resource Languages:** Only a few thousand labeled examples needed.
- **Healthcare & Legal NLP:** HIPAA-compliant adaptation without retraining the base model on private data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Soft Prompts Live in Embedding Space

Let the pre-trained model be a transformer with vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$, embedding matrix $E \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert \times d}$, and a frozen encoder stack producing hidden state $h_L \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$.

For a hard prompt $p = (p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ composed of discrete tokens, the input to layer 0 is:

$$
h_0 = [E(p_1);\, E(p_2);\, \dots;\, E(p_n);\, E(x_1);\, \dots;\, E(x_m)]
$$

where $[;\,]$ denotes sequence concatenation along the time axis.

**Step 1.** In **Prompt Tuning**, we *abandon* the requirement that $p_i$ must be a real token from $\mathcal{V}$. Instead, we allow $p_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to be any vector.

**Step 2.** Define the soft prompt as a matrix of free parameters:

$$
P = \begin{bmatrix} p_1^\top \\ p_2^\top \\ \vdots \\ p_n^\top \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}
$$

**Step 3.** The layer-0 input becomes:

$$
h_0 = [P;\, E(x)]
$$

**Step 4.** The cross-entropy loss for a single example $(x, y)$ where $y$ maps to verbalizer word $v(y)$ is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\log \frac{\exp(W_{v(y)}^\top h_L)}{\sum_{v' \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(W_{v'}^\top h_L)}
$$

**Step 5.** Since $\theta$ is frozen, the only learnable parameters are entries of $P$. The gradient of $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $P$ flows through every transformer layer via the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial h_L} \cdot \frac{\partial h_L}{\partial h_{L-1}} \cdots \frac{\partial h_1}{\partial h_0} \cdot \frac{\partial h_0}{\partial P}
$$

**Step 6.** Crucially, $\frac{\partial h_0}{\partial P} = I$ (identity in the prompt slots), so gradients arrive in the prompt embeddings *untransformed*. This is why Prompt Tuning needs many epochs to converge — the signal is far from the input.

**Step 7.** SGD update:

$$
P^{(t+1)} = P^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P}\bigg\vert_{P^{(t)}}
$$

**Step 8.** Total trainable parameters for Prompt Tuning:

$$
\text{Params}(P) = n \cdot d
$$

For a model with $d=4096$ and $n=20$ tokens, this is only **81,920 floats** — roughly **0.01%** of a 7B parameter LLM.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Pillars

| Feature | Prompt Tuning (Lester) | Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang) | P-Tuning v2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Where injected** | Input embedding only | Every attention layer (K, V) | Every layer |
| **Trainable params** | $n \times d$ | $L \times n \times (d_k + d_v)$ | $L \times n \times 2d$ |
| **Performance at scale** | Matches full FT (10B+ params) | Matches full FT across scales | Slightly better on small models |
| **Storage per task** | ~80 KB | ~5 MB | ~5 MB |
| **Independence from $\theta$** | High | Medium | Medium |
| **KTU Exam Likelihood** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction KTU Examiners Love:** Prompt Tuning injects *only* at the embedding layer. Prefix Tuning injects *at every layer's K and V projections*. Students often confuse the two — the Board typically awards 2 marks for correctly stating "Prefix Tuning modifies keys and values at every attention layer."

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 7-billion parameter LLM ($d = 4096$) is being adapted to a sentiment classification task using Prompt Tuning with $n = 10$ soft tokens. Calculate:
(a) The number of trainable parameters.
(b) The compression ratio vs. full fine-tuning.
(c) The total storage in KB (using 4-byte float32).

**Solution:**

(a) Trainable parameters:

$$
\text{Params}(P) = n \times d = 10 \times 4096 = 40{,}960
$$

(b) Compression ratio:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{40{,}960}{7 \times 10^9} = 5.85 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.000585\%
$$

This is even smaller than the commonly cited 0.01% — well within the "parameter-efficient" regime.

(c) Storage:

$$
\text{Storage} = 40{,}960 \times 4\,\text{bytes} = 163{,}840\,\text{bytes} \approx 160\,\text{KB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 160 KB file is *all you need* to deploy 1,000 different downstream task variants on the same frozen 7B model — a **multi-tenant AI** architecture impossible with full fine-tuning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture: Prompt-Tuning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Pre-trained LLM theta frozen] --> B[Input Text x]
    C[Soft Prompt P trainable] --> B
    B --> D[Input Embedding Layer]
    D --> E[Transformer Layer 1]
    E --> F[Transformer Layer L]
    F --> G[LM Head]
    G --> H[Output Tokens y]
    I[Task Loss L cross entropy] --> H
    I --> J[Gradient dL/dP]
    J --> K[Update P only]
    K --> C
    A -.no gradient.-> I
```

**Reading the diagram:** Dashed lines indicate frozen paths; solid arrows show gradient flow. Note that $\theta$ receives no gradient updates — only $P$ does.

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: Three PEFT Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Prompt Tuning Lester 2021]
        A1[Token Embeddings E] --> B1[Soft Prompt P nxd]
        B1 --> C1[Input Concat]
        C1 --> D1[Frozen Transformer]
    end
    subgraph S2[Prefix Tuning Li Liang 2021]
        A2[Input Embedding] --> D2[Frozen Transformer L1]
        B2[Prefix K1 V1] --> D2
        D2 --> D3[Frozen Transformer L2]
        B3[Prefix K2 V2] --> D3
        D3 --> D4[Frozen Transformer LL]
        B4[Prefix KL VL] --> D4
    end
    subgraph S3[P-Tuning v2]
        A3[Deep Prompts every layer] --> D5[Frozen Transformer]
    end
```

### 4.3 End-to-End Training Loop Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DS as Task Dataset D
    participant PT as Soft Prompt P
    participant M as Frozen LLM theta
    participant OPT as Optimizer SGD Adam
    DS->>PT: Sample batch x
    PT->>M: Concat P with E to x
    M->>M: Forward pass h L
    M->>OPT: Compute L
    OPT->>PT: Backprop dL dP
    OPT->>M: No update on theta
    Note over PT,P: P converges after few thousand steps
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Multi-Tenant Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BASE[Single Frozen Base Model theta]
        M[7B LLM shared GPU instance]
    end
    subgraph TENANT1[Tenant A Customer Support]
        P1[Soft Prompt 1 160 KB]
    end
    subgraph TENANT2[Tenant B Legal Docs]
        P2[Soft Prompt 2 160 KB]
    end
    subgraph TENANT3[Tenant C Medical]
        P3[Soft Prompt 3 160 KB]
    end
    P1 --> M
    P2 --> M
    P3 --> M
    M --> OUT1[Response A]
    M --> OUT2[Response B]
    M --> OUT3[Response C]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This multi-tenant architecture is **only** feasible because prompts are tiny and swappable. With full fine-tuning, each tenant would need a separate 14 GB model copy — economically and operationally impossible at scale.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning" in the context of prompt engineering. Mention any two advantages over traditional fine-tuning.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) is a class of techniques that adapts a large pre-trained model to downstream tasks by updating only a small fraction of parameters, typically through learnable prompts, adapters, or low-rank matrices, while keeping the original model weights frozen.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Storage Efficiency** — A soft prompt of ~160 KB replaces a 14 GB fine-tuned model copy, enabling multi-tenant deployment.
2. **No Catastrophic Forgetting** — Since the base weights $\theta$ remain unchanged, the model retains all its original capabilities while acquiring the new task.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two advantages — total 3.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between hard prompts and soft prompts with a suitable example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Prompt | Soft Prompt |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Representation | Discrete tokens from vocabulary | Continuous vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ |
| Readable by humans | Yes | No |
| Optimization | Manual / heuristic | Gradient-based |
| Example | "Translate to French: Hello" | Embedding vector $p_1 = [0.12, -0.87, \dots, 0.34]$ |

*(Award 1 mark per row × 3 distinguishing features; full 3 marks for complete table.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students routinely lose 3–4 marks by (a) confusing Prefix Tuning with Prompt Tuning, (b) forgetting to specify *which layer* the prompt is injected, and (c) omitting the verbalizer step. Always state **(i) injection location**, **(ii) trainable parameter count formula**, and **(iii) loss function explicitly** in your answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Prompt Tuning Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*(a)* Explain the architecture of **Prompt Tuning** as proposed by Lester et al. (2021). Include a clearly labeled diagram and the mathematical formulation of the optimization objective. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A team is adapting a 3-billion parameter LLM ($d=2560$) to a 5-class intent classification task using Prompt Tuning with 8 soft tokens. Calculate the number of trainable parameters, the compression ratio, and storage in MB (float32). Discuss why this approach is preferred over full fine-tuning in low-resource scenarios. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Architecture of Prompt Tuning — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Model Freezing (1 mark)**
A pre-trained transformer $M$ with parameters $\theta$ is loaded. All entries of $\theta$ are frozen: $\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = 0$ for the entire training duration.

**Step 2: Soft Prompt Definition (1 mark)**
A learnable prompt matrix $P \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ is created, where $n$ is the number of virtual tokens and $d$ is the embedding dimension. These are *not* tied to any vocabulary entry.

**Step 3: Input Construction (1 mark)**
For an input $x$ with tokens $(x_1, \dots, x_m)$, the embedding layer output becomes:

$$
h_0 = [P;\, E(x_1);\, E(x_2);\, \dots;\, E(x_m)]
$$

**Step 4: Forward Pass Through Frozen Transformer (1 mark)**
The full transformer processes $h_0$ producing hidden state $h_L$ at the final layer, then the LM head maps $h_L$ to vocabulary logits.

**Step 5: Loss and Optimization (2 marks)**
For task $T$ with dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ and verbalizer $v: \mathcal{Y} \to \mathcal{V}$:

$$
P^* = \arg\min_{P} \sum_{i=1}^{N} -\log \frac{\exp(W_{v(y_i)}^\top h_L^{(i)})}{\sum_{v' \in \mathcal{V}} \exp(W_{v'}^\top h_L^{(i)})}
$$

Only $P$ is updated via backpropagation; all other gradients are discarded.

**Step 6: Verbalizer (1 mark)**
The class label $y$ is mapped to a vocabulary word $v(y)$ — for sentiment: `positive` / `negative`; for intent: `book`, `cancel`, `check`, `greet`, `help`.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for visual aid — 1 bonus mark if sketched.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Trainable parameters (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Params}(P) = n \times d = 8 \times 2560 = 20{,}480
$$

**Step 2: Compression ratio (2 marks)**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{20{,}480}{3 \times 10^9} = 6.827 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.000683\%
$$

This is **146,484× fewer** parameters than full fine-tuning.

**Step 3: Storage in MB (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Storage} = 20{,}480 \times 4\,\text{bytes} = 81{,}920\,\text{bytes} = \frac{81{,}920}{1024^2} \approx 0.078\,\text{MB}
$$

**Step 4: Why preferred in low-resource scenarios (1 mark)**

In low-resource settings, full fine-tuning overfits dramatically due to the huge parameter count vs. small dataset. Prompt Tuning has only 20,480 parameters, making it inherently **regularized** and resistant to overfitting — a perfect fit for few-shot or scarce-data regimes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*(a)* Compare and contrast **Prompt Tuning**, **Prefix Tuning**, and **P-Tuning v2** with respect to: (i) injection location, (ii) trainable parameter count, and (iii) typical use cases. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A healthcare startup must adapt a frozen medical LLM to 12 different hospital tenants, each with private patient data. Explain with a block diagram how soft-prompt-based PEFT enables this multi-tenant deployment. Why is full fine-tuning economically infeasible here? *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison — 7 marks**

| Criterion | Prompt Tuning | Prefix Tuning | P-Tuning v2 |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Injection Location (2 marks)** | Input embedding layer only | Every transformer layer's K and V matrices | Every transformer layer, similar to prefix but more uniform |
| **Trainable Params (2 marks)** | $n \times d$ | $L \times n \times (d_k + d_v)$ | $L \times n \times 2d$ |
| **Use Case (2 marks)** | Large LLMs (10B+); multi-tenant SaaS | Mid-size models; generation tasks | Small models (100M–1B); classification with limited data |
| **Performance (1 mark)** | Matches full FT only at large scale | Matches full FT across scales | Slight edge on smaller models |

*(Award marks per row; tabular presentation earns 1 bonus mark.)*

---

**(b) Multi-Tenant PEFT Architecture — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Problem Statement (1 mark)**
A single frozen medical LLM $\theta$ must serve 12 hospitals, each with their own private patient datasets $\mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{D}_2, \dots, \mathcal{D}_{12}$. Full fine-tuning would require 12 separate copies of the 14 GB model → 168 GB total storage and 12× GPU memory.

**Step 2: PEFT Solution (2 marks)**
Train 12 distinct soft prompts $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_{12}$ on each hospital's data while keeping $\theta$ frozen. Each $P_i$ is ~160 KB → total prompt storage = 12 × 160 KB ≈ 2 MB. The base model is loaded **once** into GPU memory.

**Step 3: Block Diagram (3 marks)**

```
                Hospital 1 Data → P1 (160 KB) ┐
                Hospital 2 Data → P2 (160 KB) ├─→ Frozen Medical LLM θ (14 GB, single copy)
                Hospital 3 Data → P3 (160 KB) ┤            ↓
                    ...                        ├─→ Tenant-Specific Response
                Hospital 12 Data → P12 (160 KB)┘
```

**Step 4: Why Full FT is Infeasible (1 mark)**
- 12 × 14 GB = 168 GB storage (vs. 14 GB + 2 MB for PEFT)
- 12× training compute cost
- Catastrophic forgetting risk if models are updated
- Violates "single source of truth" for medical knowledge

*(1 bonus mark for mentioning HIPAA compliance benefit of not retraining on raw private data.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **PEFT** is the umbrella term; **Prompt Tuning** and **Prefix Tuning** are the two flagship methods.
- ✅ Soft prompts live in $\mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ — they are **not** real tokens from $\mathcal{V}$.
- ✅ Prompt Tuning injects at the **embedding layer only**; Prefix Tuning injects at **every attention layer's K, V**.
- ✅ Trainable parameter count for Prompt Tuning = $n \times d$ (≈ 0.01% of base model size).
- ✅ The **verbalizer** maps class labels to actual vocabulary words — never skip this in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Loss function** is standard cross-entropy over the verbalizer word's logit; gradient flows *back* to $P$ only.
- ✅ **Multi-tenant deployment** is the killer application: one frozen model + many tiny prompts.
- ✅ **Compression ratio** of ~0.01% is the magic number examiners love to see quoted.
- ✅ Soft prompts are **not human-readable** — they are dense, opaque vectors.
- ✅ Prefix Tuning is more expressive but uses more parameters; Prompt Tuning scales better at 10B+ parameter regime.
- ✅ In low-resource settings, PEFT outperforms full FT due to inherent regularization.
- ✅ The chain rule for gradient flow: $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial P}$ traverses all $L$ transformer layers unchanged — hence slower convergence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Prompt Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of Prompt Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for *Prompt Engineering (PECST868)*, **Applications of Prompt Engineering** refers to the systematic deployment of structured natural language instructions (prompts) to direct Large Language Models (LLMs) and generative AI systems toward accomplishing domain-specific, real-world tasks across software, business, healthcare, education, legal, and creative industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *Prompt Engineering Applications* are the practical, industry-aligned use-cases where carefully designed prompts, context windows, system messages, and example-driven patterns are engineered to extract reliable, accurate, and task-specific outputs from foundation models such as GPT, LLaMA, Gemini, Claude, and Mistral.

Mathematically, an application of prompt engineering can be abstracted as a mapping function:

$$f: P \times C \rightarrow O$$

Where:
- $P$ = Prompt (structured input text)
- $C$ = Context (retrieved documents, memory, system instructions)
- $O$ = Output (response generated by the LLM)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Master Chef Analogy":**
> Think of an LLM as an extraordinarily talented but **extremely literal master chef** who can cook *any* dish in the world, possessing every ingredient ever known. However, this chef:
> 1. **Cannot read your mind** — you must specify the dish.
> 2. **Follows your instructions verbatim** — vague instructions yield vague food.
> 3. **Remembers only the last few orders** (the context window).
> 4. **Cooks better when shown examples** ("here is a perfect Biryani, now make one similar").
>
> **Prompt Engineering is the act of writing the perfect "order ticket"** for this chef. The clearer, more structured, and example-rich your ticket, the better the dish. *Applications* of prompt engineering are the different "restaurants" (domains) where this chef works — sushi bars, bakeries, hospitals, law firms, and coding bootcamps.

## 1.3 Why Applications Matter in KTU 2024

- **Industry 4.0 / 5.0 alignment**: KTU emphasizes *Outcome-Based Education (OBE)* where graduates must demonstrate application-level skills.
- **NEP 2020 mandate**: Skill-based, employable knowledge — applications bridge the gap between theory and industry.
- **Cognitive level**: This topic maps to **Revised Bloom's Level 3 (Apply)** and **Level 4 (Analyze)** in the KTU CO mapping.

## 1.4 Classification of Prompt Engineering Applications

| S.No | Application Domain | Primary Task | Common LLM Use |
|------|--------------------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | Software Engineering | Code generation, debugging, refactoring | GitHub Copilot, CodeWhisperer |
| 2 | Content Creation | Articles, blogs, marketing copy | GPT-4, Claude, Jasper |
| 3 | Customer Support | Chatbots, ticket triage, FAQs | Intercom, Drift, custom RAG bots |
| 4 | Education | Tutoring, quiz generation, explanations | Khanmigo, EduChat |
| 5 | Healthcare | Clinical summarization, patient Q&A | Med-PaLM, Hippocratic AI |
| 6 | Legal | Contract review, case summarization | Harvey AI, CoCounsel |
| 7 | Data Analytics | NL-to-SQL, report generation | LangChain, pandas-agent |
| 8 | Research | Literature review, hypothesis generation | Elicit, Consensus |
| 9 | Creative Arts | Story writing, poetry, scripts | Sudowrite, NovelAI |
| 10 | Multimodal | Text-to-image, text-to-video | DALL·E, Midjourney, Sora |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The application of prompt engineering rests on three pillars from transformer architecture theory:

1. **In-Context Learning (ICL)** — The model's ability to learn a task from examples provided *within* the prompt itself, without any weight updates.
2. **Instruction Tuning Alignment** — Foundation models are post-trained on instruction-response pairs, making them sensitive to prompt phrasing.
3. **Attention Mechanism** — The self-attention layer allows the model to weight every token in the prompt, meaning *where* and *how* information appears in the prompt dramatically affects outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theoretical Principle:**
> A well-engineered application prompt maximizes **signal-to-noise ratio** within the model's limited attention budget and context window (typically **4K to 200K tokens** in modern LLMs).

## 2.2 The Six Pillars of Prompt Engineering Applications

### Pillar 1: Content Generation Applications
- **Use cases**: Blog writing, marketing copy, product descriptions, social media posts.
- **Key techniques**: Persona prompting, tone control, audience specification.
- **Prompt pattern**: *Role + Task + Audience + Constraints + Format*.

### Pillar 2: Code Generation & Software Engineering
- **Use cases**: Function synthesis, test case generation, bug fixing, code translation between languages.
- **Key techniques**: Few-shot code examples, chain-of-thought debugging, comment-driven generation.
- **Industry tools**: GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Tabnine, Replit Ghostwriter.

### Pillar 3: Question Answering & Knowledge Retrieval (RAG)
- **Use cases**: Enterprise search, document Q&A, customer support.
- **Key techniques**: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), chain-of-verification, citation prompting.
- **Architecture**: Query → Retriever → Top-K Documents → Prompt Constructor → LLM → Answer with citations.

### Pillar 4: Reasoning & Analytical Applications
- **Use cases**: Mathematical problem solving, logical deduction, strategic planning, data analysis.
- **Key techniques**: Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Tree of Thoughts (ToT), ReAct, Self-Consistency.
- **Formula**: For $N$ sampled reasoning paths, the self-consistency answer is:

$$\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(a_i = a)$$

Where $a_i$ is the answer from the $i$-th sampled chain and $\mathbb{1}$ is the indicator function.

### Pillar 5: Conversational & Agentic Applications
- **Use cases**: Personal assistants, workflow automation, multi-step task execution.
- **Key techniques**: Tool-use prompting, function calling, memory-augmented dialogue, agent loops.
- **Agent cycle**: Observe → Think → Act → Observe (ReAct loop).

### Pillar 6: Multimodal & Creative Applications
- **Use cases**: Text-to-image, text-to-video, text-to-audio, design generation.
- **Key techniques**: Negative prompting, style weighting, seed control, iterative refinement.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Prompt-to-Output Mapping | $O = M_{\theta}(P, C)$ | $M_{\theta}$: model weights, $P$: prompt, $C$: context |
| 2 | Softmax Temperature Sampling | $p_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j / T)}$ | $T \to 0$: greedy, $T \to \infty$: uniform |
| 3 | Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling | $V_p = \min V \text{ s.t. } \sum_{x \in V_p} P(x) \ge p$ | $p \in [0,1]$, typically $p=0.9$ |
| 4 | Self-Consistency Voting | $\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(a_i = a)$ | $N$: number of sampled chains |
| 5 | ROUGE-L Score (F-measure) | $F_{lcs} = \frac{(1+\beta^2) R_{lcs} P_{lcs}}{R_{lcs} + \beta^2 P_{lcs}}$ | Evaluates summarization quality |
| 6 | BLEU Score | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $BP$: brevity penalty, $p_n$: n-gram precision |
| 7 | Perplexity | $PPL(X) = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p_{\theta}(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ | Lower = better language model |
| 8 | Context Window Utilization | $\eta = \frac{T_{used}}{T_{max}} \times 100\%$ | $T_{max}$: max tokens, e.g., 128K |
| 9 | Hallucination Rate | $H_{rate} = \frac{\text{Unsupported claims}}{\text{Total claims}}$ | Lower is better, target $H_{rate} < 0.05$ |
| 10 | Token Cost Estimation | $C_{total} = n_{in} \cdot c_{in} + n_{out} \cdot c_{out}$ | $c_{in}$, $c_{out}$: per-token cost in USD |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in *production-grade* LLM applications. For example, a company building a customer-support chatbot must monitor $H_{rate}$ to comply with regulations, and compute $C_{total}$ to forecast API bills.

## 2.4 The Universal Application Prompt Template (UAPT)

A KTU-recommended template applicable across *all* application domains:

```
[SYSTEM]     → Role + Expertise + Constraints
[CONTEXT]    → Background data, retrieved docs, memory
[INSTRUCTION]→ Verb-driven task ("Analyze", "Generate", "Classify")
[EXAMPLES]   → 1-5 few-shot demonstrations
[INPUT]      → The actual user query / data
[FORMAT]     → Output schema (JSON, table, markdown, length)
[GUARDRAILS] → What NOT to do (negative constraints)
```

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software firms**: Reduce developer time by 30-55% using code-generation prompts (per GitHub's official Copilot data).
- **Healthcare**: Reduces clinician documentation time by ~70% (Nuance DAX Copilot deployments).
- **Legal**: Cuts contract review time from 90 minutes to 12 minutes (Harvey AI benchmarks).
- **Education**: Personalized tutoring at scale in rural Kerala schools via vernacular language prompts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Prompt to Production-Ready Application Pipeline

The transformation from a *raw user query* to a *deployed prompt-engineered application* follows a rigorous six-stage derivation.

### Stage 1 — Requirement Decomposition

$$\text{Task} \rightarrow \langle \text{Goal}, \text{Input}, \text{Output}, \text{Constraints}, \text{Evaluation} \rangle$$

**Explanation of transition:** Every application requirement is decomposed into the **GIOCE** quintuple — *Goal, Input, Output, Constraints, Evaluation*. For example, an e-commerce "product description generator" decomposes to:
- Goal: Persuasive product copy
- Input: Product attributes (name, specs, price)
- Output: 150-word SEO-friendly paragraph
- Constraints: No false claims, brand voice consistency
- Evaluation: BLEU vs. human reference, A/B conversion rate

### Stage 2 — Prompt Schema Construction

$$P_{final} = P_{system} \oplus P_{context} \oplus P_{instruction} \oplus P_{examples} \oplus P_{input} \oplus P_{format}$$

**Explanation of transition:** The six components defined in Section 2.4 (UAPT) are **concatenated** (denoted $\oplus$) in a specific order. Order matters because transformer self-attention gives *positional bias* — early tokens often receive disproportionate attention weight.

### Stage 3 — Sampling Parameter Optimization

$$p_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / T)}$$

**Explanation of transition:** The raw logits $z_i$ from the LLM's output head are passed through a **temperature-scaled softmax** to produce a probability distribution over the vocabulary of size $V$. This governs *creativity vs. determinism*:

**Worked numerical example:**
Given logits $z = [2.0, 1.0, 0.5]$ and $T = 1.0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \frac{e^{2.0}}{e^{2.0} + e^{1.0} + e^{0.5}} = \frac{7.389}{7.389 + 2.718 + 1.649} = \frac{7.389}{11.756} \approx 0.629 \\
p_2 &= \frac{e^{1.0}}{11.756} = \frac{2.718}{11.756} \approx 0.231 \\
p_3 &= \frac{e^{0.5}}{11.756} = \frac{1.649}{11.756} \approx 0.140
\end{aligned}$$

If we lower $T = 0.5$, the distribution sharpens (more deterministic):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \frac{e^{4.0}}{e^{4.0} + e^{2.0} + e^{1.0}} = \frac{54.598}{54.598 + 7.389 + 2.718} = \frac{54.598}{64.705} \approx 0.844 \\
p_2 &= \frac{7.389}{64.705} \approx 0.114 \\
p_3 &= \frac{2.718}{64.705} \approx 0.042
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Rule:** Use $T \le 0.3$ for **code generation** and **legal summarization** (deterministic). Use $T \in [0.7, 1.2]$ for **creative writing** and **brainstorming** (diverse).

### Stage 4 — RAG Context Injection (for Knowledge Applications)

$$P_{augmented} = P_{system} \oplus \text{TopK}(q, \mathcal{D}) \oplus P_{query}$$

Where the retrieval function is:

$$\text{TopK}(q, \mathcal{D}) = \arg\max_{d \in \mathcal{D}}^{K} \; \text{sim}(E_q, E_d)$$

**Explanation of transition:** For a user query $q$, the system encodes it via an embedding model $E$ and retrieves the top-$K$ most similar documents from a vector database $\mathcal{D}$ using cosine similarity $\text{sim}$. These are prepended to the prompt, giving the LLM *grounded* knowledge to reduce hallucinations.

### Stage 5 — Self-Consistency Voting (for Reasoning Applications)

$$\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(a_i = a)$$

**Worked example:** A math problem is sent to the LLM 5 times with $T=0.7$ to get 5 reasoning chains. Suppose the answers are: $a_1=42, a_2=42, a_3=41, a_4=42, a_5=39$.
- Count of 42: 3
- Count of 41: 1
- Count of 39: 1

$$\hat{a} = 42 \quad \text{(majority vote)}$$

### Stage 6 — Evaluation & Guardrails

$$\text{Quality Score} = w_1 \cdot \text{BLEU} + w_2 \cdot \text{ROUGE-L} + w_3 \cdot (1 - H_{rate}) + w_4 \cdot \text{Reliability}$$

Where weights $w_i \ge 0$ and $\sum w_i = 1$.

**Explanation of transition:** A composite Quality Score combines automated metrics with human/LLM-judged reliability. Applications failing this gate are sent back to Stage 2 for prompt refinement — closing the MLOps loop.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Application → Technique → Tool

| Application | Best-Fit Prompting Technique | Recommended Tool / Model | Critical Metric |
|-------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| Code Generation | Few-shot + CoT | GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet | Pass@k on HumanEval |
| Customer Support | RAG + Persona Prompting | Custom + Pinecone | CSAT, $H_{rate}$ |
| Medical Q&A | Role + Chain-of-Verification | Med-PaLM 2 | F1 on MedQA |
| Legal Review | Role + Structured Output | Harvey, Clause | Precision on CUAD |
| Math Reasoning | Self-Consistency + ToT | GPT-4, Gemini 1.5 | GSM8K accuracy |
| Creative Writing | High-$T$ + Persona | Claude 3 Opus | Human preference |
| Data Analytics | NL-to-SQL + ReAct | LangChain SQL Agent | Execution accuracy |
| Tutoring | Socratic Chain-of-Thought | Khanmigo, GPT-4 | Learning gain |
| Image Generation | Negative + Style Prompting | Midjourney v6, DALL·E 3 | CLIP score, FID |
| Translation | Few-shot + Glossary Injection | NLLB, GPT-4 | BLEU, chrF |

## 3.3 End-to-End Application Build: "KTU Student Doubt-Solver Chatbot"

**Step 1**: Define goal → Answer KTU syllabus doubts in Malayalam/English.

**Step 2**: Collect corpus → KTU 2024 syllabus PDFs, previous QPs, textbooks.

**Step 3**: Build RAG:

$$\text{chunk}_i = \text{Splitter}(\text{doc}_j, \text{size}=512, \text{overlap}=64)$$

Each chunk is embedded: $e_i = E(\text{chunk}_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$.

**Step 4**: Prompt template:

```
SYSTEM: You are a KTU professor. Answer using ONLY the provided context.
CONTEXT: {{retrieved_chunks}}
INSTRUCTION: Answer the student's question in {{language}}. Cite module number.
INPUT: {{student_query}}
FORMAT: Markdown with [Module X] citations.
GUARDRAILS: Do not answer questions outside KTU 2024 syllabus.
```

**Step 5**: Deploy with $T = 0.3$ (factual), Top-p = 0.9, max_tokens = 800.

**Step 6**: Evaluate using RAGAS framework:

$$\text{RAGAS} = \frac{1}{4}\left(S_{faith} + S_{ans\_rel} + S_{ctx\_rel} + S_{ctx\_prec}\right)$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture: Prompt Engineering Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UQ[User Query Input] --> RP[Requirements Decomposition]
    RP --> PS[Prompt Schema Builder]
    PS --> RT[Retriever Module]
    KB[(Vector Knowledge Base)] --> RT
    RT --> AUG[Context Augmentation]
    PS --> AUG
    AUG --> LLM[LLM Inference Engine]
    LLM --> SAMP[Sampling and Decoding]
    SAMP --> GUARD[Output Guardrails Filter]
    GUARD --> VAL[Evaluation Layer]
    VAL -->|Pass| RESP[Response to User]
    VAL -->|Fail| FB[Feedback Loop]
    FB --> PS
```

## 4.2 Six Major Application Domains Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PE[Prompt Engineering Core] --> A1[Software Engineering Apps]
    PE --> A2[Content Generation Apps]
    PE --> A3[Conversational AI Apps]
    PE --> A4[Analytical and Reasoning Apps]
    PE --> A5[Educational Apps]
    PE --> A6[Multimodal Creative Apps]
    A1 --> T1[GitHub Copilot]
    A1 --> T2[CodeWhisperer]
    A2 --> T3[Jasper AI]
    A2 --> T4[Copy.ai]
    A3 --> T5[Intercom Fin]
    A3 --> T6[Custom RAG Bots]
    A4 --> T7[LangChain Agents]
    A4 --> T8[pandas AI]
    A5 --> T9[Khanmigo]
    A5 --> T10[EduChat]
    A6 --> T11[Midjourney]
    A6 --> T12[Sora]
```

## 4.3 Reasoning Application: Chain-of-Thought + Self-Consistency Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Complex Question] --> COT1[Chain of Thought Sample 1]
    Q --> COT2[Chain of Thought Sample 2]
    Q --> COT3[Chain of Thought Sample 3]
    Q --> COTN[Chain of Thought Sample N]
    COT1 --> A1[Answer 1]
    COT2 --> A2[Answer 2]
    COT3 --> A3[Answer 3]
    COTN --> AN[Answer N]
    A1 --> V[Majority Voting Aggregator]
    A2 --> V
    A3 --> V
    AN --> V
    V --> FA[Final Consolidated Answer]
```

## 4.4 RAG-Based Application Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Ingestion[Offline Document Ingestion]
        D1[Source PDFs and Docs] --> SPL[Text Splitter]
        SPL --> EMB[Embedding Model]
        EMB --> VDB[(Vector Database)]
    end
    subgraph Query[Online Query Path]
        QU[User Question] --> QEM[Query Embedder]
        QEM --> SIM[Similarity Search Top K]
        VDB --> SIM
        SIM --> PROMPT[Prompt Constructor]
        PROMPT --> LLM[LLM with Retrieved Context]
        LLM --> OUT[Grounded Answer with Citations]
    end
```

## 4.5 Agentic Application: ReAct Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBS1[Observe Environment] --> TH[Think and Reason]
    TH --> AC[Take Action via Tool]
    AC --> OBS2[Observe Result]
    OBS2 -->|Goal Not Met| TH
    OBS2 -->|Goal Achieved| DONE[Final Answer]
```

## 4.6 Application Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Application Task] --> Q1{Requires External Knowledge?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| RAG[Use RAG Application Pattern]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Requires Multi-Step Reasoning?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| REA[Use ReAct or Tree of Thoughts]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Needs Tool or API Calls?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| AGT[Use Agentic Pattern]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Creative or Factual Output?}
    Q4 -->|Creative| CRE[Use High Temperature Persona Prompt]
    Q4 -->|Factual| FAC[Use Low Temperature Structured Prompt]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Dec 2024]**

### Question 1: Define Prompt Engineering Applications with two real-world examples.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Prompt Engineering Applications refer to the practical deployment of carefully designed natural language instructions to direct Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform domain-specific, real-world tasks. Two real-world examples:
1. **GitHub Copilot** — uses code-completion prompts to auto-generate functions from natural language comments.
2. **Harvey AI** — uses role + RAG prompts to assist lawyers in contract review and case law summarization.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two valid examples.)*

### Question 2: List any three application domains of prompt engineering and the primary prompt technique used in each.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Domain | Primary Technique |
|--------|-------------------|
| Software Engineering | Few-shot code examples + Chain-of-Thought debugging |
| Customer Support | Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) + Persona Prompting |
| Mathematical Reasoning | Self-Consistency + Chain-of-Thought voting |

*(1 mark per correct domain-technique pair.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Pattern, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture** as a major prompt engineering application. Discuss its advantages over vanilla prompting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** RAG is a prompt engineering application pattern that combines an external *retriever* (typically a vector database + embedding model) with a *generator* (LLM) to ground responses in factual, up-to-date, domain-specific knowledge. Formally:

$$P_{augmented} = P_{system} \oplus \text{TopK}(E_q, \mathcal{D}) \oplus P_{query}$$

**Architecture Stages (3 marks):**
1. **Ingestion**: Documents are split into chunks (≈512 tokens) and embedded into a vector DB.
2. **Retrieval**: User query $q$ is embedded and top-$K$ similar chunks are fetched via cosine similarity.
3. **Augmented Prompt Construction**: Retrieved chunks are prepended to the system prompt.
4. **Generation**: LLM produces a grounded answer citing the retrieved sources.

**Advantages over vanilla prompting (2 marks):**
- Reduces **hallucinations** by anchoring outputs in retrieved evidence.
- Enables **knowledge updates** without expensive fine-tuning.
- Provides **source citations** for transparency and auditability.
- Allows **domain specialization** (legal, medical, KTU syllabus) with minimal data.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Self-Consistency** techniques used in reasoning applications of prompt engineering. Derive the majority-vote formula and solve: Given 7 sampled CoT answers $\{7, 7, 8, 7, 6, 7, 9\}$, compute the final answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting (2 marks):**
CoT prompting instructs the LLM to "think step by step" before producing a final answer. It elicits intermediate reasoning tokens that act as a scratchpad, dramatically improving performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks.

**Self-Consistency (2 marks):**
Self-Consistency samples $N$ independent CoT chains (typically $N \in [5, 40]$) at temperature $T > 0$ and selects the most frequent final answer through majority voting.

**Derivation of majority-vote formula (2 marks):**
Let $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_N$ be the final answers from $N$ sampled chains. The self-consistency estimate is:

$$\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(a_i = a)$$

This estimator minimizes the probability of error when the per-chain accuracy $p > 0.5$, because the binomial tail probability $\Pr(\text{majority wrong})$ decreases exponentially with $N$.

**Numerical solution (1 mark):**
Counts: 7 appears 4 times, 8 appears 1 time, 6 appears 1 time, 9 appears 1 time.

$$\hat{a} = \arg\max_{a} \text{count}(a) = 7 \quad \text{(with frequency 4/7)}$$

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice
