**Q. (a)** Discuss the role of prompt engineering in **software engineering applications**. Explain with examples how few-shot prompting and comment-driven generation accelerate code development. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of PE in Software Engineering (3 marks):**
Prompt engineering has revolutionized software development by enabling *AI pair programmers*. Tools like GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Amazon CodeWhisperer use prompts constructed from:
- File context (open files in IDE)
- Cursor position
- Comment-driven natural language specifications
- Retrieved similar code snippets

**Few-Shot Prompting in Code Generation (2 marks):**
By providing 2-3 example function-implementation pairs in the prompt, the LLM infers the coding pattern, style, and library preferences. Example pattern:
```
Example 1: sort a list → return sorted(list)
Example 2: find max → return max(list)
Task: compute average → return sum(list)/len(list)
```

**Comment-Driven Generation (2 marks):**
A natural language comment such as `# function to validate KTU roll number format` causes the LLM to generate a regex-based validator. Studies show this boosts developer productivity by **30-55%** (GitHub 2023 official report).

---

**Q. (b)** Compare the application of prompt engineering in **healthcare** versus **legal** domains. What guardrails and evaluation metrics are critical in each? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Healthcare Applications (2 marks):**
- Clinical note summarization (Nuance DAX).
- Patient Q&A chatbots (Med-PaLM 2).
- Radiology report generation.
- Drug interaction checking.

**Legal Applications (2 marks):**
- Contract review and clause extraction (Harvey AI, Clause).
- Case-law summarization.
- Legal Q&A grounded in statutes.
- Document due-diligence.

**Critical Guardrails — Healthcare (1.5 marks):**
- HIPAA / DISHA compliance (India's Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act).
- $H_{rate} < 0.01$ (near-zero hallucination tolerance).
- Human-in-the-loop clinician approval mandatory.
- Metrics: F1 on MedQA, recall on adverse-drug-event detection.

**Critical Guardrails — Legal (1.5 marks):**
- Citation accuracy and precedent verification.
- Confidentiality (client-attorney privilege).
- Human lawyer sign-off on all outputs.
- Metrics: Precision on CUAD dataset, BLEU vs. senior-partner-edited drafts, citation correctness rate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "applications" with "techniques" — applications are *domains* (healthcare, legal), techniques are *methods* (CoT, RAG, few-shot). Examiners deduct **1-2 marks** for this swap.
> 2. **Always cite formulas** when discussing evaluation (BLEU, ROUGE-L, hallucination rate) — verbal descriptions alone fetch only partial marks.
> 3. **Diagrams must have labeled arrows and blocks** — unlabelled flowcharts receive **at most 50% marks** in the diagram portion.
> 4. **Avoid generic answers** like "AI is used in many fields" — KTU 2024 scheme demands *specific tool names, specific prompt patterns, and specific metrics*.
> 5. **In RAG questions**, students often forget to mention the *embedding model* and *vector database* — examiners specifically check for these terms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3: Applications of Prompt Engineering**

- ⭐ **Definition**: Application = practical deployment of engineered prompts across real-world domains.
- ⭐ **GIOCE Decomposition**: Every application needs *Goal, Input, Output, Constraints, Evaluation*.
- ⭐ **Six Pillars**: Software, Content, Conversational, Reasoning, Education, Multimodal.
- ⭐ **UAPT Template**: System → Context → Instruction → Examples → Input → Format → Guardrails.
- ⭐ **RAG formula**: $P_{augmented} = P_{system} \oplus \text{TopK}(E_q, \mathcal{D}) \oplus P_{query}$.
- ⭐ **Self-Consistency formula**: $\hat{a} = \arg\max_a \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(a_i = a)$.
- ⭐ **Temperature rule**: $T \le 0.3$ for factual/legal/code; $T \in [0.7, 1.2]$ for creative writing.
- ⭐ **Evaluation metrics to memorize**: BLEU, ROUGE-L, Perplexity, Hallucination Rate, RAGAS, Pass@k.
- ⭐ **Industry tools to mention**: GitHub Copilot, Harvey AI, Med-PaLM 2, Midjourney, Khanmigo, LangChain.
- ⭐ **Critical guardrails**: HIPAA (healthcare), citation accuracy (legal), human-in-the-loop (all high-stakes).
- ⭐ **Common KTU mistakes**: Confusing applications vs. techniques, missing diagram labels, omitting formulas.
- ⭐ **NEP 2020 alignment**: OBE Level 3 (Apply) + Level 4 (Analyze) — focus on *how* and *why*, not just *what*.
- ⭐ **Revision mantra**: *"Prompt is the new program; context is the new compiler; evaluation is the new debugger."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prompt Engineering in Chatbots and Conversational AI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prompt Engineering in Chatbots and Conversational AI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prompt Engineering in Chatbots and Conversational AI** is the disciplined practice of designing, structuring, and iteratively refining the natural-language instructions, contextual scaffolds, and conversational metadata supplied to a Large Language Model (LLM) so that it behaves as a coherent, task-aligned, persona-consistent, and safe dialogue agent.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECST868)**, the term specifically refers to the engineering of three distinct prompt layers that govern every conversational turn:

1. **System Prompt (Instructional Layer)** – A persistent, hidden pre-prompt that defines the bot's persona, scope, behavioural constraints, and output schema.
2. **Developer/Context Prompt (Operational Layer)** – Injects retrieved documents, tool outputs, function-calling schemas, and ephemeral state into the model's context window.
3. **User Prompt (Interactional Layer)** – The live, multi-turn exchange between the end-user and the assistant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Airline Check-In Counter**
> Imagine a chatbot as an airline check-in agent. The **system prompt** is the training manual the agent received on day one (uniform, tone, rules, what to do when a passport is missing). The **developer prompt** is the live flight roster on the screen in front of them (today's data). The **user prompt** is the passenger standing at the counter saying *"I'd like a window seat."* If the manual is vague, the agent gives inconsistent service. If the roster is wrong, the agent books the wrong seat. If the passenger's request isn't understood, the conversation breaks. **Prompt engineering is writing the perfect manual, keeping the roster accurate, and coaching the agent to ask clarifying questions.**

### 1.3 Why It Matters in the KTU Syllabus

KTU examiners in the **2024 Scheme** have explicitly framed chatbot prompt engineering around four measurable outcomes:

| Outcome ID | Course Outcome (CO) Statement |
|------------|-------------------------------|
| **CO3** | Design and evaluate prompt pipelines for task-specific conversational systems. |
| **CO4** | Apply advanced prompting strategies (few-shot, CoT, ReAct) to multi-turn agents. |
| **CO5** | Mitigate hallucination, bias, and jailbreak risks through prompt-level guardrails. |

The key performance metrics expected to be **memorised verbatim** for the exam are:

- **Tokens** – the atomic unit of LLM input/output (≈ 0.75 English words).
- **Context Window** – the maximum number of tokens the model can attend to in a single inference.
- **Temperature (T)** – a sampling parameter in the range **[0.0, 2.0]** controlling randomness.
- **Top-p (Nucleus Sampling)** – cumulative probability threshold, typically **[0.0, 1.0]**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer Prompt Architecture

Every modern conversational AI (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA-based agents) operates on a layered prompt stack. Understanding the **hierarchy** is essential for KTU 14-mark questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — System Prompt (Static, Highest Priority)**
> Defines: Persona, role, tone, hard constraints, output format (JSON/Markdown), refusal behaviour, and safety policy.
> Example header: *"You are MedBot, a cautious clinical decision-support assistant. You never provide a diagnosis; you always suggest the user consult a licensed physician."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 2 — Developer / Context Prompt (Dynamic, Mid Priority)**
> Carries: Retrieved documents (RAG chunks), tool/function definitions (JSON Schema), previous conversation summary, and user metadata (location, tier, language).

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 — User Prompt (Volatile, Lowest Priority)**
> Carries: The raw, untrusted user utterance. The system prompt must be robust enough to override malicious user attempts.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Flow of a Conversational Turn

The following sequential logic governs **every single response** of a chatbot:

1. **Tokenisation** – The full prompt stack (system + context + user history + new query) is converted into tokens.
2. **Context Window Validation** – If `total_tokens > context_window`, a **truncation** or **summarisation** strategy must run.
3. **Embedding & Forward Pass** – The transformer attends to all tokens via self-attention.
4. **Decoding Strategy Selection** – Greedy, Beam Search, Top-k, Top-p, or Min-p sampling is applied based on the `temperature` setting.
5. **Post-Processing** – Output is parsed, validated against the schema, and the new turn is appended to conversation history.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for the Module 3 examination. **Memorise the units and ranges verbatim.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Range / Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Token Estimation | $\text{Tokens} \approx \dfrac{\text{Words} \times 1.33}$ | 1 token ≈ 4 chars (English) |
| 2 | Context Window Budget | $C_{\text{available}} = W - (S + R + O)$ | $W$ = window size, $S$ = system, $R$ = response reserve, $O$ = overhead |
| 3 | Softmax Temperature Sampling | $p_i = \dfrac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j / T)}$ | $T \in [0, 2]$; $T=0$ → greedy |
| 4 | Top-p (Nucleus) Cutoff | $\sum_{i \in V_p} p_i \geq p$, smallest such $V_p$ | $p \in [0, 1]$, typical $p = 0.9$ |
| 5 | Perplexity (Quality Metric) | $\text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \log p(x_i)\right)$ | Lower is better |
| 6 | BLEU Score (Conversational N-gram Overlap) | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | $\text{BP}$ = brevity penalty |
| 7 | Hallucination Rate (Operational) | $H_r = \dfrac{\mid \text{Fabricated Responses}\mid}{\mid \text{Total Responses}\mid}$ | Target $H_r < 5\%$ |
| 8 | Task Success Rate (TSR) | $\text{TSR} = \dfrac{\text{Successfully Completed Dialogues}}{\text{Total Dialogues}} \times 100\%$ | Production target $\geq 90\%$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** When writing the Softmax equation, students often **forget to divide by $T$** or place $T$ in the numerator without justification. If $T > 1$, the distribution flattens (more creative); if $T < 1$, it sharpens (more deterministic). Always state this intuition in the exam.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Prompt Strategy Used |
|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Healthcare** | Symptom triage chatbots | Persona-locked system prompt + RAG over medical KB |
| **Banking** | Account-assistant bots | Function-calling prompt for transaction APIs |
| **EdTech** | Doubt-solving tutors | Chain-of-Thought (CoT) + Socratic system prompt |
| **E-Commerce** | Product recommendation | Few-shot persona prompt + structured JSON output |
| **IT Helpdesks** | Ticket resolution | ReAct prompt (Reason + Act loop) over tool docs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Softmax with Temperature (Step-by-Step)

This is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 3. Examiners expect **every intermediate line**.

**Step 1 — Start with the standard softmax probability for token $i$:**

$$p_i = \frac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j)}$$

where $z_i$ is the logit (raw score) produced by the model for the $i$-th token in the vocabulary $V$.

**Step 2 — Introduce the temperature hyperparameter $T$ as a divisor on the logits:**

$$p_i^{(T)} = \frac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / T)}$$

**Step 3 — Analyse the boundary case $T \to 0$:**

$$
\lim_{T \to 0} p_i^{(T)} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } z_i = \max(z) \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This collapses the distribution into **greedy decoding** — the model always picks the single highest-scoring token. **Conversion Logic:** As $T$ shrinks, the exponent amplifies differences between logits, forcing all probability mass onto the winner.

**Step 4 — Analyse the boundary case $T \to \infty$:**

$$
\lim_{T \to \infty} p_i^{(T)} = \frac{1}{V}
$$

The distribution becomes **uniform** — every token in the vocabulary is equally likely. **Conversion Logic:** Dividing by an enormous $T$ flattens all exponents toward zero, so $\exp(0) = 1$ for every term, yielding a uniform $1/V$.

**Step 5 — Practical conversational rule of thumb:**

- **Factual Q&A bot (low creativity):** $T = 0.0$ to $0.3$.
- **Balanced assistant (default):** $T = 0.7$.
- **Creative brainstorming bot:** $T = 1.0$ to $1.2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for chatbots:** A customer-support bot with $T = 1.5$ will hallucinate policy details. A poem-generator with $T = 0.0$ will produce the same rhyme every run. The prompt engineer must **explicitly instruct** the API to set $T$ correctly, since the model has no internal concept of "appropriate creativity."

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Prompting Strategies for Chatbots

| Strategy | Mechanism | When to Use in a Chatbot | Failure Mode if Misapplied |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Zero-Shot** | Direct instruction, no examples | Simple FAQ bots, single-intent tasks | Hallucinates on ambiguous queries |
| **One-Shot** | 1 example in system prompt | Standardising output format (e.g., date) | Over-fits to that single example |
| **Few-Shot** | 3–5 examples in system prompt | Tone-matching, persona cloning | Token budget explosion |
| **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** | *"Let's think step by step"* | Math/logic troubleshooting | 3–4× slower inference |
| **ReAct (Reason+Act)** | Interleaved Thought → Action → Observation | Tool-using agents (booking, queries) | Infinite loops if tool fails |
| **Self-Consistency** | Sample N CoT answers, majority vote | High-stakes medical/legal | Compute cost is $N \times$ |
| **Persona Prompting** | *"You are X with trait Y"* | Brand-voice consistency | Persona can be jailbroken |
| **Constitutional AI** | Embedded principles self-critique | Refusal-style moderation | Over-refusal, robotic tone |
| **RAG-Augmented** | Inject retrieved docs into context | Knowledge-grounded Q&A | Irrelevant chunks poison context |
| **Function Calling** | JSON-Schema tool definitions | API orchestration | Hallucinated argument values |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Context Window Budget

**Problem:** A chatbot has a context window $W = 8{,}192$ tokens. The system prompt consumes $S = 450$ tokens. The user wants a response reserve of $R = 1{,}000$ tokens. A RAG retriever injects 6 document chunks averaging $D = 320$ tokens each. The conversation history is currently $H = 1{,}800$ tokens. Frameworks and overhead consume $O = 200$ tokens. **How many additional tokens of user query can be accepted before truncation?**

**Step 1 — State the budget equation:**

$$C_{\text{available}} = W - (S + R + O)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**

$$C_{\text{available}} = 8192 - (450 + 1000 + 200)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sum inside the parentheses:**

$$C_{\text{available}} = 8192 - 1650 = 6542 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the RAG injection load ($6 \times 320$):**

$$\text{RAG Load} = 6 \times 320 = 1920 \text{ tokens}$$

$$C_{\text{remaining}} = 6542 - 1920 = 4622 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the conversation history:**

$$C_{\text{user}} = 4622 - 1800 = 2822 \text{ tokens}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to approximate words:**

$$W_{\text{user}} \approx 2822 \times 0.75 \approx 2117 \text{ words}$$

**Final Answer:** The system can accept **2,822 tokens (≈ 2,117 words)** of new user input before a summarisation pass must run.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Conversational AI System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Utterance] --> B[Input Sanitiser and Tokeniser]
    B --> C[Context Window Manager]
    C --> D[Conversation Memory Store]
    C --> E[RAG Retriever]
    C --> F[Tool and Function Registry]
    D --> G[Prompt Assembler]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[LLM Inference Engine]
    H --> I[Output Validator and Schema Checker]
    I --> J[Response Renderer]
    J --> K[Final Chat Reply]
    I --> L[Refusal and Safety Filter]
    L --> M[Safe Fallback Message]
    H --> N[Telemetry and Metrics Logger]
    N --> O[Hallucination Monitor]
    N --> P[Task Success Rate Tracker]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: A Single Chatbot Turn with RAG and Tool Use

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant FE as Frontend Chat UI
    participant OR as Orchestrator
    participant RAG as RAG Retriever
    participant LLM as LLM API
    participant TOOL as External Tool API
    U->>FE: Types "Book me a flight to Delhi tomorrow"
    FE->>OR: Forward raw user message
    OR->>RAG: Query knowledge base for flight policies
    RAG-->>OR: Return top 3 relevant policy chunks
    OR->>OR: Assemble system prompt plus context plus user query
    OR->>LLM: Send full prompt stack
    LLM-->>OR: Return JSON with intent and tool call
    OR->>TOOL: Execute flight_search function with extracted args
    TOOL-->>OR: Return flight options
    OR->>LLM: Send tool result back for natural language synthesis
    LLM-->>OR: Return final conversational answer
    OR->>FE: Stream response to user
    FE->>U: Display "Here are 3 flights to Delhi for tomorrow..."
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Conversational Context Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ReceivingTurn: New user message
    ReceivingTurn --> Tokenising: Sanitise input
    Tokenising --> BudgetCheck: Count tokens
    BudgetCheck --> Assembling: Within window
    BudgetCheck --> Summarising: Over window limit
    Summarising --> Assembling: Compress old turns
    Assembling --> Inferencing: Send to LLM
    Inferencing --> Validating: LLM returns output
    Validating --> Streaming: Schema matches
    Validating --> Fallback: Schema mismatch or refusal
    Streaming --> Idle: Append turn to history
    Fallback --> Idle: Log error and recover
    Idle --> [*]: Session ends
```

### 4.4 Prompt Engineering Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the chatbot's primary task?] --> Q2{Needs external data?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use RAG augmented prompt]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Needs API calls?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Use function calling prompt]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Needs step by step reasoning?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Use Chain of Thought prompt]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8{Needs consistent persona?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9[Use persona system prompt]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10[Use zero shot instruction prompt]
    Q3 --> Q11[Test in staging with golden set]
    Q5 --> Q11
    Q7 --> Q11
    Q9 --> Q11
    Q10 --> Q11
    Q11 --> Q12{Meets TSR target?}
    Q12 -- Yes --> Q13[Deploy to production]
    Q12 -- No --> Q14[Refine prompt and re test]
    Q14 --> Q11
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a "system prompt" in the context of conversational AI. How does it differ from a "user prompt"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **system prompt** is a hidden, persistent instruction string supplied to an LLM before the user conversation begins. It defines the chatbot's persona, scope, behavioural constraints, tone, and output format. It is set by the developer and is **not visible** to the end-user. A **user prompt**, in contrast, is the live, turn-by-turn input typed or spoken by the end-user during the conversation. The system prompt has higher priority and cannot be overridden by the user prompt unless the system prompt is poorly designed. *(Award full 3 marks for explicitly mentioning the priority and visibility difference.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three differences between traditional rule-based chatbots and modern LLM-based conversational AI.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | Rule-Based Chatbot | LLM-Based Conversational AI |
|------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | Uses hand-crafted decision trees and intent-matching | Uses large neural transformer models |
| 2 | Fails on out-of-scope queries (no match → fallback) | Handles open-domain, unseen queries via semantic understanding |
| 3 | Requires manual addition of every new intent | Learns new patterns from prompt examples and context |
| 4 | Deterministic, no hallucination | Probabilistic, can hallucinate, requires guardrails |

*(Any three valid points carry 1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer prompt architecture** used in modern conversational AI systems. Describe the role of each layer with one real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Softmax with Temperature** equation step-by-step. Analyse the two boundary cases $T \to 0$ and $T \to \infty$ and state which conversational use case corresponds to each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The **three-layer prompt architecture** consists of:

1. **System Prompt (Instructional Layer):** A static, developer-authored string that sets the chatbot's identity and global rules.
   *Example:* *"You are FinBot, a banking assistant. Never reveal account numbers. Always ask for the last 4 digits of the card before transactions."* (2 marks)

2. **Developer / Context Prompt (Operational Layer):** Dynamic, runtime-injected content such as retrieved documents, tool outputs, and conversation summaries.
   *Example:* A RAG system injecting the user's last 3 transaction summaries into context before answering *"What was my largest expense last month?"* (2 marks)

3. **User Prompt (Interactional Layer):** The raw, untrusted end-user input for the current turn.
   *Example:* *"Can you tell me my balance?"* (1 mark)

A neat block diagram showing the three layers stacked, with arrows showing the order of concatenation before being sent to the LLM, is expected. *(2 marks for the diagram + correct labelling.)*

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Start with vanilla softmax: $p_i = \dfrac{\exp(z_i)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j)}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Introduce temperature: $p_i^{(T)} = \dfrac{\exp(z_i / T)}{\sum_{j} \exp(z_j / T)}$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Case $T \to 0$: The largest logit dominates; $p_i \to 1$ for $\arg\max(z)$, others $\to 0$. This is **greedy decoding** — used for **factual Q&A bots, code completion, and customer support** where determinism is critical. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4:** Case $T \to \infty$: All logits scaled to zero; $p_i \to 1/V$ for every token — **uniform random sampling**. Used for **maximum diversity / creative brainstorming chatbots**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5:** State the practical rule of thumb: $T \in [0, 0.3]$ for factual, $T \approx 0.7$ balanced, $T \in [1.0, 1.2]$ for creative. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **ReAct (Reason + Act)** prompting pattern for tool-using chatbots. Write a minimal pseudo-prompt template and trace one full iteration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conversational AI has a context window $W = 16{,}384$ tokens. The system prompt is $S = 600$ tokens, response reserve $R = 1{,}500$ tokens, framework overhead $O = 350$ tokens. RAG injects 8 chunks of 280 tokens each. Conversation history is $H = 4{,}200$ tokens. Compute the **remaining user-query budget** in tokens and approximate words. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**ReAct** interleaves **Thought → Action → Observation** cycles inside the prompt. The chatbot is instructed to *think* about what it needs, *act* by calling a tool, and *observe* the tool's response before continuing. (2 marks for definition)

**Minimal pseudo-prompt template:**

```
You have access to these tools:
- search_kb(query: str) -> str
- book_slot(date: str, time: str) -> str

Use this format strictly:
Thought: <your internal reasoning>
Action: <tool_name>[<args>]
Observation: <tool output returned by system>
... (repeat Thought/Action/Observation as needed)
Final Answer: <user-facing natural language response>
```

(2 marks for the template)

**Trace of one iteration:**

- User: *"What slots are free tomorrow?"*
- **Thought:** "I need to check availability for tomorrow."
- **Action:** `search_kb[availability_date="tomorrow"]`
- **Observation:** "Slots at 10:00, 14:00, 16:00 are free."
- **Final Answer:** "Tomorrow I can offer you 10 AM, 2 PM, or 4 PM. Which works?"

(3 marks for the complete trace)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** $C_{\text{available}} = 16384 - (600 + 1500 + 350) = 16384 - 2450 = 13934$ tokens. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2:** RAG load $= 8 \times 280 = 2240$ tokens. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** $C_{\text{remaining}} = 13934 - 2240 = 11694$ tokens. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** Subtract history: $C_{\text{user}} = 11694 - 4200 = 7494$ tokens. *(1 mark)*

**Step 5:** Convert to words: $7494 \times 0.75 \approx 5621$ words. *(1 mark)*

**Final Answer:** **7,494 tokens ≈ 5,621 words** of fresh user input can be accepted. *(1 mark for the explicit final statement)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing "prompt" with "fine-tuning":** Prompt engineering does NOT modify model weights. Examiners deduct 2 marks if students claim fine-tuning.
> 2. **Forgetting units in formula answers:** Writing $T = 1$ without stating the range $[0, 2]$ costs a mark.
> 3. **Drawing the system prompt *after* the user prompt:** The correct order is **System → Context → User History → New User Query**. Reversing the order is a structural error worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the persona in the diagram:** System prompt diagrams without a labelled "Persona" or "Tone" block are marked incomplete.
> 5. **Skipping the boundary-case analysis in the Softmax derivation:** $T \to 0$ and $T \to \infty$ MUST be shown. Skipping them forfeits 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Prompt Engineering in Chatbots and Conversational AI**
>
> - **Three prompt layers:** System (static) → Developer/Context (dynamic) → User (volatile). Priority order is **System > Context > User**.
> - **Softmax with Temperature:** $p_i^{(T)} = \exp(z_i/T) / \sum_j \exp(z_j/T)$. $T \to 0$ = greedy, $T \to \infty$ = uniform.
> - **Top-p sampling:** Cumulative probability cutoff, default 0.9. Combine with temperature for fine control.
> - **Context Window Budget:** $C_{\text{user}} = W - (S + R + O) - \text{RAG} - H$. Always reserve response tokens.
> - **Token estimation:** 1 token ≈ 0.75 English words. Plan for 1.33× word count.
> - **ReAct pattern:** Thought → Action → Observation loop. Essential for tool-using agents.
> - **RAG + Persona = Robust Bot:** Always combine a strong system-prompt persona with grounded retrieval to minimise hallucination.
> - **Evaluation metrics:** Perplexity (lower=better), BLEU (n-gram overlap), Hallucination Rate (target <5%), TSR (target ≥90%).
> - **Function Calling:** Define tools as JSON Schema inside the system/context prompt. The model emits structured JSON, your code executes it.
> - **Multi-turn memory:** Store conversation history in a session-scoped buffer. When buffer exceeds budget, run a **summarisation pass** to compress old turns.
> - **Jailbreak defence:** Persona-locked system prompt + output schema validation + refusal-of-instruction-override clause.
> - **Persona prompt template:** *"You are [ROLE]. Your tone is [TONE]. You must [RULES]. If asked to violate RULES, politely refuse and offer [FALLBACK]."*
> - **CoT for chatbots:** Append *"Let's think step by step."* for reasoning-heavy queries; disable for latency-critical FAQ flows.
> - **Self-Consistency:** Run N parallel CoT samples, return the majority-voted answer — used in high-stakes medical/legal bots.
> - **Constitutional AI:** Embed a list of principles; the model critiques its own draft against them before replying.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Content Generation: Creative Writing, Code Generation, and Data Augmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Content Generation: Creative Writing, Code Generation, and Data Augmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Content Generation in Prompt Engineering?

**Content Generation** in the context of Prompt Engineering refers to the structured process of using carefully engineered natural language instructions (prompts) to guide a Large Language Model (LLM) into producing novel, high-quality, and task-specific outputs across three principal domains: **creative writing**, **programmatic code**, and **synthetic data**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** Content Generation is the prompt-driven synthesis of domain-specific artefacts (textual narratives, executable source code, or augmented datasets) by leveraging the latent knowledge and probabilistic next-token prediction capabilities of pre-trained foundation models, conditioned on user-supplied context windows.

Mathematically, this is framed as learning a conditional probability distribution:

$$P(\mathcal{Y} \mid \mathcal{X}, \theta)$$

where $\mathcal{Y}$ is the generated content, $\mathcal{X}$ is the engineered prompt, and $\theta$ represents the frozen parameters of the LLM.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Content Generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Syllabus Focus:** Students must master prompt patterns for *Creative Writing*, *Code Generation*, and *Data Augmentation*, as these represent the three highest-frequency industrial use-cases of LLMs in 2024–2026.

### Pillar 1: Creative Writing
This involves the generation of **narrative, poetic, descriptive, or persuasive** textual content. The LLM acts as a co-author, producing stories, marketing copy, scripts, and lyrical compositions.

### Pillar 2: Code Generation
This refers to the synthesis of **executable source code** in languages such as Python, Java, C++, JavaScript, or SQL, including functions, classes, unit tests, and documentation strings (docstrings).

### Pillar 3: Data Augmentation
This is the **synthetic expansion of training datasets** by generating new, label-preserving examples from a small seed corpus. It is critical in low-resource NLP, computer vision, and classification tasks.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef & Recipe Card" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a Master Chef (the LLM) locked inside a kitchen. The Chef knows ten thousand recipes by heart (training data) but is **blind** — the Chef cannot see your pantry or your guests. The only communication channel is a narrow slot through which you pass a **Recipe Card** (the prompt). 
>
> - A vague card ("Make food") → unpredictable output.
> - A precise card ("Make a gluten-free, low-sodium mushroom risotto for 4 vegan guests, served in 30 minutes") → a perfect dish.
> - **Prompt Engineering IS the art of writing the perfect Recipe Card.**

In this analogy:
- **Creative Writing** = the Chef improvising a brand-new tasting menu.
- **Code Generation** = the Chef writing down a brand-new recipe in standard notation.
- **Data Augmentation** = the Chef preparing 50 small variations of one dish to feed a taste-testing panel (training a classifier).

## 1.4 Core Metrics Governing Generation Quality

Across all three pillars, the following **standard metrics** are universally used:

| Metric | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Perplexity (PPL)** | $\text{PPL}$ | Measures how "surprised" the model is by the output (lower = better) |
| **BLEU Score** | $B$ | n-gram overlap between generated and reference text (0–1) |
| **ROUGE-L** | $R_L$ | Longest common subsequence recall for summarization |
| **Pass@k** | $P@k$ | Fraction of problems solved in $k$ attempts (code) |
| **Diversity Score** | $D$ | Distinct n-grams divided by total n-grams (data aug.) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The most commonly examined metric in Module 3 is **Pass@k** for code generation and **Distinct-n** for data augmentation. Memorize these formulas.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Stack of Content Generation

Every successful content generation task follows a **four-stage pipeline**:

1. **Intent Specification** — Define the goal, audience, format, and constraints.
2. **Prompt Construction** — Select a prompt pattern (zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, ReAct, etc.).
3. **Model Sampling** — Choose decoding strategy (greedy, beam, top-$k$, top-$p$, temperature $\tau$).
4. **Post-Processing & Validation** — Filter, parse, and validate the output (e.g., compile code, regex-match JSON).

## 2.2 Pillar I: Creative Writing — Prompt Patterns

### Pattern A: Persona Prompting
Assigning a role to the LLM dramatically steers style and vocabulary.

> *"You are an award-winning travel journalist. Write a 200-word opening paragraph for a feature article on the backwaters of Kerala..."*

### Pattern B: Constraint Stacking
Forcing the model to obey hard constraints (word count, rhyme scheme, POV, tense).

### Pattern C: Temperature & Top-$p$ Control
The **creativity dial** of the LLM is governed by the **softmax temperature** $\tau$:

$$P_{i} = \frac{\exp(z_i / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$$

where $z_i$ is the logit of token $i$, $V$ is vocabulary size, and $\tau \in (0, \infty)$.

> [!NOTE]
> - $\tau \to 0$: deterministic, repetitive, factual.
> - $\tau = 1$: standard sampling.
> - $\tau > 1$: increasingly creative, risky, hallucinatory.

**Top-$p$ (nucleus) sampling** restricts sampling to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability mass exceeds $p$:

$$\sum_{i \in S_p} P_i \geq p, \quad S_p = \text{smallest such set}$$

## 2.3 Pillar II: Code Generation — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.3.1 The Code-as-Sequence Paradigm
LLMs treat source code as a **token sequence** (often using subword tokenizers like BPE on source). Training corpora include GitHub repositories, StackOverflow, and LeetCode solutions.

### 2.3.2 The Pass@k Metric (CRITICAL for KTU)
The probability that **at least one of $k$ generated samples** passes all unit tests is:

$$P_{@k} = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$$

where $n$ is total samples drawn, $c$ is the number of correct samples, and $k$ is the budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Favorite):** If $n = 10$ samples are drawn, $c = 4$ are correct, and $k = 5$ are evaluated, then $P_{@5} = 1 - \binom{6}{5}/\binom{10}{5} = 1 - 6/252 = 0.9762$. The bias-corrected form divides the numerator by $(n-c)$.

### 2.3.3 Code-Specific Prompt Patterns
- **Docstring-to-Code**: Provide a function signature + docstring → fill the body.
- **Test-Driven Prompting (TDD-LLM)**: Provide failing tests → generate code that passes.
- **Chain-of-Code (CoC)**: Force the model to think in pseudocode before emitting real code.
- **Self-Reflection / Self-Debug**: Ask the LLM to critique and fix its own output.

## 2.4 Pillar III: Data Augmentation — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.4.1 The Low-Resource Problem
In classification, a small dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $N \ll 10{,}000$ causes overfitting. Augmentation synthesizes a larger $\mathcal{D}' = \mathcal{D} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\text{syn}}$ to improve generalization.

### 2.4.2 The Label-Preservation Constraint
For augmentation to be valid, the synthetic sample $x_i'$ must satisfy:

$$f(x_i') = f(x_i) = y_i$$

i.e., the true class label must remain unchanged.

### 2.4.3 Diversity Metrics
- **Distinct-n**: $\text{Distinct-}n = \frac{\vert \text{unique n-grams} \vert}{\vert \text{total n-grams} \vert}$
- **Self-BLEU**: Lower is better (avoid mode collapse).
- **MAUVE**: Measures distributional similarity to human text.

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Domain | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(y \mid x) = \text{softmax}(z / \tau)$ | All | Sampling with temperature $\tau$ |
| 2 | $P_{@k} = 1 - \binom{n-c}{k}/\binom{n}{k}$ | Code | Pass@k probability |
| 3 | $\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Writing | n-gram precision with brevity penalty |
| 4 | $\text{ROUGE-L} = F_\beta(\text{LCS})$ | Writing | F-measure on longest common subsequence |
| 5 | $\text{PPL} = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log P(x_i \mid x_{<i})\right)$ | All | Geometric mean of inverse token probability |
| 6 | $D_n = \vert \text{unique}_n \vert / \vert \text{total}_n \vert$ | Augmentation | Lexical diversity of synthetic set |
| 7 | $\text{BP} = \min\left(1, e^{1 - r/c}\right)$ | Writing | BLEU brevity penalty |
| 8 | $\tau \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{entropy} \uparrow$ | All | Higher temp → flatter distribution |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note:** In the table above, vertical bars are used *only* to delimit columns — not for absolute value notation. If you need $\vert x \vert$ in a KTU exam answer sheet, write it as $\lvert x \rvert$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Pillar | Industrial Application |
|---|---|
| **Creative Writing** | Marketing copy, news summarization, screenplay drafting, game dialogue |
| **Code Generation** | GitHub Copilot, Cursor, automated unit test synthesis, legacy code migration |
| **Data Augmentation** | Low-resource sentiment classification, medical NER, fraud-detection, multilingual MT |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Temperature-Scaled Softmax (Creative Writing Control)

**Goal:** Show how the temperature $\tau$ reshapes the token distribution to control creativity.

**Step 1 — Start with raw logits.**
The LLM outputs a vector of raw logits $z = (z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_V)$ for vocabulary $V$.

**Step 2 — Apply temperature scaling.**
Divide every logit by $\tau$:

$$z' = \left(\frac{z_1}{\tau}, \frac{z_2}{\tau}, \ldots, \frac{z_V}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the softmax function to obtain probabilities.**

$$P_i(\tau) = \frac{\exp(z_i / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$$

**Step 4 — Examine the limiting cases.**

\begin{aligned}
\lim_{\tau \to 0^+} P_i(\tau) &= \mathbf{1}[i = \arg\max_j z_j] \quad \text{(deterministic, greedy)} \\
\lim_{\tau \to \infty} P_i(\tau) &= \frac{1}{V} \quad \text{(uniform random)} \\
\tau = 1 &\Rightarrow \text{standard Boltzmann distribution}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Compute the entropy of the distribution.**

The Shannon entropy is:

$$H(\tau) = -\sum_{i=1}^{V} P_i(\tau) \log P_i(\tau)$$

A larger $\tau$ produces a flatter distribution and higher entropy, corresponding to greater creative diversity — but also a higher risk of hallucination and incoherence.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Pass@k Metric (Code Generation Evaluation)

**Goal:** Derive the unbiased estimator for the probability that at least one of $k$ independently drawn code samples passes all unit tests.

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Let $n$ be the total number of code samples drawn (with replacement) from the LLM. Among them, $c$ are correct (pass all tests). We want $P(\text{at least 1 correct in a random subset of size } k)$.

**Step 2 — Complement rule.**

\begin{aligned}
P_{@k} &= 1 - P(\text{all } k \text{ are incorrect}) \\
&= 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Numerical demonstration (KTU Exam Example).**
Suppose $n = 200$, $c = 80$, $k = 10$.

\begin{aligned}
P_{@10} &= 1 - \frac{\binom{120}{10}}{\binom{200}{10}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{\binom{200}{10} \cdot \prod_{i=0}^{9}\frac{120-i}{200-i}}{\binom{200}{10}} \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=0}^{9}\frac{120-i}{200-i} \\
&= 1 - \frac{120 \cdot 119 \cdot 118 \cdot 117 \cdot 116 \cdot 115 \cdot 114 \cdot 113 \cdot 112 \cdot 111}{200 \cdot 199 \cdot 198 \cdot 197 \cdot 196 \cdot 195 \cdot 194 \cdot 193 \cdot 192 \cdot 191}
\end{aligned}

This evaluates to $P_{@10} \approx 0.9999$, i.e., nearly certain success if 40 % of samples are correct.

**Step 4 — Bias correction (Chen et al., 2021).**
For small $n$, the unbiased estimator is:

$$\hat{P}_{@k} = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}} \cdot \frac{(n-k+1)\cdot \text{adjustment}}{(n-c-k+1)\cdot \text{adjustment}}$$

In practice, KTU students are expected to know the **basic form** of Pass@k only.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: BLEU Score for Creative Writing Evaluation

**Step 1 — Compute modified n-gram precision.**
For $n \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, compute clipped precision $p_n$ where each n-gram in the candidate is capped by its maximum count in any reference.

**Step 2 — Geometric mean of log precisions.**

$$\log \text{BLEU} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n, \quad w_n = \frac{1}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the brevity penalty (BP).**

\begin{aligned}
\text{BP} &= 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } c > r \\
\exp(1 - r/c), & \text{if } c \leq r
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}

where $c$ is the length of the candidate and $r$ is the length of the reference.

**Step 4 — Final score.**

$$\text{BLEU} = \text{BP} \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Prompt-Pattern Selection

| Use-Case | Best Prompt Pattern | Recommended $\tau$ | Recommended Top-$p$ | Validation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Poetry / Fiction | Persona + Few-shot | 0.9 – 1.2 | 0.95 | ROUGE-L + human eval |
| Marketing copy | Constraint stack + CoT | 0.7 – 0.9 | 0.9 | A/B CTR test |
| Bug-free code | TDD + Self-debug | 0.2 – 0.4 | 0.95 | Compiler + unit tests |
| Boilerplate code | Zero-shot, docstring-to-code | 0.1 – 0.2 | 0.9 | Compiler |
| Low-resource text aug. | Paraphrase + back-translation | 0.8 – 1.0 | 0.95 | Classifier F1 + Distinct-n |
| Tabular SMOTE-like aug. | Conditional generation | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.9 | KS-test + label fidelity |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An LLM generates $n = 50$ code samples for a competitive-programming problem. $c = 5$ pass all hidden tests. Compute Pass@1, Pass@5, and Pass@10.

\begin{aligned}
P_{@1} &= 1 - \frac{\binom{50-5}{1}}{\binom{50}{1}} = 1 - \frac{45}{50} = 0.10 \\
P_{@5} &= 1 - \frac{\binom{45}{5}}{\binom{50}{5}} = 1 - \frac{1{,}175{,}724}{2{,}118{,}760} \approx 0.445 \\
P_{@10} &= 1 - \frac{\binom{45}{10}}{\binom{50}{10}} = 1 - \frac{3{,}365{,}978{,}880}{10{,}272{,}278{,}170} \approx 0.672
\end{aligned}

**Interpretation:** A **10 % single-shot success** rises to a **67 % chance of success** if we are allowed 10 attempts — this is why code-generation systems in industry use $k = 5$ to $k = 10$ samples.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Content Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Intent Specification] --> B[Prompt Pattern Selection]
    B --> C[Zero-shot]
    B --> D[Few-shot]
    B --> E[Chain of Thought]
    B --> F[ReAct and Tools]
    C --> G[LLM Inference Engine]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Decoding Strategy]
    H --> H1[Greedy]
    H --> H2[Beam Search]
    H --> H3[Top-k Sampling]
    H --> H4[Top-p Nucleus]
    H1 --> I[Post Processing]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Creative Writing Output]
    I --> K[Code Generation Output]
    I --> L[Data Augmentation Output]
    J --> M[Quality Evaluation]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Pass at k and BLEU and ROUGE]
    M --> O[Human Review Loop]
    N --> P[Deployment or Dataset Release]
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Three-Pillar Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CREATIVE_WRITING
        C1[Persona Prompt] --> C2[LLM as Author]
        C2 --> C3[High Temperature Sampling]
        C3 --> C4[BLEU and ROUGE and Human Eval]
    end
    subgraph CODE_GENERATION
        K1[Docstring and Test Prompt] --> K2[LLM as Programmer]
        K2 --> K3[Low Temperature Sampling]
        K3 --> K4[Compiler and Unit Test Loop]
        K4 --> K5{Tests Pass}
        K5 -->|No| K6[Self Debug Reflection]
        K6 --> K2
        K5 -->|Yes| K7[Final Code Artifact]
    end
    subgraph DATA_AUGMENTATION
        D1[Seed Corpus and Label] --> D2[Paraphrase and Back Translation]
        D2 --> D3[Conditional LLM Sampling]
        D3 --> D4[Label Fidelity Filter]
        D4 --> D5[Augmented Dataset Release]
    end
```

## 4.3 Sampling Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Sampling Strategy] --> S1{Need Determinism}
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Use Greedy Decoding]
    S1 -->|No| S3{Need Diverse Outputs}
    S3 -->|No| S4[Use Beam Search]
    S3 -->|Yes| S5{Short or Long Form}
    S5 -->|Long Form| S6[Top-p = 0.95 plus Temp 0.9 to 1.1]
    S5 -->|Short Form| S7[Top-k = 40 plus Temp 0.7 to 0.9]
    S6 --> S8[Validate with ROUGE or BLEU]
    S7 --> S8
    S2 --> S9[Validate with Exact Match]
    S4 --> S9
```

## 4.4 Prompt-Pattern to Output Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[All Prompt Patterns] --> P1[Zero-Shot]
    P0 --> P2[Few-Shot]
    P0 --> P3[Chain-of-Thought]
    P0 --> P4[ReAct]
    P0 --> P5[Self-Consistency]
    P1 --> O1[Best for simple factual creative tasks]
    P2 --> O2[Best for style mimicry in writing]
    P3 --> O3[Best for algorithmic code problems]
    P4 --> O4[Best for tool-using code agents]
    P5 --> O5[Best for mathematical reasoning prompts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and node labels contain raw uppercase text only — no markdown bold, no HTML tables, no reserved keywords used as standalone node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set A]`
**Define Content Generation in the context of Prompt Engineering. List the three principal pillars covered in Module 3.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Content Generation is the process of using engineered natural-language prompts to instruct a Large Language Model (LLM) to synthesize novel outputs in text, code, or data form. The three principal pillars of Module 3 are:
1. **Creative Writing** — narrative, lyrical, persuasive, and descriptive text.
2. **Code Generation** — executable source code, functions, classes, and tests.
3. **Data Augmentation** — synthetic expansion of training datasets to mitigate low-resource conditions.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for naming the pillars, 1 mark for one-line descriptions.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**Write the formula for the Pass@k metric used in code generation evaluation. State the meaning of every variable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$P_{@k} = 1 - \frac{\binom{n-c}{k}}{\binom{n}{k}}$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of code samples generated,
- $c$ = number of samples that pass all unit tests,
- $k$ = evaluation budget (number of samples actually inspected / allowed),
- $P_{@k}$ = probability that at least one of the $k$ samples is correct.

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variable definitions, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set A]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **temperature** $\tau$ and **top-$p$ nucleus sampling** in controlling the creativity of an LLM during creative writing tasks. Derive the temperature-scaled softmax distribution and discuss the effect of $\tau \to 0$ and $\tau \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **four-stage operational pipeline** for content generation. For each stage, give one concrete example applied to a *story-writing* task. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Temperature and Top-p in Creative Writing (7 marks)**

The LLM produces raw logits $z_i$ for the next token. The temperature-scaled softmax is:

$$P_i(\tau) = \frac{\exp(z_i / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V} \exp(z_j / \tau)}$$

**Limiting behaviour (3 marks):**

\begin{aligned}
\tau \to 0^+ &: \quad P_i \to \mathbf{1}[i = i^*] \;\; \text{where } i^* = \arg\max_j z_j \quad \text{(deterministic)} \\
\tau \to \infty &: \quad P_i \to 1/V \quad \text{(uniform — maximum entropy)} \\
\tau = 1 &: \quad \text{standard Boltzmann}
\end{aligned}

**Top-p sampling (2 marks):** Restricts sampling to the smallest set $S_p$ of tokens whose cumulative probability mass first exceeds $p$ (typically $p = 0.9$ to $0.95$). This dynamically trims the "long tail" of low-probability tokens, improving coherence while preserving diversity.

**Creative writing recommendation (2 marks):** Use $\tau \in [0.9, 1.1]$ combined with $p = 0.95$ for high-creativity prose; use $\tau \in [0.3, 0.5]$ with $p = 0.9$ for tightly controlled marketing copy.

**(b) Four-Stage Pipeline (7 marks)**

| Stage | Description | Story-Writing Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Intent Specification | Define goal, audience, format | "Write a 500-word mystery set in Munnar for young adults, 3rd-person POV" |
| 2. Prompt Construction | Pick pattern (persona, CoT, few-shot) | "You are a bestselling Indian author. Here are two opening paragraphs in your style: ... Now continue..." |
| 3. Model Sampling | Choose decoding params | $\tau = 1.0$, top-$p = 0.95$, max tokens = 600 |
| 4. Post-Processing | Filter, validate, refine | Read-through for repetition; character-name consistency check; human edit |

*(Award 1.5–2 marks per stage with a coherent example; 1 mark for pipeline diagram or logical flow.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set B]`

**(a)** Explain **Data Augmentation** in prompt engineering. Discuss the label-preservation constraint and the **Distinct-n** diversity metric with formula. Show how an LLM can be used to paraphrase a seed sentence while preserving its sentiment label. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A code-generation LLM produces $n = 100$ candidate solutions for a sorting problem. Out of these, $c = 25$ are correct. Compute **Pass@1, Pass@5, and Pass@10**. Comment on why industrial code-LLMs sample multiple times. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Data Augmentation (7 marks)**

Data Augmentation is the synthesis of new training examples $(x', y)$ from a seed set $\{(x, y)\}$ to enlarge the effective dataset size and improve classifier generalization, especially in low-resource scenarios ($N < 10^4$). **(1 mark)**

**Label-preservation constraint (2 marks):** Every synthetic sample $x'$ must satisfy $f(x') = y$ where $f$ is the true labelling function. For sentiment, paraphrasing "The movie was fantastic" → "The film was outstanding" must remain **positive**.

**Distinct-n metric (2 marks):**

$$D_n = \frac{\vert \text{unique n-grams in } \mathcal{D}_{\text{syn}} \vert}{\vert \text{total n-grams in } \mathcal{D}_{\text{syn}} \vert}$$

Higher $D_n$ = more lexical diversity = less mode collapse.

**LLM-based paraphrase example (2 marks):**
- Seed: "The customer service was appalling and rude." (label: NEGATIVE)
- Prompt: *"Paraphrase the following review while keeping the negative sentiment: <seed>"*
- Synthetic: "The support team behaved dreadfully and was discourteous." (label: NEGATIVE ✓)

**(b) Pass@k Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given $n = 100$, $c = 25$.

\begin{aligned}
P_{@1} &= 1 - \frac{\binom{99}{1}}{\binom{100}{1}} = 1 - \frac{99}{100} = 0.01 \;\; \text{(1 mark)} \\
P_{@5} &= 1 - \frac{\binom{75}{5}}{\binom{100}{5}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{17{,}259{,}390}{75{,}287{,}520} \\
&\approx 0.771 \;\; \text{(3 marks for derivation and calculation)} \\
P_{@10} &= 1 - \frac{\binom{75}{10}}{\binom{100}{10}} \\
&\approx 0.943 \;\; \text{(3 marks for derivation and calculation)}
\end{aligned}

**Commentary (0–1 mark):** Even with only 25 % single-shot accuracy, allowing 10 attempts raises success to **94.3 %**. This is precisely why industrial code-completion tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, CodeWhisperer) generate $k = 5$–$10$ candidates and rank them by log-probability or test-feedback signals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Board Valuation Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing temperature with top-p.** Examiners frequently deduct **1–2 marks** when students state that "high temperature means more accurate" — the correct relationship is *higher temperature = more diverse/random = less accurate but more creative*.
> 2. **Swapping $n$ and $c$ in Pass@k.** Always remember: $n$ = *total* samples generated; $c$ = *correct* ones; $k$ = *budget*. Mixing these is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting the brevity penalty in BLEU.** Candidates shorter than the reference must include the $\text{BP} = \exp(1 - r/c)$ term, or risk losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the label-preservation clause** in data-augmentation answers — this is the most common single-line omission in Module 3, costing **2 marks**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 High-Yield Points**

- [ ] **Three pillars of Content Generation**: Creative Writing, Code Generation, Data Augmentation. (Most-favoured Part A question)
- [ ] **Temperature $\tau$**: divisor in the softmax; $\tau \to 0$ = deterministic, $\tau \to \infty$ = random. **Higher $\tau$ = more creative** but riskier.
- [ ] **Top-$p$**: nucleus sampling; smallest token set with cumulative probability $\geq p$.
- [ ] **Pass@k formula**: $1 - \binom{n-c}{k}/\binom{n}{k}$. **Memorize variable meanings.**
- [ ] **BLEU**: clipped n-gram precision × brevity penalty. Range $[0, 1]$.
- [ ] **ROUGE-L**: longest common subsequence F-measure.
- [ ] **Perplexity**: $\exp(-\text{mean log-prob})$; lower is better.
- [ ] **Label-preservation constraint** in data augmentation: $f(x') = y$.
- [ ] **Distinct-n** measures lexical diversity: unique/total n-grams.
- [ ] **Four-stage pipeline**: Intent → Prompt → Sampling → Post-Processing.
- [ ] **Few-shot > Zero-shot** for style mimicry in creative writing.
- [ ] **TDD-LLM / Self-Debug patterns** boost code-generation reliability.
- [ ] **Industrial code-LLMs sample $k = 5$–$10$ candidates** and rank them.
- [ ] **MAUVE** and **Self-BLEU** are advanced augmentation metrics; only MAUVE-vs-human is high-yield.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prompt Engineering for Sentiment Analysis, Classification, and Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST868 - PROMPT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prompt Engineering for Sentiment Analysis, Classification, and Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Prompt Engineering for NLP Tasks** is the disciplined design, structuring, and refinement of natural language instructions fed to a Large Language Model (LLM) to elicit accurate, consistent, and structured outputs for specific downstream Natural Language Processing (NLP) workloads — namely **Sentiment Analysis**, **Text Classification**, and **Machine Translation (MT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sentiment Analysis** is the NLP task of computationally identifying, extracting, and categorizing opinions, emotions, and attitudes expressed in a piece of text — typically classified into polarity labels such as *positive*, *negative*, *neutral*, or fine-grained emotions (*joy*, *anger*, *sadness*, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Text Classification** is the supervised NLP task of assigning one or more predefined categorical labels to a document, sentence, or token. Sub-tasks include *intent classification*, *topic labeling*, *spam detection*, and *named-entity recognition (NER)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Machine Translation (MT)** is the sub-field of computational linguistics that uses software to translate text or speech from a *source language* to a *target language*. In the LLM era, this is reframed as *Neural Prompted Translation* — translation as a next-token prediction task governed entirely by the prompt.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you hire a brilliant but **literal-minded polyglot intern** who speaks 100+ languages and has read the entire internet. You will never write a "program" for this intern — you will simply *instruct them in English*.

- **For Sentiment Analysis**: Think of it as asking the intern, *"Read this Amazon review and tell me if the customer is happy, sad, or furious — and rate it from 1–5."* The intern has intuition, but you must *frame* the question so they don't ramble.
- **For Classification**: Picture sorting 10,000 support tickets into piles labeled *Billing*, *Technical*, *Account*. You give the intern **examples** of each pile (few-shot) and tell them: *"New ticket arrives → you tell me the pile name in exactly one word."*
- **For Translation**: The intern already speaks both languages, but if you say *"Translate this"* without context, they may translate *formally* or *literally* or *creatively*. Your prompt decides which.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept**: In the prompt-engineering paradigm, the **model is frozen (zero/few weight updates)**, and *all* task knowledge is injected through the **prompt context window**. The prompt is, therefore, a *program in natural language*.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $A = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Balanced Classification |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Imbalanced Sentiment |
| **BLEU Score** | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Translation Quality |
| **chrF / ROUGE-L** | — | Translation / Summarization |
| **Exact Match (EM)** | — | Slot-filling, NER |

Where $P$ = Precision, $R$ = Recall, $BP$ = Brevity Penalty, $p_n$ = n-gram precision, $w_n$ = n-gram weight (typically $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Prompting Paradigms

### 2.1.1 Zero-Shot Prompting
- The model receives **only the task instruction** and the input — no worked examples.
- Relies entirely on the LLM's pre-trained instruction-following ability (RLHF / SFT alignment).
- Best for: simple polarity sentiment, high-resource language translation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero-Shot Template Skeleton**:
> `Task Description` + `Input` + `Output Format Constraint`

### 2.1.2 Few-Shot Prompting (In-Context Learning — ICL)
- The prompt contains $k$ labeled exemplars $(x_i, y_i)$ before the test query.
- The number of exemplars $k$ is called the **shot count**.
- Performance typically **scales log-linearly** with $k$ until context-window saturation.

$$P_{\text{few-shot}}(y \mid x) \approx P_{\text{LLM}}(y \mid \underbrace{I}_{\text{instruction}}, \underbrace{(x_1, y_1), \dots, (x_k, y_k)}_{\text{demonstrations}}, \underbrace{x}_{\text{query}})$$

### 2.1.3 Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting
- Forces the model to emit *intermediate reasoning tokens* before the final label.
- **Why?** It allocates more *computation depth* (more forward-pass tokens) to hard classification (e.g., sarcasm, mixed sentiment, ambiguous intent).
- CoT improves accuracy on tasks requiring multi-hop logical deduction by **≈ 15–40%** on benchmarks like SST-2 and MultiNLI.

## 2.2 Prompt Anatomy for Each Task

### A. Sentiment Analysis Prompts
**Key Engineering Decisions:**
1. **Granularity specification** — binary (pos/neg) vs. ternary (pos/neg/neu) vs. fine-grained (5-star, Ekman-6).
2. **Output constraint** — single token (`"positive"`) vs. structured (JSON with `{"sentiment": "...", "confidence": 0.0–1.0}`).
3. **Domain priming** — *"You are a financial-analyst sentiment classifier..."* vs. *"You are a movie-review sentiment classifier..."*.
4. **Calibration** — *"Respond with a confidence score; if uncertain, output 'unsure'."*

### B. Classification Prompts
**Key Engineering Decisions:**
1. **Closed vs. open label set** — explicitly enumerate all possible classes to prevent hallucinated labels.
2. **Label-definition augmentation** — provide a 1-line *definition* per class to disambiguate edge cases.
3. **Tie-breaking instructions** — *"If two labels apply, return the more specific one."*
4. **Format rigidity** — force single-token or single-line output for easy post-processing.

### C. Translation Prompts
**Key Engineering Decisions:**
1. **Language pair specification** — use ISO 639-1 codes (`"en"`, `"ml"`, `"hi"`) for zero ambiguity.
2. **Register / tone control** — *"Translate in formal Malayalam"*, *"Use Bangalore Hindi slang"*.
3. **Glossary injection** — supply domain-specific term mappings inside the prompt.
4. **Faithfulness vs. fluency** — explicit instruction: *"Translate literally, do not paraphrase idioms."*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | In-Context Learning posterior | $P(y \mid x, C) \propto P_{\text{LLM}}(y \mid I, D, x)$ | Few-shot classification |
| 2 | Log-linear scaling with shots | $\text{Acc}(k) \approx a \log(k) + b$ | Optimal shot-count tuning |
| 3 | Brevity Penalty (BLEU) | $BP = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c > r \\ e^{1 - r/c} & \text{if } c \le r \end{cases}$ | Translation eval |
| 4 | n-gram precision | $p_n = \frac{\sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \min(\text{count}_C, \text{count}_R)}{\sum_{\text{n-gram} \in C} \text{count}_C}$ | BLEU computation |
| 5 | Macro-F1 | $F_1^{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{\ell=1}^{L} F_1^{(\ell)}$ | Multi-class sentiment |
| 6 | Softmax temperature effect | $P_i = \frac{\exp(z_i / \tau)}{\sum_j \exp(z_j / \tau)}$ | Sampling diversity |
| 7 | Log-prob confidence | $\text{Conf}(y) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x)$ | Self-verification |
| 8 | Cross-entropy loss (oracle) | $L = - \sum_{i} y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Prompt distillation reference |

Where $C$ = context window, $D$ = demonstrations, $I$ = instruction, $L$ = number of label classes, $\tau$ = temperature, $T$ = token length of $y$, $c$ = candidate length, $r$ = reference length.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Prompt Strategy Used |
|---|---|---|
| **FinTech** | Real-time news sentiment for trading | Few-shot + JSON output + domain priming |
| **Healthcare** | Classify patient feedback into complaint categories | CoT + label definitions + tie-breaking |
| **E-Commerce** | Multilingual product description translation (en → ml/hi) | Glossary injection + register control |
| **Legal-Tech** | Classify case documents into IPC sections | Closed label set + format rigidity |
| **Customer Support** | Auto-route tickets by intent | Zero-shot with calibrated confidence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb**: Always include a *format constraint* in production prompts — *"Respond with exactly one of: [positive, negative, neutral]. No other text."* — because unconstrained LLMs *will* ramble, breaking downstream parsers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: BLEU Score for a Translation Prompt

The **Bilingual Evaluation Understudy (BLEU)** score measures n-gram overlap between a candidate translation $C$ produced by the LLM and one or more human reference translations $R = \{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_M\}$.

### Step 1 — Compute the Modified n-gram Precision

For each n-gram $g$ of length $n$ in the candidate $C$:

$$\text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(g) = \min\!\left(\text{Count}_C(g),\ \max_{j=1}^{M}\text{Count}_{R_j}(g)\right)$$

This *clips* the candidate count at the maximum count found across all references, preventing gaming via repetition.

### Step 2 — Aggregate into n-gram Precision

$$p_n = \frac{\sum_{g \in \text{n-grams}(C)} \text{Count}_{\text{clip}}(g)}{\sum_{g \in \text{n-grams}(C)} \text{Count}_C(g)}$$

### Step 3 — Combine Across n-gram Orders (Geometric Mean)

The standard BLEU uses uniform weights $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

$$BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Brevity Penalty

To penalize overly short translations that game high precision via brevity:

$$
BP = 
\begin{cases} 
1, & \text{if } c > r \\
\exp\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{c}\right), & \text{if } c \le r
\end{cases}
$$

where $c$ = total length of candidate corpus, $r$ = length of *effective reference corpus* (closest reference length summed across sentences).

**Conversion Logic**:
- If the candidate is **longer** than the reference → no penalty ($BP=1$).
- If the candidate is **shorter** → exponential penalty grows as $\frac{r}{c}$ increases.
- This makes a 5-word "good good good good good" output score **near zero**, even if every word matches.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Confidence-Scored Classification Prompt

Many production systems require a *probability-like* confidence score, not just a label. We extract this from the LLM's output token log-probabilities.

### Step 1 — Extract Per-Token Log-Probabilities

For an output sequence $y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_T)$:

$$L_t = \log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, x, I)$$

These log-probabilities are exposed by the LLM API as `logprobs` for the top-$k$ tokens at each position.

### Step 2 — Compute Sequence Log-Probability

$$\log P(y \mid x, I) = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$$

### Step 3 — Normalize by Length (Geometric Mean)

To avoid bias toward shorter outputs:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{Conf}(y) = \exp(\bar{L})$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Probability Across Candidate Labels

If we have $K$ candidate labels $\{y^{(1)}, \dots, y^{(K)}\}$ (e.g., *positive*, *negative*, *neutral*):

$$P(y^{(k)} \mid x) = \frac{\exp(\bar{L}^{(k)} / \tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \exp(\bar{L}^{(j)} / \tau)}$$

where $\tau$ is a **temperature** parameter. Lower $\tau$ → sharper distribution; higher $\tau$ → softer.

**Conversion Logic**: This recovers a true posterior over labels using only the LLM's *unconditional* next-token beliefs, enabling **soft, calibrated multi-class classification without any model fine-tuning**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Prompt-Pattern Selection

| Task Sub-Type | Recommended Pattern | Output Format | Token Cost | Accuracy Gain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binary sentiment | Zero-shot + role | Single token | **Low** | Baseline |
| Fine-grained 5-class | Few-shot (k=5) | Single token | **Medium** | +8–12% |
| Sarcasm / mixed | CoT + self-ask | JSON | **High** | +20–35% |
| Intent classification | Few-shot + label defs | Single token | **Medium** | +10–15% |
| High-resource translation | Zero-shot + glossary | Free text | **Medium** | Baseline |
| Low-resource translation | Few-shot (parallel pairs) + register | Free text | **High** | +15–25% |
| Domain-specific NER | CoT + schema | JSON | **High** | +18–30% |

## 3.4 Iterative Prompt Refinement Loop

A formal four-stage optimization cycle used in production:

$$
\text{Prompt}_0 \xrightarrow{\text{Evaluate on dev set}} \text{Metrics}_0 \xrightarrow{\text{Error analysis}} \text{Failure Modes} \xrightarrow{\text{Patch prompt}} \text{Prompt}_{i+1}
$$

Each iteration must:
1. Hold out a **fixed dev set** (typically 200–500 examples) for stable measurement.
2. Stratify errors by **class / language / domain** to localize prompt bugs.
3. Apply *one* change at a time (instruction rephrasing, example swap, format tweak) for causal attribution.
4. Re-evaluate to confirm improvement before committing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often bundle *multiple* prompt changes together, then cannot explain which change caused the metric shift. The Board examiner will *explicitly* deduct marks for "uncontrolled experimentation" in design questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Prompt Engineering Pipeline for the Three Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw User Input Text] --> B[Task Router / Classifier]
    B --> C1{Is it Sentiment?}
    B --> C2{Is it Classification?}
    B --> C3{Is it Translation?}

    C1 --> D1[Sentiment Prompt Template]
    D1 --> E1[Few-Shot Demonstrations DB]
    E1 --> F1[LLM Inference Engine]
    F1 --> G1[Output Parser: Label + Confidence]
    G1 --> H1[Sentiment Result Positive Negative Neutral]

    C2 --> D2[Classification Prompt Template]
    D2 --> E2[Label Schema and Definitions]
    E2 --> F2[LLM Inference Engine]
    F2 --> G2[Output Parser: Single Label Token]
    G2 --> H2[Class Label: Billing Technical Account]

    C3 --> D3[Translation Prompt Template]
    D3 --> E3[Glossary and Register Spec]
    E3 --> F3[LLM Inference Engine]
    F3 --> G3[Output Parser: Target Language Text]
    G3 --> H3[Translated Output]
```

## 4.2 Sentiment-Analysis Prompt Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Define Granularity Binary vs 5 class] --> B[Choose Pattern Zero vs Few vs CoT]
    B --> C[Add Domain Priming Role Prompt]
    C --> D[Inject Output Constraint JSON or Token]
    D --> E[Add Calibration Confidence Unsure]
    E --> F[Assemble Final Prompt]
    F --> G[Run on Dev Set]
    G --> H{Metric Target Met}
    H -- No --> I[Error Analysis and Patch]
    I --> A
    H -- Yes --> J[Deploy to Production]
```

## 4.3 Translation Prompt Architecture with Cross-Lingual Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Text in Language L1] --> B[Pre-Processor: Normalize and Detect L1]
    B --> C[Prompt Composer]
    C --> D1[System Instruction: Role Expert Translator]
    C --> D2[Language Pair Spec: L1 to L2 ISO Codes]
    C --> D3[Register Control: Formal or Colloquial]
    C --> D4[Glossary Lookup: Domain Term Map]
    C --> D5[Few-Shot Parallel Pairs for Low Resource L2]
    D1 --> E[Assembled Multilingual Prompt]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[LLM Engine]
    F --> G[Post-Processor: Strip Noise and Verify L2]
    G --> H[Final Translation Output in L2]
```

## 4.4 Output Parsing & Validation Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw LLM String Output] --> B[Step 1: Strip Whitespace and Markdown]
    B --> C[Step 2: Try JSON Parse]
    C --> D{Parse Success}
    D -- Yes --> E[Extract Fields Sentiment Label Confidence]
    D -- No --> F[Step 3: Regex Match for Allowed Labels]
    F --> G{Match Found}
    G -- Yes --> H[Return Label with Default Confidence 0.5]
    G -- No --> I[Step 4: Fallback: Return Unsure and Log]
    E --> J[Validated Structured Output]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: Any production-grade prompt system **must** have a parsing/validation layer — raw LLM strings are *untrusted* and cannot be passed to downstream code without sanitization. A diagram showing only the prompt → LLM → user path will be marked **incomplete**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **zero-shot** and **few-shot** prompting for sentiment analysis. State one situation where zero-shot is preferred and one where few-shot is mandatory.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Zero-shot prompting** provides only the task instruction and the input to the LLM — no worked examples — relying entirely on the model's instruction-following alignment (RLHF/SFT). **Few-shot prompting** (in-context learning) prepends $k$ labeled examples $(x_i, y_i)$ to the prompt so the model infers the input→label mapping from demonstrations.  
- **Zero-shot preferred**: high-volume, low-stakes tasks such as social-media brand monitoring where the polarity labels are *obvious* (positive/negative) and inference cost must be minimized.  
- **Few-shot mandatory**: fine-grained or domain-specific classification — e.g., *5-star movie review* rating or *fintech news sentiment* — where the boundary between adjacent classes is fuzzy and the LLM's pre-trained intuition misclassifies edge cases. *(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List **three** engineering controls you would embed in a production prompt for an LLM-based English→Malayalam translator to ensure *consistent* and *culturally appropriate* output.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **ISO 639-1 language-pair specification** at the start — e.g., *"Translate the following English text into Malayalam (ml)."* — to prevent the LLM from guessing a related but wrong language (e.g., Tamil).  
2. **Register / tone constraint** — e.g., *"Use formal Malayalam suitable for a government press release; avoid anglicized loanwords."* — to fix the sociolinguistic register.  
3. **Glossary injection** of domain-specific terminology — e.g., a JSON block mapping `"machine learning" → "യന്ത്ര പഠനം"` — to enforce consistent technical vocabulary across calls.  
*(3 marks: 1 per control)*

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

> **KTU 2024 Rule**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Sentiment + Classification Design*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** Design a **few-shot prompt** for a 4-class customer-feedback classifier with classes *{Complaint, Praise, Query, Suggestion}*. Show the complete prompt, the choice of $k$, and justify the design choices. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss how **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** prompting improves classification accuracy on *ambiguous* customer feedback, and write a sample CoT prompt for detecting the *dominant emotion* (joy, anger, sadness, fear) in a tweet. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Choice of $k$**: We select $k = 6$ demonstrations (2 per non-trivial class), because the literature shows performance plateaus beyond $k \approx 8$ for binary/4-class tasks and $k=6$ fits comfortably in a 4 K-token context window. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Demonstrations chosen** to cover *boundary cases* (e.g., a "Praise" that is actually a *Suggestion*, a "Query" with *Complaint* undertone). *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Complete Prompt**:
```
You are a customer-feedback classifier for an e-commerce platform.
Classify the following feedback into EXACTLY ONE of these 4 labels:
  - "Complaint" : user is unhappy about a service failure
  - "Praise"    : user is happy with the product
  - "Query"     : user is asking a question
  - "Suggestion": user is proposing an improvement

Respond with ONLY the label in lowercase. No explanation.

Examples:
Feedback: "Why was my refund not processed yet?"
Label: query

Feedback: "The new UI is great, but the search bar should auto-suggest."
Label: suggestion

Feedback: "I am furious that my package arrived broken!"
Label: complaint

Feedback: "Wow, the sound quality blew me away, amazing product."
Label: praise

Feedback: "Can you add dark mode? The white background hurts my eyes."
Label: suggestion

Feedback: "Your delivery partner was rude to me today, unacceptable."
Label: complaint

Now classify:
Feedback: "{USER_INPUT}"
Label:
```

**Step 4 — Justification of design choices** *(3 marks)*:
1. **Closed label set enumerated** → prevents hallucinated labels.
2. **One-line definition per label** → disambiguates *Praise vs. Suggestion* (both can be positive).
3. **Boundary-case demonstrations** (5th & 6th examples) → teaches the model the *distinguishing cue* (proposing change = suggestion; reporting failure = complaint).
4. **Format rigidity** ("Respond with ONLY the label") → enables trivial post-processing.
5. **Role-priming** ("You are a customer-feedback classifier…") → activates the relevant subdomain of the LLM's pre-trained knowledge.

#### **Model Solution — (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Why CoT helps ambiguous input** *(3 marks)*: Ambiguous customer feedback often contains *mixed signals* (e.g., *"The product is excellent, but the seller was rude"* has both *joy* and *anger*). A single-label classifier without reasoning tends to default to whichever cue appears first ("lexical bias"). CoT forces the LLM to:
- Identify *all* candidate emotions present.
- Weigh their relative salience.
- Justify the dominant label.

This allocates more *inference compute* (extra forward-pass tokens) to the decision, mirroring how a human annotator would resolve ambiguity.

**Step 2 — Sample CoT Prompt for Dominant Emotion in a Tweet** *(4 marks)*:
```
You are an expert emotion analyst. Given a tweet, identify ALL
emotions present and then decide the DOMINANT emotion.

Allowed emotions: {joy, anger, sadness, fear, surprise, disgust}

Think step-by-step:
  Step 1: Quote the emotionally charged phrases.
  Step 2: List every emotion triggered and why.
  Step 3: Pick the DOMINANT emotion.
  Step 4: Output a JSON object: {"emotions": [...], "dominant": "..."}

Tweet: "{TWEET}"

Reasoning:
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Loss**:
> 1. Students often forget the **closed label enumeration** — model answer requires the *exact* class set spelled out, not implied. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. In CoT questions, students *describe* the technique but do not **write the prompt**. Examiner expects the literal prompt text. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. Students choose $k$ without justifying with respect to *context-window* and *performance plateau* — a $k$ value without justification is incomplete. **(−1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Translation + Cross-lingual Engineering*

**(a)** Explain the **three failure modes** of naive LLM translation prompting and propose specific engineering remedies for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a **production-grade translation prompt** for translating English medical discharge summaries into Malayalam, with full system + user prompt, glossary integration, and a post-processing validation step. Justify each component. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)**

| # | Failure Mode | Description | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
